George Clough made copies of various makers, including Montagnana. The instrument was also inscribed with the name of the person for whom it was made.
John Clutterbuck was employed by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons as a bow maker until 1971.
Albert Coad was active in Redruth from 1916 and in Penzance from 1930. Influenced by W.

Lawrence Cocker was a self-taught, ingenious, and innovative maker of violins and bows, highly respected and admired by colleagues in the English violin trade. In 1949, he received a Diploma of Honour at The Hague.
James Radford Coggin was an artist, sculptor, and musician originally from Salisbury, NC. He began making violins in 1968 in New York and worked full-time as a violin maker from the 1980s.
André Coinus was a pupil of L. Cherpitel.

Firm information about Prosper Colas' early life and training is not forthcoming, but it seems that he was born in Coincourt and apprenticed locally before arriving in Paris in 1871, probably to work for J.B. Vuillaume .
James Cole was a pupil of Tarr and was associated with Craske. His work varied greatly in quality, although his cellos were generally more successful.
Jean Colin was known for a quinton. He also created a violin housed in a Berlin museum, featuring very open 'c' bouts and a resinous yellow-brown varnish.
Jean Baptiste Colin worked in the Mirecourt style. The model was based on Stradivari.
Louis Collenot was the grandson of F. Caussin and a pupil of H.
Raymond Collenot was the elder son and pupil of Louis Collenot . He had a workshop in Paris on rue Labat starting in 1931.
Claude Nicolas Collin studied under N. F.
Charles Collin-Mézin was the son and assistant of Charles Jean-Baptiste Collin. He traveled extensively and spent time in America before returning to Mirecourt in 1900.
Son of Charles Jean-Baptiste Collin-Mézin fils.
Charles Jean-Baptiste Collin-Mézin, originally from Mirecourt, was the son of Claude Nicolas Collin and studied under N. F.
French maker. Present shop in Munich.
F. W.
Joseph Collingwood established himself in Ottumwa from 1882. He was a prolific self-taught maker who worked on Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Camillo Colombo studied under L. Bisiach around 1913 and later established himself independently at 13 viale col di Lana.
Matthew Coltman worked for the bow department of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons from 1977 until 1982, when he left to work for Edward Withers Ltd.
Walter Ewing Colton was a violinist and a self-taught amateur maker in New York around 1880. He was influenced by George Gemunder.

Antonio Comuni was a maker based in Piacenza, primarily known for his guitars, though he also produced violins noted for their characteristically long soundholes. His violin output dates from around 1816 onwards.
Louis M. Condax was a research chemist for Eastman-Kodak.
Georges Cone succeeded Boulangeot in 1928 and served as a repairer for the conservatoire and philharmonic orchestras in Lyons. A prolific maker, he was credited with over 200 violins and 25 cellos.
Contemporary Hungarian maker. Son of Istvan Konya Senior and named after him.
C. G.
The work exhibited decent quality in the typical Mirecourt style. It featured a flat model and an unremarkable yellow varnish.

Georges Contal was a pupil of Poiron and settled in Paris in 1899 at 113 Avenue Daumesnil after extensive travels across Europe. He was associated with Arnould in the development of a new mandolin form.
Luigi Contavalli was a proficient maker of his own models and Stradivari patterns. He was a prolific maker of cellos, using good golden-yellow varnish and locally sourced timber.
Primo Contavalli was the son and pupil of Luigi Contavalli. He was a fairly prolific maker of instruments, using personalized Stradivari and Guarneri models, and applied a golden-yellow varnish.
Alfredo Contino was a pupil of Vincenzo Postiglione from 1902 and succeeded to the business in 1916. He was best known for his very precise copies of work from the Gagliano family.

José Contreras was the greatest Spanish violin maker, known as 'Il Grenadino. ' He worked in Madrid from around 1740 and was strongly influenced by Stradivari, whose instruments were becoming recognized in the Spanish court at that time. However, his style differed significantly from Stradivari's own, exhibiting an impromptu feeling in both finish and technique.
Hugh Wyatt Cooper was a watchmaker and violin maker. He was active in Glasgow from 1893.
After starting work in the case department in 1917, Arthur Copley transferred W.E. Hill & Sons' bow department, where he remained for 30 years, though he had little affinity for the work.

Work shows similarities with that of Andreas Statler as well as Bernardo Calcagno , with whom he lived and probably shared a workshop around 1730. Not the same maker as the Jacopo Filippo Cordano who was active in Genoa around 1770.
George Corsby was originally a shoemaker from Northampton. He operated at 8 Princes Street, London from 1835 to 1841, and then moved to 9 Princes Street from 1841 to 1869.
Giorgio Corsini was a violinist and a self-taught violin maker, guided by R. Fredi.
Andrea Cortese was a self-taught violin maker from a wealthy Genoese family, influenced by C. Candi.

Eugène Corvisier worked for Deroux and Audinot after being a pupil of Cunault, taking over Audinot's shop located at 17 Boulevard Bonne-Nouvelle in 1908, which was originally the atelier of S. Vuillaume.
Antonio Costa was active from 1919. He created copies of Guadagnini.
Giovanni Battista Costa created fine work in a distinctively Seraphin style, possibly as a pupil. The soundholes were elegant and Amati-like with a high arch.
Louis Charles Courtier studied under Aubry. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Camille Couture worked in the rue Jeanne-Marie, Montreal. He created original models with good workmanship.
Joseph Couturieux was a pupil of Vuillaume. He was rarely encountered and was both signed and branded.
The item featured double purfling and was finished with yellow or red varnish. It bore the brand: 'Couturier Michel A Paris. '.
Nicolas Couturieux created some well-made examples in the style of Bernardel. Others were of the more typical flat, yellow Mirecourt type, inspired by Didier Nicolas.
William Cox created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. He prepared the amber varnish himself.
Thomas Craig was a music publisher, instrument maker, and repairer. He varnished and sold imported German trade instruments.
Charles Cramond trained under Joseph Ruddiman and took over his business in 1810 at 27 Netherkirkgate, Aberdeen. One violin was jointly labelled by Ruddiman and Cramond.
George W. Crank exhibited at the Music Trades Convention in St.

George Craske first studied violin making with William Forster in London and began making instruments for Thomas Dodd and the firm of Muzio Clementi in the early 19th century. After relocating several times in search of a satisfactory place to open ... a shop, he settled in Birmingham, where he began an extremely prolific career as a talented copyist of Amati , Guarneri , and Stradivari .
James Crawford was employed by V. C.
Bartolomeo Cristofori was a significant figure in the history of musical instruments. He was most renowned as a maker of keyboards and as the inventor of the piano.
Herbert Charles Cross worked for T. Toomey from 1950.

Nathaniel Cross, son of an engraver, began his violin-making career in the early 1700s and partnered with Barak Norman around 1713. A Norman viol from that year is inscribed 'Nathaniel Cross wrought my back and belly', and Cross' name appeared alongside Norman's on a violin label in 1724, which included the address 'St Paul's Churchyard'.
John Crowther began work for John Kennedy in Clare Market. He subsequently operated from his own shops at 2 Ely Court around 1778 and at 22 Fetter Lane around 1792.
Armand Cuisset studied the violin at the Brussels Conservatoire. He subsequently trained as a maker in various Belgian and German workshops.

Georges Cunault trained with Sebastien Vuillaume and Miremont from 1874 to 1882 and established his own workshop in 1880. He occupied several addresses in Paris, including 53 Faubourg Poissoniere in 1882, 29 rue des Martyrs in 1884, 6 rue Clauzet in 1889, and 31 rue Navarin in 1893, before moving to 19 rue Navarin.

Mirecourt bow maker Eugène Cuniot, known as "Cuniot-Hury", trained and worked with his father until his father's death in 1884, when Cuniot-Hury took over the firm. Mirecourt was in the midst of tremendous growth as a center of the violin trade, and in particular the influence of Charles Nicolas Bazin held sway in bow making ... circles.
Student of Otto Erdesz and subsequently worked in Toronto, Paris and Cremona. In 1984 Curtin set up a joint making studio with Gregg Alf in Ann Arbor.
Joseph Curtin and Gregg Alf set up a joint violin making studio, Curtin & Alf, in Ann Arbor in 1984. They dissolved the partnership after 12 years.

Johannes Bernardus Cuypers was the son of Johannes Theodorus Cuypers and the younger brother of Johannes Franciscus. He was a lesser maker compared to his father and brother, serving as an assistant in the family workshop.

Johannes Francis Cuypers, son and assistant of Johannes Theodorus, worked for his father until his death in 1808, including a period from 1783 to 1823 spent in Liedestraat, Amsterdam. Recognized as the most refined maker of the family, he produced more Stradivarian flat arches with a polished finish, sometimes imitating the blackened chamfers of a Stradivarius head.

One of the most prominent makers from The Netherlands and among the first to turn away from Amati in favor of a Stradivari model, Johannes Theodorius Cuijpers worked in the Hague from 1752 to till the early 1800s. It has been suggested that he received ... some training in France, but historical evidence on the subject is inconclusive.
Mario D'Alessandro trained at R. Wurlitzer & Co. under Roman Klier starting in 1948 and later studied with Simone Sacconi.

Dario D'Attili emigrated to the USA in 1935. He worked for Jargo Petrenela in New York from 1937 to 1938, for Emil Herrmann from 1938 to 1951, and for Rembert Wurlitzer from 1951 to 1974, alongside Simone Sacconi.

Alessandro D'Espine is one of the more enigmatic makers of the 19th Century Turin school. A nobleman by birth, D'Espine worked as a surgeon and dentist to the nobility, and probably engaged in violin making as a serious hobby rather than as a profession.
Jean Frédéric Schmitt's workshop in Lyon. Output three levels of quality: "Vincent", "Delaune" and "Daber".
Franck Daguin was born in Lyon, where he studied cabinet-making before apprenticing in bowmaking in the J.F Schmitt workshop. He opened his own workshop in 1991.
Frans Waldemar Dahlén was active as an amateur from 1884 and turned professional around 1890. He established himself in Göteborg in 1920.

The instruments of Giuseppe Dall’Aglio present a fascinating blend of refinement and eccentricity. At first glance they can seem irregular—sometimes even awkward—with middle bouts that draw too sharply inward and an overall geometry that refuses to conform to the cleaner, more measured outlines of his contemporaries.

Pietro Antonio Dalla Costa worked in nearby Treviso and was classified as a maker of the Venetian school. His work was neat and charming in the Amati manner, although it was somewhat weaker than that of the finest Venetian makers of the period.
Sebastian Dallinger created very good work on the Stainer model, featuring a high arch and deep edge flute. Instruments produced later were less extreme.
Edmond Daniel was the son of Charles Daniel and worked for Gand in Paris. Later, he took over his father's business in Marseilles at a new address, 5 rue Paradis, until after 1852.
Silvio Danieli owned one violin featuring ebony inlaid edges. A cello was also reported by Vannes.
Samuel Wesley Daniels was the husband of Constance Daniels. He was a self-taught amateur maker starting in 1936.
George Darby was a violin and bow maker. He created models based on Stradivari, Guarneri, Amati, and Gagliano.
Charles Claude François Darche was a pupil of N. F.
Hilaire Darche was the son of Joseph Darche and trained at Gaillard and Nicolas Ainé in Mirecourt. He later worked for N.
Nicolas Darche was the brother of Joseph and Charles Francois Darche. He trained in Mirecourt and was employed by N.
Paul Darche was the son and pupil of Charles Claude Francois Darche. He trained in Mirecourt before rejoining his father in Brussels.
Jean-Auguste Darte worked in Mirecourt from 1865. He was a pupil, son-in-law, and successor of N.
William Davidson worked in Edinburgh, creating instruments based on a roughly Stradivarian model. He used commercial amber varnish, which was either yellow or dark red.
The viola featured very deep ribs, with purfling positioned well in from the edge. It had a very flat arch and was low set over long soundholes.
John Day was a violinist and an amateur maker. He created copies of his own Guarneri del Gesii violin.
William Samuel Day took up violin making in 1924 after retiring from the Customs service, working in his shop in Plymouth with his son, who was also named William Samuel.

Evidently an adventuresome young man, Paolo de Barbieri ran away from home to become a sailor at the age of 13 and returned to Genoa six years later, in 1908. He then apprenticed to Cesare Candi before serving in the navy and returning to work for his teacher.
Thomas De Bomé was a dilletante, yet he achieved good results. Instruments were labeled in the Musèe de la Musique, Paris.
Filippo De Filippi produced good work and varnish. It remained currently unknown and unverified.
Remo De Giorgio created Guarneri, Stradivari, and personal models featuring dark golden or golden-red varnish. He gained awards in Padua in 1932 and in Cremona in 1936.
Augusto De Gregori sent several instruments of his own make to Russia through a musician he knew. He created Gagliano models and was also recognized for a quartet made in the manner of Dalla Corte.
Giovanni Battista De Lorenzi was not a violin maker; he was an organ builder and a general music dealer. Some instruments bore his label.

Gennaro De Luccia worked in Casigliano and Mercato Cilento, Salerno. He was primarily a maker of guitars and mandolins but also produced a significant number of violins with clear orange-yellow varnish, which were mostly exported to the USA.
Michele De Luccia was a pupil of his cousin Gennaro de Luccia and his uncle Michele (I). He worked early in Italy, primarily focusing on plucked instruments.
Vincenzo De Luccia was the brother of Michele de Luccia (II). He was a pupil of Matteo and his father Michele (I).

Carlo De March was a Venetian violin and bow maker who studied under Vestoli in Lugo before relocating to Venice in 1940. His fine workmanship was influenced by Degani, and he became an active bow maker in 1955.
Umberto De Peccati specialized in double basses and also made plucked instruments with yellow-red spirit varnish in classical models. He exhibited in Cremona in 1937.
Augustino De Planis created rather loose work, typical of the Genovese school. The soundholes were long and angled, and the arch was flat with pale yellow-gold varnish over a grey ground.
Augusto (Angelo) de Rub. The model resembled those of Stainer, Amati, or Guidantus.
Nicola de Rubeis was known as Italiano detta il Romagnolo. He created his work in Nizza in the year 192..
Ralph de Saint-Croix created an amateur work on a clumsy model. It featured red varnish and was made in London in 1905.

Cosmo De Souza worked with the Chanot family in Manchester, London, and Paris around 1890. He operated independently in Leeds and Bradford starting in 1899, in Birmingham in 1903, and at Lacey Green (Cheshire) in 1906.

Pietro Paolo De Vitor was of Venetian origin according to his label. His work was rarely encountered, but it featured striking form and workmanship, representing an interesting blend of early Brescian and later Rogeri styles.

Valentino De Zorzi opened a shop in Pistoia in 1880, having previously fought with Garibaldi's forces before working as a blacksmith and armourer in Bologna. He was commissioned by Count Vieri Ganucci Cancellieri in Pistoia to build his first violin.
Mark Dearlove worked in Leeds from 1808 to 1849. He employed Thomas Absam, John Gough, and John Fryer at different times.

Mark William Dearlove, son and pupil of Mark Dearlove, took over his father's workshop in Boar Lane in 1828. He entered a brief partnership with John Charles Fryer, with their business listed as 'Dearlove and Fryer' at Benson's Yard, 23 Boar Lane.

Michele Deconet was born near Strasbourg and traveled widely as a violinist. Traditionally it has been assumed that he took up violin making in Venice in the mid-18th century, but in fact there is no conclusive historical evidence that he ever held a formal apprenticeship or registered as a violin-maker in that city or elsewhere.
Georges Defat was the son and pupil of Émile Laurent. After apprenticing with P.
Pierre Defresne trained in Paris. He was appointed luthier to the Duke of Luxembourg.
Domenico Degani created Amati and Maggini models, frequently featuring carved female heads instead of the scroll. He also made guitars, viols, and lutes.
Eugenio Degani was the son and pupil of Domenico Degani . In 1868, he moved to Montagnana, where he began making violins at least by 1872, winning a gold medal at the Treviso Esposizione.
Giulio Degani was the son of Eugenio , with whom he worked from an early age. After his father’s death in 1901 he took over the family’s Venice workshop until emigrating to the USA in 1915.

A very rare and interesting maker whose well-defined work is of high quality but somewhat eccentric in form. A little reminiscent of Rogeri, the outline has short `c' bouts and the scroll is distinguished by the very deeply cut throat beneath the volute.
Alfredo Del Lungo was the son and pupil of Giuseppe del Lungo. He worked at the Conservatorio Cherubini in Florence and the Accademia in Sienna.
Gérard J. Deleplanque made many and various bowed and plucked stringed instruments.
Louis Delignon was the younger son of Charles Delignon. He worked for Collin-Mezin, G.
Auguste Delivet served an apprenticeship in Mirecourt. He was the nephew and pupil of Victor Rambaux.
Alfonso Della Corte created copies that were very competent and, in some instances, quite convincing. He used a suitable clear golden-yellow varnish.
Marcel Deloger apprenticed to C. Quenoil.

François Joseph Delprato worked in Paris with Marchand, then in Chicago with Lyon & Healy, and in New York in 1918, before returning to Toulouse, France, in 1926, and finally to Houecourt, Vosges. He created copies of classical instruments that displayed very finely controlled workmanship.

Marcel Demey was a pupil of Pierre Jean Henri Hel from 1909 and later served as his assistant until Hel's death in 1937. He continued to work at Hel's shop until 1943 for Hel's widow before establishing his own business in Lille.
H. Denis made commercial instruments at the Laberte-Humbert factory.
Honoré (Jean-Joseph) Derazey apprenticed in Mirecourt and was later employed in various ateliers in Paris. He established his own business in Mirecourt, which quickly became very successful.

Justin Amedée Derazey was the son and pupil of Honoré Derazey . He established his own workshop in Mirecourt around 1850 and succeeded Nicolas fils in 1884, after which his instruments featured the brand of J.

Georges Deroux was a pupil of Honore Derazey and was known as 'Deroux père. ' In 1846, he established his own business in Mirecourt, which became a training ground for many luthiers, including the Hill family members who later founded the leading European firm of expertise and restoration in London in 1882. He left Mirecourt in 1880 to work with Mennesson in Rheims.

Sébastien Auguste Deroux worked as the son and pupil of Georges Deroux . He was employed by Silvestre in Lyons from 1866 to 1869, and then by Miremont in Paris from 1873 to 1884, where he became the head of the workshop.
Giuseppe Desiato worked in the Via Speransella in Naples, where he made several very respectable instruments known in the style of Stradivari and Gagliano, using good materials.

Pietro Paolo Desideri created models of Guadagnini or Guarneri derivation, characterized by coarsely finished and rather undistinguished workmanship that utilized mediocre material, enhanced by a good quality golden-brown varnish with good texture and consistency. The printed labels included: P.
Joseph D. Deulin moved from Madison to Washington D.
John G. Deveau used varnish that was prepared by Calvin L.
John Devereux worked for Bernard Simon Fendt. He emigrated to Fitzroy, Australia in 1864, drawn by the gold rush of the time (according to the Hill Archive).

Frank Devoney received guidance from several local makers but developed a strong interest in Savart's work on acoustics and the principles of Ghladni-pattern plate tuning. In 1893, he published a book of his findings titled The Acoustics of Violin-making in Edinburgh.
Armando Di Lelio studied under Rudolfo Paralupi . He created a personal model that was well finished.
Alban Dick apprenticed to A. Wilfer and was later employed by A.
Edward Dickenson worked on a coarsely exaggerated Stainer pattern.
William Dickie worked on Cremonese models with red-yellow varnish, according to Henley. His work is presently unknown.
A fine small viola was signed on the interior, with the address listed as Drury Lane. One example resembled the early Hill workshops, featuring a flat arch and pale golden yellow varnish.
Dmitri Didchenko was a refugee of the Russian Revolution. He worked in Poznan and Warsaw, Poland, starting in 1917.
Antoine Didelin was the son of Henry Didelin . He registered as a luthier in 1779.
J. Didelot used a fictitious name on factory-produced work.

Marius Didier apprenticed at Thibouville-Lamy in Mirecourt. He was one of the finest makers from Mirecourt and received several awards for his work that imitated classical Cremonese instruments, particularly the 1716 Stradivari 'Nessie' violin.
Paul Didier was the son of Marius Didier and worked for his father until 1936, after which he was located in Metz. He received medals at Paris and Toulouse.
August Diehl was the son and pupil of Friedrich Diehl. He worked in Hamburg starting in 1875.
Johann Diehl was the eldest son of Martin Diehl (I). His work was mostly inspired by Stradivari, although it exhibited some crude details.

Born in 1890, Amédée Dieudonné learned violin making with Gustave Bazin , son of bow maker Charles Nicolas . He worked for the Darché firm in Brussels before settling in his ... hometown of Mirecourt around 1920.
Luigi Digiuni was active from 1920. He created an individual outline and model, with many made based on classical proportions.
Gaetano Dionelli worked in the style of Scarampella. He was a pupil of Dall'Aglio and was particularly known for cellos and basses.
Justin Victor Diter apprenticed to Paul Bailly. He worked for Blanchard in Lyons from 1890 to 1896.
Harry Dobbs was a self-taught maker starting in 1970. He completed over 100 instruments, primarily violas.
Marco Dobretsovich moved to Cairo in 1914. He was a pupil of Pollastri in Bologna around 1920.
L. Dobrianski was chiefly a repairer and restorer.

Makers of the Dodd family were among the most important in the 19th century London bow trade. While John I stands out for his craftsmanship and immense productivity, his father Edward, brother James I, and nephews James II and Edward ...

Edward Dodd was a bow and string maker. He worked in partnership with his brother James (II) at various addresses, as listed for James (II), until around 1836, when he remained alone in Vauxhall after James departed for Finsbury.

James Dodd was a bow maker of considerable importance. He was the son of Edward Dodd and established his business at 9 Granby Buildings, Lambeth, with the assistance of his sons James (II) and Edward (II).

Known as the 'English Tourte ' for his leading role in the development of the modern bow in Britain, John 'Kew' Dodd was initially employed as a fine metalworker. Proximity to this trade afforded him the tools and expertise to develop the screw mechanisms being pioneered in Paris around the same time, and Dodd probably took up ... bow making in the 1770s–80s.

Younger brother of John Dodd , Thomas Dodd began working as a bowmaker around 1784 but by 1794 had switched to making and dealing in violins. In the early years he employed Bernhard Simon Fendt I and John Frederick Lott I, both of whom were responsible for the great number of excellent cellos and double basses the shop produced. ...
Edward Newman (Edward II) Withers. Son and pupil of Edward (I), active from 1856.

Giovanni Dollenz was initially thought to have learned his craft from Cremonese violin maker Lorenzo Storioni during Storioni's itinerant travels to Trieste in the early 1800s. However, proof of Storioni's presence there is limited to several instruments bearing Trieste labels.

Giuseppe Dollenz was the son and pupil of Giovanni Dollenz, a Trieste violin maker. More prolific than his father, he followed a similar style in the 19th century, although his varnish was somewhat inferior, being less transparent and light in color.
Heinz Dölling was a first-class bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Otto Gustav Dölling.

Hermann Dölling was the son and pupil of Hermann Moritz Dölling. He worked for Ludwig Glasel, followed by Ernst Liebich in Breslau, and finally August Riechers in Berlin before returning to the family business in Markneukirchen, which became the 'Vogtländische Musikinstrumenten-Fabrik' in 1886.
Louis Dolling studied under Karl Gustav Otto in Leipzig. He later worked for Ernst Liebich in Breslau from 1877 to 1883.
Max Dolling was the son and pupil of Hermann Dölling (I). He made commercial instruments on a factory basis.

Arnold Dolmetsch was a violinist and a pupil of Vieuxtemps in Brussels from approximately 1871 to 1873. He came to London to finish his studies at the Royal College of Music, where he developed an interest in the historical performance of music.
Lucien Dolphyn studied the violin at Ghent Conservatoire. He had a limited output as a violin maker and used orange-red oil varnish.
James W. Dooley was a self-taught violin maker.

Nicolaus Döpfer was the father-in-law of Martin Diehl and Jacob Steininger , and he effectively founded the Mainz school of violin makers. He was appointed as the instrument maker to the Court in Mainz.
Johann (Hans) Andreas Dörffel was the best maker of this family. The model was large, with an arching that was not too exaggerated.
Michael Dötsch apprenticed to Michael Kohlbacher in Luby (Schönbach). He worked in various ateliers across Europe from 1894 to 1908.
William Henry Dow emigrated to Australia in 1854. He was a prolific maker who used Stradivari and, later, Guarneri models.
Charles Drouin was the son and pupil of Etienne Drouin and succeeded Baur-Bourdot. He took over the name and business of 'Chipot-Vuillaume' in 1897.
Etienne Drouin was a well-regarded workman in the upper class of commercial work. The varnish was a typical yellow Mirecourt.
Louis Drouin was born and trained in Mirecourt. He emigrated to the USA in 1915 and worked with Delunet in New York.
Frantisek Xavier Drozen studied under J. B.
Victor Paul Dubois worked for F. P.
Nicolas Duchaine apprenticed at Mirecourt as a violin and bow maker. He supplied bows to F.
Nicolas Duchaine was a bow maker. He was the son, pupil, and assistant of Nicolas Duchaine (I).
Colin Duchene created a Stradivari model featuring orange varnish, as reported by Henley; however, this claim was not verified by other sources and may be a misreading of Jean-Baptiste Colin.
Nicolas Duchesne branded and signed instruments 'à Paris', although this was a common fiction among Mirecourt makers. His shop was known as 'A la Ville de Padoue'.
André Paul Dugad was a violin and bow maker. He worked for Caressa & Français in Paris from 1920 to 1932.
Aubrey Dugarde reported the information, but it remained unverified by other sources.

One of the most successful and prolific English violin makers of the 18th century, Richard Duke established his workshop in central London around 1743. Over the course of its existence the enterprise grew to encompass multiple locations within the city and employed numerous makers of instruments and bows, including William Napier ... and John Betts , who bought the shop from Duke's daughter Anne after his death in 1783.
George Duncan was a pupil and successor to Walter Plain . He worked in Glasgow from 1875 at 21 Brunswick Street, which was previously the premises of Walter Plain , and later at 33 Queen Street.
James Duncan was active from around 1890. He crafted violins and cellos based on the Stradivari pattern, featuring large scrolls.
Robert Duncan was the most significant maker of the Aberdeen school. His work, very refined and based on the Stainer model, was comparable to contemporary Florentine craftsmanship.
John Dunthorne made a decently constructed cello based on a loosely Amati form; the front featured very thick wood with a light golden colored varnish and painted purfling, bearing this label.
Adolphe Dupary apprenticed to Darte and was employed by P. Bailly.
Georges Louis Dupuy was a pupil of Vatelot and Brugere. In 1939, he established a workshop with his father at 4 rue Demours.
Philippe Dupuy was the son and pupil of Georges Dupuy and the grandson of E. Sartory.
Herman Gerard Duwaer was the son of Pieter Duwaer, a piano maker. He began his career as a cabinet maker and registered as a violin maker in 1883.
Carolus Joseph Dvorak was the son and pupil of Jaroslav Dvorák. His interpretations of Cremonese work featured shaded varnish and were quite similar to those of Lupot.
Jan Baptista Dvorak apprenticed to Jan Kulik from 1840 to 1844. He worked in Budapest for J.
Jaroslav Antonin Dvorák was the son and pupil of Jan Baptista Dvorak . He independently established himself in Prague starting in 1885.

George Dyker produced well over 40 violins and some cellos based on the Stradivari model. His work resembled that of George Haxton from Glasgow; it was very cleanly executed and featured a thick oil varnish of orange color.
Arthur W. Dykes was born in Leeds, Yorkshire.
George Langton Dykes was the son and pupil of Harry Dykes. He became an active violin maker at the age of 12 and also received training from Paul Bailly, who had worked for his father.
David Earl was one of the last bow makers employed by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons , where he apprenticed under Brian Alvey .
Eugène Eberle was the son of Oscar Eberle, a professor of cello at Rotterdam Conservatoire. He apprenticed with C.

Johann Ulrich Eberle was a pupil of Thomas Edlinger (III) in Prague and established his own workshop in 1727. Recognized as one of the finest makers of the Prague school, he followed the Stainer model while occasionally incorporating elements from Cremona.

Only recently has credible biographical information concerning the violin makers of the Neapolitan school come to light. Until the experts who have researched this material publish their archival findings, we leave the birth and death dates of these makers as previously accepted.
Gustav Edel was a musician and an amateur maker. The instrument was a Stradivari model, well executed with his own varnish.
Ernest Edler was the son and pupil of J. Henry Edler.
Son of Friedrich Edler (III), brother of Friedrich (IV), and Richard. Worked for J.
Contemporary bowmaker in LA

Born in Salt Lake City, Rock Eggen is now based in Denver, Colorado where he makes, sells, and repairs instruments. Eggen’s recognitions include first place in Violin Workmanship at the 1988 VSA International Competition.
20th-century Neapolitan maker, mainly of plucked instruments (mandolins, guitars) and some bowed strings.
George Nicolas Einsele worked for William Lewis & Son in Chicago, where he made approximately 70 instruments from 1912 to 1914. He returned to Brunswick around 1915.
Sandor Elek was a pupil of Bohak in Budapest. He created good copies of classical Italian work.

Albert Ellersieck was a self-taught German maker who worked in Rostock, Mecklenburg from 1878 to 1892, in Greiz, Thuringia in 1895, and finally in Berlin starting in 1900. He received several exhibition medals for his craftsmanship, which was noted for being good, although often slightly oversized.

Helmuth Ellersieck, son of Albert Ellersieck, apprenticed with Ernest Kreul in Markneukirchen from 1900 to 1903. He later worked for Otto Möckel and August Friedel in Berlin, and subsequently for Hjorth in Copenhagen.
William Elliot was a fairly prolific maker known for his refined French style based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. He produced works that reflected this distinctive approach.
J. F.
Contemporary maker and repairer based in Wales, UK.

Francesco Emiliani worked on via del Governo Vecchio, Rome. He persisted with a hybrid Amati/Stainer model of slightly diminished scale, which was beautifully made and very Italianate, featuring fine soft golden oil varnish.
John K. Empsall was a violinist of the Bradford Symphony Orchestra and an amateur maker.
The production was commercial factory output, branded as 'F. & R. E' or 'F.
Charles Enel apprenticed to Grillon and Gustave Bazin. He worked for Mougenot and Deroux in Mirecourt, then in Switzerland and Germany, and finally with Silvestre and Maucotel in Paris.
Apprenticed to Leon Mougenot 1916. Worked with his uncle Charles in Paris 1919-1934.
Gulbrand Enger was a pupil of Jacobson and Vuillaume. He served as a maker to the Danish court.
Hagbarth Enger was the son and pupil of Gulbrand Enger . He worked in several cities across Europe before returning to his father's shop in Copenhagen.
Andreas Engleder was a pupil of his uncle Joseph Fischer in Regensburg and of J. C.
Moved to Budapest in 1927 and studied as an artist at the Academia of Fine Arts. Active violin maker from 1941.
Franz Anton Ernst was a celebrated violinist and concert master. He was a violin maker from 1785, using the Stradivari model.
Johann Anton Ertl established his workshop at 863 Schulerstrasse, Vienna. He was probably the brother of Karl Ertl .
Robert Ervine was a violin and bow maker. He produced decent violins based on the Stradivari model.
The personal model sometimes featured doubled purfling. The workmanship was undistinguished.
Raffaele Esposito was the son of Giovanni Esposito. He created a Gagliano model that featured yellow-brown varnish.
Clément Eulry was a bow maker and a pupil of Nicolas Maire. He also occasionally made violins and at least one mandolin.
Jean Eulry apprenticed at Laberte & Magnié from 192é to 1923. He worked for A.
Francois Eury was the elder brother or father of Jacob Eury.

Jacob Eury, for some time mistakenly identified as Nicolas Eury, initially studied violin making with his father François but soon took up bow making. After eight years of military service Eury returned to Mirecourt and may have apprenticed in the workshop of Jean ... 'Grand' Adam .
Karl Euschen worked in Saarbruck. He was employed at Hug & Co in Zurich from 1919 to 1922 under Tenucci.
Jacques Charles Eve established himself at the sign 'A La Fortune' at various addresses: rue Dauphine, S. Germain around 1757, rue Culture S.
David Ewan was a violinist and composer in the traditional Scottish style. He was also a skilled and professional violin maker.

Charles Eyles was an artist and a fairly prolific amateur violin maker from around 1890. He was active in Bedford, Wangford, Harpenden, and Bournemouth, using models derived from Stradivari and Guarneri.
Antonio Fabiani was a violinist and professor of music. He won diplomas in Rome and studied under Andrea and Leandro Bisiach as well as Simone Sacconi.
Gennaro Fabricatore was the son and pupil of Gennaro Fabricatore (I). He primarily made guitars, and a few violins and violas in the Gagliano style are known.
Giovanni Battista Fabricatore made plucked instruments, with only a few violins known. He created them in Naples in 1811, in S.

Venetian violin maker Luigi Fabris probably learned his craft from Antonio Indri or Pietro Valentino Novello , who were the only active makers in Venice in the early 1800s. Evidence of Fabris' notoriety dates from the 1830s, so it is reasonable to assume ... he started to work independently around 1830.
Fra Agostino Facini was a monk of the Order of Saint Jean de Dieu. He created Cremonese models featuring nicely carved scrolls and a fine yellow-brown varnish.

Before moving to Turin in 1894, Annibale Fagnola was employed as a baker and seems to have had no direct relationships with any violin makers. The workshop of Marengo-Rinaldi was the only active violin making operation in Turin in the years immediately following Fagnola's arrival, and it is likely that he cultivated his first contacts there before opening his own business in 1899.
Annibalotto Fagnola was the nephew of Annibale Fagnola and an unwilling pupil of his uncle. After 1939, he made only a few instruments in his uncle's workshop, having inherited the business.
Harvey Fairbanks trained as a violinist in New York. He moved to Binghamton and began making violins with Arthur Howe around 1940.
Joseph Falaise created models based on Amati and Stradivari; they were somewhat clumsy and featured high-set soundholes. The varnish was a thin golden-brown.
Auguste Falisse apprenticed to Delinet in Mirecourt. He worked briefly for Silvestre and Maucotel in Paris, and then with Mougenot in Brussels.

Initially employed by the firm of Monzino and Sons , Erminio Farina became acquainted with colleague Riccardo Antoniazzi in the first years of the twentieth century and was quickly taken under his wing. The two collaborated closely from about 1910 until ...
Musical ambitions dashed by World War I. Apprenticed to József Metzker.
Celeste Farotti, born Celeste Farotto, became interested in violin making around 1895 at the age of 31. After some amateur experimentation, he was encouraged to move to Milan.

Trained in his uncle Celeste's workshop, many of Celestino Farotti's early instruments bear his uncle's label and are almost indistinguishable in style. After Celeste's death in 1928 Celestino began to cultivate a more individual approach, with wider purfling and softer edges.
William James Farrell was a self-taught maker. He was active from 1916 in Caledonian Road, London, and emigrated to Australia in 1920, living in Brisbane and then in Concord, near Sydney from 1923.
Maurice Fauconier was a violin and bow maker from a family of wind instrument makers. He studied under Georges Apparut in Mirecourt.
Thomas L. Fawick was an automotive engineer who became involved in violin making around 1948 in Cleveland.
Walter Richard Feiler was the son and pupil of Richard Feiler. He used models derived from Stradivari and Guarneri.

Bernard Simon Fendt worked as a nephew and pupil of François Fent at his workshop in Paris from about 1783 until François' death in 1796. In 1798, he moved to London, possibly due to events stemming from the French Revolution, and immediately joined Thomas Dodd's shop alongside J.

Bernard Simon Fendt II undoubtedly learned the craft of violin making from his father Bernhard Simon I in the workshop of John Betts , where the two men worked until the mid-1820s. Following his departure from the Betts firm, Fendt entered into several interesting partnerships, one with a teacher of dancing and fencing.
Jacob Fendt was the third son of Bernhard S. Fendt (I).
Martin Fendt was the second son of Bernhard S. Fendt (I).
William Fendt was the son and assistant of Bernard S. Fendt (II).
Luigi Fenga studied and worked in Paris, London, Rome, and Naples. He established himself in Catania from 1890.
François Fent was a pupil of A. Gedler and moved to Paris around 1760, working in Montmartre near the rue du Mail or cul-de-sac St Pierre.
Alvina de Ferenczi came from a family of important Hungarian violin makers. She immigrated to America in 1904 and lived in Colorado.

Karl (Károly) (Charles) Ferenczy-Tomasowsky, nephew of Sandor Ferenczy, apprenticed with Zach in Vienna before moving to Berlin in 1888 and later to Rotterdam in 1895. In 1902, he emigrated to New York, where he died the same year.

William Ferguson established TRTF Mary's Wynd and was likely a pupil of Matthew Hardie, employing a similar 'long Strad' pattern with a back length of 36 cm (141/8"). His work showed a strong connection to that of Blair and his cousin George Ferguson, though it was not as effective.
Eric Tore Ferm won a gold medal at Stockholm in 1948 and received a diploma at The Hague in 1949 for a Stradivari model.
Giuseppe Ferrari was a trained musician inspired by E. Sofritti, a pupil of A.
Pietro Alfonso Ferraroni was active as a luthier and sculptor from 1929. He was an associate of Digiuni, Schiavi, and Tatar.
William Ferrier was active as a photographer in Dundee from 1880. He made 30 violins of modest quality, which featured yellow varnish.
Fernandino Ferroni was a pupil of De Zorzi. He was an influential maker of the Florentine school in the 20th century, established in Borgo San Frediano.
Charles Claude Fétique studied under Paul Bailly. He worked for the firms Thibouville-Lamy and Laberte in Mirecourt.
Emile Fétique worked for Derazey and later for Gautrot before establishing himself in rue de Turenne, Paris. He made well-crafted instruments.

Jules Fétique was born in Mirecourt three years after his brother of Victor , and followed in his footsteps by apprenticing with Émile Miquel and Charles Nicolas Bazin . In 1902 ... he moved to Paris to join the workshop of Eugène Sartory , also collaborating with his brother on occasion.
Marcel Gaston Fétique was a bow maker and the son, pupil, and assistant of Victor Francois. Active in Paris since 1913, he established his workshop at 93 rue de Maubeuge in 1930.

Born in Mirecourt, Victor François Fétique was apprenticed to Charles Claude Husson and trained further with Sigisbert Maline, Émile Miquel , and Nicolas Bazin . His early bows ... bear the hallmarks of this schooling, with narrow heads and heels mounted with miter joints.
Johann Ulrich Fichtl created a Tyrolean Amati-Stainer model. The workmanship was neat, well-proportioned, and utilized good materials.

Martin Mathias Fichtl was the son and pupil of Martin Mathias Fichtl (I) and ranked among the finest early Viennese makers. He utilized a disciplined Stainer model, characterized by a beautifully finished appearance and devoid of the exaggerated traits often found in Stainer imitations.
Carl Friedrich Ficker was the son, pupil, and successor of Johann Christian Ficker III. He was admitted to the violin maker's guild in 1806 and took over his father's business in 1822.

Christian Samuel Ficker was admitted to the violin maker's guild in 1791. He produced professional work based on a Stainer-inspired model, characterized by a fairly clean and professional finish, though the detail work was done poorly.
Active in the 20th century. In 1902 set up business in Markneukirchen with his cousin, Ernst Heinrich Roth.

Johann Christian Ficker was the son and pupil of Johann Caspar Ficker in Markneukirchen and was admitted to the violin makers guild in 1755. Among the better makers of this family, he used the common Stainer model, which he executed fairly accurately.

Johann Christian Ficker was the son of Johann Christian Ficker II and a notable maker within the Ficker school. He crafted high-arched Stainer models characterized by narrow edges and well-defined purfling, with Staineresque scrolls that featured an exaggerated last turn of the eye due to an extended cut into the boss.

Johann Gottlob Ficker was the son and pupil of Johann Caspar Ficker, admitted to the violin makers guild in 1764. His work exhibited a superior quality of the typical Ficker style based on the Stainer model, characterized by a well-cut scroll featuring a concentric volute with an extended last turn behind the eye and a swan-neck pegbox.
Johann Otto Ficker was the son and pupil of Wilhelm Ficker . His career, which included fine pieces based on classical and Tyrolean models, was cut short by his death in World War I.
Georges Charles Fillion apprenticed at Mirecourt. He worked in Paris, Berne, and spent two years with Hill & Sons in London.
James Findlay worked as a weaver and was also known as a fiddle player. He was an active maker from around 1829 and was credited with 500 instruments.
Siegfried Finkel was a bow maker and a pupil of his father-in-law Paul Weidhass. He moved to Brienz, Switzerland, in 1952.
Brazilian maker based in San Paolo.
Giuseppe Antonio Finolli created the Amatisé model. The work featured fine materials and a well-made scroll.

Son of Bolognese violin maker Raffaele Fiorini , Giuseppe Fiorini first trained with his father and opened his own independent business in 1885, when he was just 24 years old. In the late 1880s Fiorini traveled to Munich to set up a partnership with Andreas Rieger, which lasted until Rieger retired in 1896, leaving Fiorini to ... run the firm.
Fictional name for a line of good quality Mirecourt violins produced by Beare and Son in London during the early 20th century. 'Modele Paolo Fiorini, Taurini / B&S faciebat anno 1923'

Generally considered the father of the modern Emilia-Romagna school, Raffaele Fiorini developed an interest in violin making around 1847 and moved to Bologna to open his own shop in 1867. Fiorini's workshop became an important starting place for a number of distinguished Bolognese makers, including Augusto Pollastri , Oreste ... and Cesare Candi , Armando Monterumici , and Raffaele's own son Giuseppe Fiorini .
A. E.
Albert William Fischer was an automotive engineer and amateur maker. He created 17 violins, one viola, and two cellos using personal models with amber-coloured varnish.
Herman Fischer was originally a bow maker active from 1895. He trained with R.
Johann Adam Fischer created decent work in the Tyrolean tradition. He applied a red-brown varnish.
Joseph Fischer was a pupil of Gedler in Füssen and worked briefly in Vienna before settling in Regensburg. His work included a large Stainer model that was well finished and accurate.
Lorenz J. Fischer trained by his grandfather and at the Austrian Violin-making School; he was also a pupil of Gagl and Reinhold Schmidt.
Philipp Jacob Fischer created a Stainer model of average achievement. The scroll was more Italianate, featuring a short last turn, but it appeared somewhat clumsy overall.
Zacharias Fischer was the son and pupil of P. J.
Carl Fischer Inc. was directed by Louis Havermann as violin importers and dealers. They retailed the work of H.

Léon Fischesser worked in Geneva in 1885, Staufen in Baden in 1900, Mulhausen in 1904, and Paris in the Faubourg Poissonière in 1905, later moving to Avenue de Villiers in 1914. He collaborated with Lucien Greilsamer on varnish research around 1906.
Charles F. Fivaz established his work in Essex Road.
Charles Flambeau created unremarkable workmanship. The spirit varnish appeared dull brown.
Pierre Flambeau trained in Mirecourt and with Koliker in Paris. His work featured a recognizable imitation of the Stradivarian model and was sturdy, finished with yellow-brown varnish.
William Fleck was an amateur violinist and maker. He produced good work based on Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Harry Fleisher was a part-time bow maker. A silver diamond or violin Thole was inlaid in the frog.
Ignacio Fleta joined his elder brother Manuel in Barcelona. He made fine violins and cellos that featured rich golden-yellow varnish.
Albert Fleury established his workshop on rue Linn and worked in the style of Fetique. His late bows were rather exaggerated, featuring a deep throat to the frog.
Benoit Fleury created a flat-arched model using plain materials. The small scroll was located on the extended pegbox, and the soundholes were crudely cut and widely set.
H. Fleury made bows that featured strong sticks, modeled after Tourte, and typically included whalebone lapping.
Nicolas Florentin created a large pattern featuring flat arching and widely-set 'f' holes. The workmanship was ordinary.
Hans Flück studied with König at the Brienz School of Violin making in 1944. He was employed by A.
Edouard Foetisch studied at Markneukirchen. He worked for M8ckel in Berlin, Hammig in Markneukirchen, and in Leipzig, Dresden, Frankfurt, Stuttgart, Vienna, and Mirecourt.
Claude Joseph Fonclause was a bow maker who was presumed to be a pupil of É. Pajeot and was active in Paris from around 1832.
Alfredo Fontana was a pupil of Carletti in Pieve di Cento. He made violins and viols.
Aristide Fontanini was the son and pupil of Andrea Fontanini. He served as the Director of the Violin-making School at Salò from 1946 to 1953.

Jacob Ford worked from various addresses, including 'corner of Davies Street and Berkeley Square' and 'South-Street, Grosvenor Square', between 1770 and 1795. He was a 'Gentlemen's Servant' by profession before becoming a lodging-house keeper.

Youngest son of William Forster III , Simon Andrew Forster followed in the family tradition of violin making and studied with his father and brother William IV. His instruments fall into two categories: lesser examples bearing a spirit varnish signed "Forster," and higher quality ones signed, "S.A.

Though William Forster II was known as "Old Forster" to distinguish him from his son William III , he himself was the son of William Forster I , who is credited with the earliest surviving instruments by a member of the family. According to the thorough ... family biography composed by his grandson Simon Andrew Forster , William II made violins, along with spinning wheels and gun stocks, in addition to being a successful music publisher.

Third in a line of important London violin makers, William Forster III made his first instrument in 1779 and worked alongside his father from that time onward. Under his tenure the family business continued to engage in a motley assortment of activities, including an ill-fated foray into the greengrocer trade that precipitated Forster's death in 1824.
Giovanni Foschini studied under Primo Contavalli . He received a silver medal for quartet at Bagnacavallo in 1982.
Fourier-Magnie made commercial instruments at the Laberte Factory in Mirecourt, France. The year was 19..
French bow maker based in Quimper. Studied with Gilles Duhaut.
Arturo Fracassi was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of C.
Emile Marcel Français was a violin and bow maker, the son and pupil of Henri Français . He worked for Fenzel in Markneukirchen and for Lyon & Healy in Chicago from 1912 to 1914.
Lucien Français worked in Paris and London before joining Jacquot in Nancy, where he established his own shop in 1922. His instruments featured a Stradivari model with dark red oil varnish.
Celso Franchi was active from 1922. He created classical models with orange or brown varnish.
Meinradus Frank created fine work on a large personal model. The middle bouts were broad and straight.
Alfred Franke was the son, pupil, and assistant of Paul Franke . He established himself independently in Dusseldorf in 1948.
Max Franke studied under Otto Jaeger in Frankfurt from 1894 to 1898. He then worked for H.
Paul Franke was the elder brother of Max Franke . He died as a result of the workshop being destroyed by Allied bombing in 1945.
Rodolph Franke was the son and assistant of Max Franke . His workshop in Weisbaden was destroyed while he was a prisoner-of-war.
Patrix Franot created some instruments in the large, flat Mirecourt model, as well as other examples in the Maggini style featuring double purfling. They had yellow or brown varnish.
Hans Franze trained with M. Schmidt in Marneukirchen.

Jean-Baptiste Frebrunet was recognized by Henley, following Lütgendorff, as a fine craftsman of the `vieux Paris' school, creating instruments based on an Amatisé model featuring coarsely-cut scrolls and red-brown varnish. Some instruments bore a fleur de lys brand on the back.
Count Fabio Fredi was a titled aristocrat and an enthusiastic luthier. He produced high-quality work on a personal model.

Rodolfo Fredi was the son and pupil of Count Fabio Fredi and moved to Rome in 1889 to study at the Conservatoire, ultimately establishing a business at 24 Via Vicenza. He was an accomplished maker, credited with 450 violins, 70 violas, and 50 cellos, as well as double basses and viols, using models primarily derived from Stradivari. ...
Paul Richard Friedel studied under Nürnberger in Markneukirchen and later worked for Koch in Tressingen. His instruments were marked with a triangular monogram: A.

John Friedrich was a pupil of Schonger in Kassel and worked for Möckel in Berlin, Hammig in Leipzig, and other locations in Germany. In 1883, he emigrated to New York and established the company John Friedrich & Bro. with his brother William.
Maximillian (Miksa) Frirsz was a leading maker of his time and period. He studied under Paul Pilat from 1899 to 1902.
Maximillian (Miksa) Frirsz. Son and pupil of Miksa Frirsz (I).
Jean Fritsch apprenticed to Dieudonne in Mirecourt. He worked for Serdet in Paris and established his own premises there in 1934.
Robert Fritsch was the brother of Jean Fritsch . He studied under Dieudonné and Enel.
Johann Benjamin Fritzsche was the brother of Carl August Fritzsche. He was appointed Maker to the Dresden court.
Mario Antonio Frosali trained as a violinist at the Florence Conservatoire. He was active as a violin maker there starting in 1932, before emigrating to the USA.
Studied violin making with Anders Rasmussen in Aarhus, Jutland, until 1910, when he opened his first workshop. In 1917 he moved to Aalborg and worked until 1927, when he went bankrupt.
John Charles Fryer was an associate of M. W.

Born in Lausanne, Switzerland, Pierre-Yves Fuchs initially learnt cabinetry, then violin making and finally apprenticed in bow making with Gilles Duhaut, Pierre Guillaume and Benoit Rolland. He has won several international awards: Gold medal contrabass, 3 awards of merit violin, viola, violoncello USA (VSA) 2000 Gold medal viola, 3 awards of merit violin, violoncello, contrabass ...
Henry Fuller created a Stradivari and Guarneri model with good workmanship. Some were made on a small model, featuring golden-red to red-brown shades of varnish.
Annibal Fulquet was the son, pupil, and successor of Sébastian Fulquet. He produced fine work based on classical models, utilizing excellent materials and a fine transparent varnish.
David Furber apprenticed traditionally in London to John Johnson. His signature appeared inside work that bore the brand of Longman & Co.

Henry John Furber was the son and pupil of John Furber and worked for John Betts, specializing in copies of the 1704 'Betts' Stradivari violin. Established in Grafton Street from 1825, he was located at 50 Britannia Row, Islington from 1846 to 1864, and at 79 Dean Street in 1885.
John Furber worked in Turnmill Street in 1810 and at 10 St John's Row from 1813. He moved to 26 Wellington Street in 1840.
Matthew Furber was the son or younger brother of David Furber . He was possibly a pupil of Thomas Smith .
Georg Fürst worked with brown varnish that was undistinguished. He was located in Mittenwald an der Iser in 1790.

Giovanni Battista Gabrielli was a pre-eminent maker of the Florentine school. He may have received some instruction from Bartolomeo Cristofori , who had worked for Nicolò Amati before arriving in Florence in 1709, where he obtained a royal appointment.

Sole pupil and true follower of Stefano Scarampella , Gaetano Gadda began to learn the trade of violin making in 1919. The two worked side by side during Scarampella's later years, with Gadda playing a crucial role in the production of his instruments, and in 1924 he agreed to supply Scarampella with one violin per month in exchange ... for his teacher's tools and models.
Joint instruments by Gaetano and Mario Gadda tend to appear after 1950, until Gaetano's death in 1956. Mario continued to copy his father's work after this point.

Mario Gadda was the son, pupil, and successor of Gaetano Gadda, establishing himself in Sant'Antonio di Porto Mantovano. A prolific maker, he worked with many patterns and specialized in copies of Balestrieri and Scarampella.
Maker and cellist based in Bienne, Switzerland.
Joseph Gaffino apprenticed to Castagneri in Paris early in the 18th century. He established his own workshop in 1742 at the sign of 'la Musette de Colin' on rue des Prouvaires.
Pierre Gaggini was the nephew and pupil of Albert Louis Blanchi, with whom he served his apprenticeship. In 1924 he came to Paris for three years to fulfill his military service.

Alessandro Gagliano was not only the first in this great and prolific family of violin makers, but also the first known Neapolitan maker. Little is known about his training, and his highly individual work is quite unlike that of the rest of his family.
Antonio Gagliano was the third son of Nicola Gagliano (I) and worked alongside his brother Giuseppe. His instruments often featured decorative inlay.
Antonio Gagliano was the son of Giovanni Gagliano and worked in partnership with his brother Raffaele. Their instruments were noted for their good sound but lacked distinction in workmanship.

Only recently has credible biographical information concerning the violin makers of the Neapolitan school come to light. Until the experts who have researched this material publish their archival findings, we leave the birth and death dates of these makers as previously accepted.

Ferdinando Gagliano, son of Nicola Gagliano, likely trained primarily under his uncle Gennaro. His work exhibited elements from both, characterized by a more restrained craftsmanship, with varnish typically thinner and harder.

Only recently has credible biographical information concerning the violin makers of the Neapolitan school come to light. Until the experts who have researched this material publish their archival findings, we leave the birth and death dates of these makers as previously accepted.

Alessandro Gagliano's two sons Nicolò and Gennaro are said to be the finest makers of the family, but of these two Gennaro's work may be the more highly prized. His instruments are of a personal character, but unlike his father he often employed ... href="/cozio-archive/browse-the-archive/makers/maker/?Maker_ID=722">Stradivari and Amati models.

Giovanni Gagliano, son of Nicolò Gagliano , initially worked with his brothers Giuseppe and Antonio before developing a distinctive style of his own. He faced challenges due to the relatively inferior quality of varnish used during his time, which often had a slightly greenish tint from the dark stained ground beneath.

Giuseppe Gagliano, son and pupil of Nicola Gagliano, was an effective and consistent maker during his career, which spanned from 1770 to 1800. While he initially attained a level of craftsmanship that rivaled his father's, his later partnership with his brother Antonio led to a general decline in quality.

A somewhat lesser maker from the Gagliano family, Antonio sometimes collaborated with his brother Giuseppe . Their combined efforts, however, rarely produced instruments as visually appealing and skillfully crafted than those of Giuseppe alone.
Sons of Nicolo and worked in partnership. Only recently has credible biographical information concerning the violin makers of the Neapolitan school come to light.

Among the great makers of the Neapolitan tradition, Nicolò Gagliano, son of Alessandro , stands out for his consistently high standards of craftsmanship and sound quality. His instruments are among the most highly sought-after of the Gagliano family.
Sons of Giovanni and business partners. Their violins are mainly of commercial quality, some with broad and ugly f-holes, indifferently varnished, some with double purfling, others with none.

Vincenzo Gagliano, the son and pupil of Raffaele Gagliano, worked alongside his father in Strada Monte Oliveto. He joined the workshop of Raffaele and Antonio Gagliano before their deaths in 1857 and 1860, contributing to the final instruments produced by the brothers, particularly cellos.
Giovanni Battista Gaibisso was a pupil of Pasquale Baraldi in Alassio. He established himself in Alassio, Genoa, after Baraldi's death in 1896.
Cesare Gaida was the son and assistant of Giovanni Gaida . He produced fine work based on classical models, but his production under his own label was small.
Began work in France. In London from 1890, but returned to Ivrea 1895-1904 where his sons Silvio and Cesare were born.
Charles Gaillard was a violin and bow maker, the son of Louis Gaillard. He was the first apprentice of C.A.
Jules Gaillard-Lajoue was the younger brother of Charles Gaillard . He received a silver medal at the 1855 Paris Exhibition for his good work.
Alfredo G. Galea studied under Coschiera in Alexandria around 1920.
Adelino Galeazzi was a musician and sculptor who collaborated with his brother Eugenio Galeazzi . The varnish used was a clear golden light or pinkish-red brown.
Eugenio Galeazzi was the brother and assistant of Adelino Galeazzi . The work was not sophisticated and featured a dark brown varnish.
Luigi Galimberti worked as a pupil of Rottola and Antoniazzi. He was employed by the Monzino company from 1928 to 1930.
Anton Galla was active in Prerov in 1926 and in Kosice, Slovakia until 1938. He subsequently established himself in Brno.

Luc Gallicane was a proponent of a varnish recipe that claimed to date from 1716, known as the 'Secret Processes of Van Eyck. ' He applied the varnish to instruments produced in factories in Mirecourt. He received medals at Geneva in 1927.
Pietro Gallinotti won medals at Geneva, Rome in 1931, and Bologna in 1936. He worked with Stradivari and Guarneri (1743 'Cannone' pattern) models, demonstrating very precise craftsmanship.
Tetto Gallo worked for George Einsele in Brunswick, Indiana, from 1925 to 1955 before establishing himself in Chicago Heights. He specialized in varnishing and antique finishes.
Claudio Gamberini was a pupil of Mozzani. He produced good quality commercial work.

Charles Adolphe Gand, son and pupil of Charles François Gand, became active around 1830 and took over the family business in 1845, running it alone until 1855 when he collaborated with his brother Eugéne under the name 'Gand Frères' until 1866. He held the title of Luthier de la Musique du Roi et du Conservatoire de Musique and was later recognized as Luthier du Conservatoire Imperial de Musique. ...

Charles Eugène Gand was the son of Charles François Gand and a pupil of both his father and his brother, Charles Adolphe. He trained as a violinist under Baillot at the Paris Conservatoire from 1840 to 1842.

Known as Gand père, Charles François Gand was born in Versailles and apprenticed with Nicolas Lupot in Paris from 1802 to 1806. During this time he worked alongside and taught August Sébastien Philippe Bernardel , whose sons would later join Gand's ... son Charles Nicolas Eugène to form the firm of Gand & Bernardel .

Francisco Gand was likely from the Gand family of Mirecourt. Paramon noted that his work was "well made, with poor wood and undistinguished varnish. " Jacquot referenced a viola featuring a transparent yellow varnish, labelled: Echo por Franco Gand en Madrid, año 1788.

Guillaume Charles Louis Gand was the second son of Charles Michel Gand and learned the craft from his father before taking over his father's shop in Versailles. His work was rare and reflected the style of his father.

Two important houses of violin making and three makers formed the basis of the prolific and successful Parisian firm of Gand and Bernardel: Charles Nicolas Eugène Gand and the Bernardel brothers Gustave and Ernest . ... The firm was established as Gand & Bernardel Frères in 1866, after the death of Charles Eugène's brother Charles Adolphe Gand .
Brothers Charles Adolphe and Charles Nicolas Eugène Gand, who partnered as Gand Frères from 1855-1866.
Giuseppe Gandolfi worked for Mozzani and Gamberini. He produced good work with gold-red varnish.
William Ganshirt trained with Walter Goss, J. A.
Pearson Gardiner worked in the Duke style, which was seldom seen and rarely labelled. His craftsmanship was quite precise.

A student of Romeo and Riccardo Antoniazzi , Ferdinando Garimberti worked for Leandro Bisiach and Giuseppe Pedrazzini ... before starting his own shop in Milan around 1920, where he performed repairs of antique instruments in addition to making new violins. His work, which shows great consistency over time, displays exceptional craftsmanship and elegance and fine attention to detail.

Eugen Gärtner established his own workshop in Stuttgart in 1891 after studying under Sprenger in Stuttgart and Simoutre in Paris. He was appointed as the maker and repairer for the King of Württemberg.
George Gaskin was an amateur maker. One instrument received endorsement from Czech violinist and composer Jan Kubelik.

Not much is known about Giorgio Gatti's background apart from his arrival in Turin in 1890. Among the active Turinese makers of the period, including Francesco Guadagnini and Romano Marengo 'Rinaldi,' Enrico ...

Joseph Gaudé trained in the Adam style but may have worked for Pajeot around 1835. He established himself independently in 1839, though he appeared to have declined and vanished from the Mirecourt records after 1881.
François Jude Gaulard was the son of Louis Gaulard. Early works indicated the influence of Pajeot.
Willis M. Gault founded the Gault School for the Professional Making and Appreciation of Bowed Instruments in 1950.
Pierre Gauthie studied under P. Lorange.
Georges Gavalda studied violin at Montpellier Conservatoire. He was taught by Huetter and later established himself in Villefranche.
Alcide Gavatelli studied under G. Antoniazzi.
François Gavinies established a workshop in Bordeaux on the rue des Minimes and later in Paris on rue St-Thomas-du-Louvre. He was appointed head of the Corporation of Luthiers in 1762.
William George Geary received guidance in violin making from W. B.
Johann Anton Gedler was the son of Norbert Gedler, who died young. He was brought up and trained by Sympertus Niggel.
Key founder of the 19th-century Viennese school. Geissenhof was born in Füssen (Allgäu) and moved to Vienna in 1772.

August Martin Gemünder, son of August Martin Ludwig, assisted his father from 1875 and took over the August Gemünder firm with his brother Rudolph upon their father's retirement in 1890. He was the creator of the "Gemünder Art Violins" and published 'The Violin World' journal starting in 1892.
August Martin Ludwig Gemunder was the son and pupil of Johann Georg Heinrich Gemünder. He worked in Regensburg and emigrated to America in 1846.
German–American violin maker George (originally Georg) Gemünder worked for J.B. Vuillaume in Paris before joining his brothers in Massachussets in 1847.
George Gemünder was the son and pupil of George Gemünder I, known as George Jr. He began working with his father in the 1870s and became a partner in the firm in 1882.
Hermann Ludwig Gemunder was the son and pupil of George Gemünder (I). He had a small output.

Oscar Henry Gemünder was the youngest son of August Gemünder and took over the family firm, August Gemünder & Sons, in 1928 after the death of his brother, August Martin. His work focused on the 'Gemünder Art Model' as well as Cremonese and Maggini models, which were mostly labeled with his father's name and countersigned by him.
Michel Genin apprenticed as a bow maker in Mirecourt, possibly under a member of the Adam family. He was the brother-in-law of P.

Giovanni Battista Genova rarely encountered as a maker; no evidence of his origins emerged from archives in Turin or Genoa. Speculative connections with Guadagnini and Guidantus lacked historical support.

Riccardo Genovese was a musician and pupil of Fagnola, active in Montiglio starting in 1922, before working in Lecco from 1927 and returning to Asti in the last year of his life. He began to pursue violin making seriously around 1922, and his early work appears less experienced compared to his output after 1930.
Carl H. George migrated to Toronto, Canada in 1886 with his family and moved to Chicago, USA in 1896.
Pierre Gerber was a pupil of Richard Meinel. He later worked for Millant-Deroux in Paris.
Émile Germain was the son of Joseph Louis Germain . He apprenticed in Mirecourt starting in 1865.
Joseph Louis Germain trained in Mirecourt before relocating to Paris in 1840. He worked for C.
Bows stamped Roger Gerome or Gerome-Devoivre. Employed Georges Barjonnet.
Giovanni Gerosa created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. He used fine wood and spirit varnish.
Treffle R. Gervais worked for Elias Howe from 1885 to 1886, and subsequently for A.
Giuseppe Giacchetti studied the violin at the Rome Conservatoire. He was a pupil of Rodolfo Fredi in lutherie.
Simeone Giamberini created a typical Florentine work featuring a golden-yellow varnish. The model was derived from Stainer.
Fabrizio Giannini created a personal model, some featuring extravagant carved ornamentation. The varnish was golden-yellow, utilizing both oil and spirit recipes.
Alfredo Gianotti learned the basic craft while a prisoner of war in the USA from 1943, with guidance from R. Bechini.
Arthur William Gibbons was a pupil of W. J.
Antonio Gibertini was active in Parma from 1820 to 1833 and in Genoa from 1834 to 1845, returning to Parma in 1845. He was known as a skilled repairer and worked for Paganini on occasion.

Giulio Cesare Gigli, born around 1724, apprenticed to Michael Platner in 1738 and became his successor in 1753 after Platner's death in 1752. He inherited the workshop, materials, and tools, continuing to work until his death in 1794.
Jeffrey James Gilbert was the son of Jeffrey Gilbert and largely self-taught. He established himself in New Priestgate, Peterborough in 1875.
Simon Gilbert was presumed to be the son of Nicolas Gilbert. He served as a violinist in the Cathedral Orchestra at Metz from 1745 to 1768.
James Gilchrist trained as a scientific instrument maker and engineer. He lived in Glasgow from 1850 and became active as an amateur maker starting in 1880.

One of England's great cello makers, Samuel Gilkes first worked for William Forster III starting around 1810. He may have had a small role in training Forster's son Simon Andrew , but Simon minimizes this relationship in his account.
William Gilkes was the son and pupil of Samuel Gilkes. He established his business at 14 Dartmouth Street in 1835.
C. Lovett Gill was an amateur maker credited with approximately 30 instruments of good workmanship.

Louis Henri Gillet was born in Nancy but probably did his early training in Mirecourt. He worked for Jérôme Thibouville-Lamy from 1906 to 1911 and served in WWI before ending up at the Laberte firm in 1921.

Benedetto Gioffredo was initially employed as a shoe maker, and took up the violin trade seriously only after relocating to Turin in 1852 with his wife Paola. In 1855 his father-in-law Teobaldo Rinaldi joined them, presumably to help run the shop thereafter known as 'Gioffredo-Rinaldi,' or simply 'Rinaldi,' which Gioffredo adopted as a sort of surname over the course of his career.
Giovanni Antonio Giorgetti created coarse unfinished work based on a Stradivari-derived outline. He used a golden-orange opaque varnish.
Mario Girardi worked in Milan from 1920 and in Trieste from 1930. He exhibited in Cremona in 1937.
Andrea Gisalberti created very coarse work. The 'c' bouts were uneven and deeply rounded, with projecting corners.
Armando Giulietti made guitars and also produced violins, violas, and cellos of good quality. He used red spirit varnish.
Robert Gladstone applied his label to factory instruments imported from Mirecourt or Saxony.
August Hermann Glaesel was born in Markneukirchen, Germany. The model was large and flat; it was well-made but featured unsophisticated red varnish.
Carl Wilhelm Glaesel was admitted to the Markneukirchen guild in 1792. He was said to have worked for Thir in Vienna and was also active in Berlin.
Conrad Glaesel worked in Leipzig, Berlin, Brussels, and Chemnitz before returning to Brussels in 1937. He received gold medals at Brussels and Turin.
Edmond Glaesel was the son and pupil of Ludwig Glaesel (I). He was later assisted by his son Theo.
Friedrich August Glaesel was a violin and guitar maker. He was associated with the Hopf school and produced decent but limited work.

Ludwig Glaesel was a member of the firm `Glaesel and Herwig' starting in 1867 and established his own workshop around 1889. He received several medals at international exhibitions and was a commercial manufacturer of instruments in graded qualities.

Glaesel & Mössner operated a commercial manufactuary in Markneuldrchen from 1873, managed by Ernst Glaesel, the son of Johann Ludwig Glaesel. Various grades and styles were identifiable by inscribed shields carved into the back of the scroll.

Christian Friedrich Glass was a violin and bow maker associated with the Hopf school. He created instruments with a rather square outline featuring outward pointing corners and purfling set very close to the edge.
Franz Johann Glass was the son and pupil of Johann Traugott, established in Strenwartensrasse, Leipzig from 1879. He was succeeded by his son, Johann, below.
Friedrich A. Glass was the son of Johann Traugott Glass .
Friedrich August Glass acknowledged good work on the Stainer model on his label. The varnish was a red-brown color of good consistency.
Friedrich August Glass produced fine professional work and was awarded a gold medal in London in 1851. His models were inspired by Stradivari and Stainer, and he used a yellow brown varnish.
William Glenister established himself at Beak Street as a businessman and self-taught amateur maker. He favored classical models, particularly the 1690 Stradivari 'Tuscan' violin.
Hermann Glier established Bellevue according to Vannes. His place of work was Cincinnati, Ohio, as stated by Wenberg.
Johann Gottleib Glier was the most successful maker of the family. He created a Tyrolean model featuring a high arch and a golden-brown varnish.
Robert C. Glier trained in Markneukirchen.
Son of Robert I and successor to his Cincinnati shop.
John Gloag was an amateur maker known for well-made Stradivari copies. A manuscript label indicated: John Gloag / Galston / Jan.. 19..
Adolf Gloor created a well-finished work based on the Stradivari model, although the detail was uncertain. He was located in Zurich at Riedackerstr. 6 in 19..

Research suggests that Francesco Gobetti may have learned the craft of violin making at home. After arriving in Venice in 1699, Gobetti got work as a shoemaker and continued to work in that capacity, and there is no evidence that he was employed at any of the important violin shops in Venice.

Francesco Goffriller, son of Matteo Goffriller, left his father's workshop in Venice in 1714 to work in Udine. He seems to have predeceased Matteo and produced instruments characterized by a coarser finish and slightly less discipline than those of his father, yet they possessed great character, enhanced by the beautiful dark red varnish and golden ground typical of Venice.

Considered to be the father of the Venetian school, Matteo Goffriller was born in the Alpine town of Bressanone close to Bolzano, where he may have been a student of Matthias Albani . After moving to Venice in 1685 to apprentice with Mathias Kaiser , ... he married Kaiser's daughter and by 1690 had inherited his teacher and father-in-law's business.
Robert Gofton was an amateur maker credited with 30 violins of decent quality.
Friedrich Goldnagl graduated from the Violin Making School in 1965 after studying under Sgarabotto in Cremona. He worked in Vienna and was later appointed as a senior restorer in Florence.
Jacob Heinrich Goldt was the son of Samuel Goldt. He primarily made lutes and viols, with a few violins produced that were considered unremarkable, although they featured fine varnish.
Karel Goll created classical models. He utilized fine materials and spirit varnish.
Aurelio Gonzalez was the nephew and pupil of Aurelio Aureli . He apprenticed to Bertucci and won a silver medal in Rome in 1930.

Solar Fernando Gonzalez was a maker of guitars, violins, and bows. He established his workshop at 3 Calle del Rollo and was appointed luthier to the Royal Palace, where he was responsible for its Stradivari quartet of decorated instruments and guitars.
Amateur violin maker who worked 1920-1938. Instruments are numbered on label.
Bert Goodwin was born in Canterbury, UK. He studied violin repairing under his grandfather, John Lucas Goodwin.
Hugh Gordon was born in Stoneyford, County Antrim. He was active in Belfast from 1825.
James Gorrie created an amateur maker's large Guarneri-derived model, occasionally featuring factory-made heads. He used commercial varnish produced by Whitelaw in yellow or red shades.
Walter Solon Goss moved to Lynn to work for the Musicians' Supply Co. and Brown Music. He established himself independently there in 1896.
Jean Gosselin was born in Bordeaux. He studied under Koliker in Paris and worked in the Stradivari model.

Anselmo Gotti was the nephew of Orsolo Gotti and a violinist who studied under Ettore Soffritti in Ferrara starting in 1922. After establishing himself independently in Ferrara, he completed several instruments left unfinished by Soffritti after his death in 1928.
Orsolo Gotti studied under Carletti, Soffritti, and Pollastri in Bologna. He won first prize in Rome in 1918.
Commercial production.
Johann Michael Gotz trained as a woodcarver. He was a prolific violin maker known for his medium high, flat-topped arches.
John Alfred Gould trained in Liverpool with W. F.
Goulding imported French and Saxon instruments as a commercial business. He also sold work made for him by Thomas Kennedy, James Brown , and other London craftsmen of the period.
Guglielmo Govoni trained as a sculptor. He had a small output and created models based on Stradivari and Gagliano.
Michael Gozzo was a pupil of his grandfather, who shared the same name. He emigrated to the USA and associated with George Lindsey in Los Angeles starting in 1926.

Antonio Gragnani probably studied in Florence, though his style differs considerably from that of late-18th century Florentine makers. He worked in Livorno, whose seaside location may have afforded him different materials from his contemporaries some 50 miles inland.
Younger son of Antonio Gragnani.

Onorato Gragnani was the son and pupil of Antonio Gragnani. His work was very similar to that of his father, though generally heavier and comparatively less refined, with the scroll being crudely finished.

Giovanni and Francesco were important figures in the 17th-century Milanese school. Many of the early Grancino work has been assumed to be by Giovanni and Francesco, but there is little definitive evidence of Francesco's role.
Born in 1637, Giovanni Grancino was one of the finest makers of the Milanese school. It is likely that his father Andrea, and perhaps even his grandfather Francesco, were also violin makers.

Son of leading 17th-century Milanese violin maker Giovanni Grancino, Giovanni Battista assisted his father in his successful workshop on the Contrada Larga, and was probably responsible for much of its output by the end of the century.
