Sadly a quarrel with rival violin maker Antonio Maria Lavazza resulted in the fatal stabbing of Lavazza, after which Giovanna Battista was sentenced to death in 1708. ...

According to the Hills in their book on the Guarneri family, he was a pupil of Nicola Amati; however, no evidence has been discovered to confirm this or even the existence of him. Instruments exist with his label, but determining authenticity is difficult.
Jean-Baptiste Grand-Gérard branded his work as from Paris but utilized the Mirecourt style. He created a large flat model finished with yellow varnish.

This Italianised name appeared on instruments crafted by J. Thibouville-Lamy in Mirecourt during the 20th century, although the labels indicated "Paris. " Instruments were produced in various grades and featured a red-brown varnish.
Jules Joseph Grandjon was the son, pupil, and successor of Prosper Gérard Grandjon. He initially worked in Paris and Amsterdam, where he took over the business of his brother L.

Prosper Gérard Grandjon was a prolific maker of various classical models, known for their good proportions and fine craftsmanship. He utilized quality materials and applied various shaded varnish colors.
Andre Granier was the son and successor of Edmond Granier. He trained with Brugere and Louis Bazin in Mirecourt.
Thomas Grater established a workshop in Birmingham on Ryland Street in 1874. He produced decent, unpretentious work based on various classical models.

William Green was associated with William Bent in Boston from 1798 to 1800 and with Deacon Allen in Medway from 1815 to 1818. He created American 'church basses' that featured a 'through-neck' design and did not have a separate top-block.
George William Greenwood was a pupil of Hesketh in Manchester. He established his own business in Rochdale in 1912.
Manfred Gregoric studied in Mittenwald. He worked for Wurlitzer in New York, then later in Philadelphia and Los Angeles.
Joseph Grienberger created instruments that were rarely seen. The model was a coarse Stradivari, featuring a well-carved scroll, fine soundholes, and dark golden-brown varnish.
Samuel Grierson created a model of Alexander Smillie . The head was commercially made and featured a soft amber varnish.
Guy Aubrey Griffin emigrated to Sydney, Australia, in 1924. He served as an assistant to A.
Karl Grimm was a musician and violin maker. His work was coarse, but the varnish was decent.
Otto Groh was active in Göttingen from 1903. He produced fine copies of classical instruments.
Leonard O. Grover worked as a music teacher and violin repairer.

Husband and wife team, Joseph Grubaugh and Sigrun Seifert began making instruments together in 1982. They have received multiple awards and prizes for tone and workmanship, including five Gold Medals and four Silver Medals from the VSA.
C. P.

Pietro Grulli, one of the last traditional late Cremonese violin makers, initially made pianos with Francesco Ghisi before turning to violin making during the wars of Italian independence in the 1860s when demand for pianos declined. His violins, derived from Amati, often feature poorly finished work, with awkward outlines and imprecise purfling.
Contemporary German bow maker taught by his father, Richard. Joint owner of the Grunke family workshop in Bubenreuth.
Bow maker born in Poland. Trained by Pfretzschner and later worked with Roderich Paesold.
Contemporary bow maker.

Antonio Guadganini, son of Gaetano II , was one of the last makers of this Turin family and was responsible for expanding the commercial activity of the family workshop. The connection to workshops in Mirecourt and Paris, especially J.B.

Carlo Guadagnini was the son, pupil, and successor of Giovanni Battista Guadagnini. After his father's death, he worked alongside his brother Gaetano as Tratelli Guadagnini, making guitars and performing violin repairs and adjustments.
Little is known about the brief life of Felice Guadagnini, grandson of G.B. Guadagnini .
Second only to his great-grandfather G.B. Guadagnini in productivity, Francesco Guadagnini inherited the family workshop in 1881 at the age of 18.
G.B. Guadagnini's grandson Gaetano II inherited the family workshop in 1817, when he was 21 years old.

Gaetano Francesco Lorenzo Guadagnini was the son, pupil, and successor of Giovanni Battista Guadagnini. In 1771, he preceded his father from Parma to Turin, but the family joined him shortly thereafter.

G.B. Guadagnini is widely regarded as the greatest violin maker of the second half of the 18th century, a title he earned through 44 years of labor and experimentation, perseverance in a multitude of regional markets, and an original style that produced some of the best instruments in history.

Giuseppe Guadagnini was the second son of Giovanni Battista Guadagnini and was known as '11 Soldate'. He was a pupil and assistant to his father, working alongside his brothers Gaetano, Carlo, and Fillippo.

Although tales dating from at least the early 19th century allege that Lorenzo Guadagnini, father of the renowned Giovanni Battista , studied with Antonio Stradivari , recent research does not support this theory of supposed lineage between G.B. Guadagnini ... and the great Cremonese tradition.
Paolo Guadagnini was the son and pupil of Francesco Guadagnini and the last in a 250-year lineage of violin makers. He first studied with his father and later collaborated with Annibale Fagnola .

Several great violin makers apprenticed directly with Nicolò Amati , and among those Andrea Guarneri holds the highest reputation as the proponent of the Amati tradition. He trained with Amati in two distinct periods from 1641 to 1654, with a four-year gap starting in 1646, and adopted his model so masterfully that some of Guarneri's ... works are indistinguishable from his teacher's.

Giuseppe Guarneri's two sons Pietro of Venice and Giuseppe 'del Gesù' both trained with and assisted him in the 1710s, but in keeping with the individuality of the Guarneri tradition their conceptions of violin making developed along strikingly dissimilar ... lines. While Pietro earns respect for his deft combination of Cremonese and Venetian styles, the rich and complex tonal quality of 'del Gesù''s instruments distinguish him as one of the greatest violin makers in history.

Giuseppe Guarneri I, known as 'filius Andreae' to distinguish him from his son Giuseppe "del Gesù" , inherited his father Andrea's shop in 1698. He had already been working for his father for quite some time, and in fact many of Andrea's later instruments ... were primarily made by Giuseppe.

Older brother of Giuseppe Guarneri filius Andrea , Pietro Guarneri began to work in his father Andrea's shop from an early age, around 1670. Equally talented as a violinist and violin maker, Pietro relocated to Mantua in the 1680s to further his playing ... opportunities at the court.

Pietro Guarneri, the elder son of Giuseppe 'filius Andreae', should rightly have been first in line to inherit his father's shop in Cremona, but after 10 years of apprenticeship in the family business he left for Venice in 1717. Violin making in Cremona was at that time dominated almost exclusively by the Stradivari family, which made doing business more difficult for the Guarneris, and Pietro found ... job security in Venice, where he was employed in the Sellas workshop alongside Carlo Tononi .

Alfredo Guastalla was a pupil of Scarampella in Mantua and worked alongside his brother Dante Guastalla . The brothers were prolific makers, credited with over 250 violins, violas, cellos, and double basses.
Dante Guastalla was the elder brother of Alfredo Guastalla . He studied under Scarampella in Mantua and worked independently in Reggiolo for a short time before Alfredo joined him.
Alexandre Sauveur Guérin was a pupil and successor of E. Daniel on rue Saint-Suffren.
Alberto Guerra was a pupil of Pollastri. He created several models that were well-made and featured a golden-red oil varnish.

Initially a violinist, Evasio Emilio Guerra showed an early talent for violin making and is thought to have received his training from Carlo Oddone and Romano Marengo Rinaldi , by extension. His modest financial circumstances required him to work quickly, ... and his lack of business acumen restricted him to building instruments almost entirely for other makers, including Fagnola , Oddone, Marengo Rinaldi and Francesco Guadagnini , supplying violins in the white.

Louis Guersan was a pupil and successor of Claude Pierray, becoming a member of the corporation of luthiers in 1748 and making head in 1769. His workshop was located in the rue Fossé-St-Germain des Prés, where he was appointed as maker and repairer to the Dauphin and to the Opéra.
Contemporary maker born in Spilamberto and currently based in San Damaso, near Modena. Initially self-taught, then a pupil of Ansaldo Poggi in Bologna from 1969 to 1980.

Though the familial relationship between them is unclear, historical documentation shows that Giovanni Guidanti inherited Floriano Guidanti's position as repairer of the Bologna Accademia Filarmonica's instruments shortly after his death in 1715. There is some evidence that Giovanni was well regarded as a maker, yet there is ... little extant information about his working life.

One of the best Spanish violin makers, especially in workmanship and style, was Joan Guillamí of Barcelona. He worked from 1721 to 1767 in his own shop, building instruments whose construction is heavily inspired by Stradivari .

Juan Guillami 'filius' was the son of Juan Guillami I and took over the workshop after his father's death. His work was influenced by his father, though it was somewhat less disciplined and utilized inferior materials.

Known as 'Le Messin', he was said to have trained with Pressenda in Turin (Vannes), although there appears to be no actual connection. He worked for several Italian workshops and established himself in Bordeaux in 1832.

Charles Joseph Théodore Guinot established himself independently around 1836. His early work reflected the style of Fonclause, while he later incorporated influences from Pajeot, featuring a metal facing at the tip of the frog.
Richard Guiton specialized in Stradivari and Guarneri models. His workmanship was effective.
August Guth studied under Bausch in Leipzig and Grimm in Berlin. He worked briefly in Breslau before establishing himself in Antwerp in 1888.
Carl Friedrich Gütter was the son of Georg Adam. He produced decent work based on the typical German models.
Fritz Gütter was a bow maker active from 1919. He produced fine quality work and was a pupil of Pfretzschner, having worked for Nürnberger.

Johann Georg Gütter was the son of Georg Adam Gütter II. He worked briefly in Erfurt around 1799 and was recognized as one of the better makers in his family, known for professional work that utilized good materials and featured yellow or dark golden-brown varnish.
Johann Gottlob Gütter produced a Stainer model. The work was decent, featuring slightly dull brown varnish.

Julius Gütter worked in Leipzig for Franz Glass and in Aachen for Holm Viertel before he emigrated to Philadelphia in 1893. There, he collaborated with Arnold Voigt and later established himself independently from 1894 to 1950.
Kurt Arno Gütter studied under O. B.
Oskar Richard Gütter apprenticed to A. Zeitler and worked for Heckel in Dresden, Gärtner in Stuttgart, R.
George H. Hadley was active in Fairfield from 1887.
Frederick Ewald Haenel trained in Saxony, Germany, and began working in 1918. He emigrated to Winnipeg, Canada, around 1938, later moving to Toronto, then Buffalo, New York.
Joseph Anton Haff moved from Augsburg to Nuremberg around 1840. He produced good work on a flat-arched model using fine materials and a brown varnish made from a combination of spirit and oil.

Joseph Anton Haff established himself in Munich in 1894, later moving to Senftenau, Bodensee, and finally Stuttgart, where he worked for Fridolin Hamma. He focused on classical models, particularly those of Amati, producing accurate and finely-finished work.
Jacques Wolfgang Hakkert was the son of Philipp Hakkert and trained in Mirecourt with Didier. He worked in Düsseldorf for L Otto and in Cologne for J.
Roscoe G. Hall utilized imported pre-carved necks and local materials.
R. M.
Anders Enfried Halvarson was a violin and bow maker. He emigrated to the USA in 1924 to work for William Lewis & Son in Chicago.
Alban Hamm created good commercial instruments based on Cremonese and Neapolitan models.
Christian Gottfried Hamm was the son and pupil of Johann Gottfried Hamm . He crafted violins in Stadr Neukirchen, near Adorf in Voigtland.
Johann Gottfried Hamm became a Master of the Violin Makers' Guild in 1764. He was regarded as the best maker of the family.
Walter Hamma was the son and successor of Fridolin Hamma (I). He trained at Mittenwald under Aschauer from 1933 to 1935 and worked for Ferdinand Jaura in Munich and Caressa & Français in Paris.
Thomas Hammett used fine materials and varnish in his Stradivari model. The initials were engraved on the interior of the back.

Johann Christian Hammig was the son and pupil of Johann Georg Hammig and was admitted to the Violin Makers' Guild in 1751. His work featured well-executed Tyrolean patterns and an extravagant soundhole design derived from Stainer, with the upper and lower wings extending across the table, bringing the lower finial very close to the corner.

Wilhelm Hermann Hammig was a violin and bow maker, the son and pupil of Wilhelm August Hammig. From 1850, he worked for Carl Grimm in Berlin, later moving to Amsterdam before returning to Markneukirchen in 1864.
John Hammond was a self-taught amateur maker and a farmer in West Burton, Wensleydale. He was credited with creating 20 violins, two violas, and one cello.
Henry Handley was an amateur violinist and an acquaintance of Sir Edward Elgar. He was a self-taught maker, credited with crafting 106 violins, six violas, and two cellos.
J. Rummelhof Hansen worked for Möckel in Berlin.
Grandson and pupil of Peter Hardie. Continued working as a schoolteacher until 1863, when he established his shop at 14 Marshall Street, Edinburgh.

John Hardie was not known to be related to any other members of the Hardies. His work was unrefined, sometimes featuring painted purfling, hollowed cheeks on the pegbox, and a thin orange-brown varnish.

The circumstances of Matthew Hardie's training are unknown. While some experts have suggested parallels between his work and that of Vincenzo Panormo and John Betts , it is entirely possible that Hardie learned his craft locally in Edinburgh.

Thomas Hardie worked independently from 1826 at 15 Shakespeare Square, Edinburgh, and from 1837 at 79 High Street, with various other addresses following. He faced challenges in maintaining a steady existence, much like his father Matthew , and ultimately died after falling down some stairs near his last residence at 5 Advocate's ...
Ronald Harding began making bows for the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in 1949.
John Edward Hardwick was a pupil of George Wulme-Hudson . He created a Stradivari model using oil varnish.

John Hare apprenticed to Rebecca Miller, the widow of George Miller, in the Draper's Company. He established himself independently in 1694 at the Fleece Tavern, located near the Royal Exchange, Cornhill, and moved to Freeman's Yard the following year.

Born in Leeds, England in 1948, Roger Hargrave initially studied fine arts and taught painting, he then transitioned to instrument making in the 1970s, training at the Newark school before joining the prestigious workshop of W. E.
Harmand was part of a family of luthiers in Mirecourt, France. Their work generally involved trade, and they were individually indistinguishable.
Louis Harmand created decent work with golden orange varnish. It was branded internally as 'L.

Nicolas Harmand was a master in the Violin Makers' Guild in 1772 and is recognized as the finest maker of the Harmand family, active in the second half of the 18th century. He was known for his small model violins, the use of fine materials, and a distinctive good red-yellow varnish.
Nicolas Joseph Harmand apprenticed in Mirecourt as an organ builder and bow maker, being the son of Louis. His work was fine but rare, and he seemed to have ceased production altogether around 1845.
Albert Harper created a Stradivari model using local materials and his own hard golden-brown varnish.
Arthur Harrass was the son and pupil of Adolf Harras. He worked for Held in Cologne and Meiter in Brunswick.

Charles Harris was born the son of a violin maker, with whom he trained. Following a fortuitous inheritance, he ascended to the aristocracy at the age of 37 and left violin making behind for more glamorous pursuits until he squandered his fortune in the mid 1830s.
Gordon Harris completed 31 violins, 33 violas, four cellos, and numerous historical instruments. He was self-taught.

John Edward Harris established himself independently in Shildon, Bishop Auckland from 1894, after being a pupil of George Chanot in London, and moved to Gateshead in 1908. He was assisted by his two sons, Ernest and John Edward Jr.
Active since 1937, he was a pupil of Edward Ikenburger. He established himself in Berkley in 1966, with assistance from his wife, Marion Fox Harrison .
Marion Fox Harrison worked with her husband Benjamin Harrison from 1966. The first violin was made in 1972, and about 20 were completed.
Homer A. Harvey was an amateur maker active in Batavia from around 1916.
Stephan (István) Havas was a fine maker of violins and bows. He studied under Brückner.
The earliest work was dated 1755. Meinrad Franck succeeded him in business and continued to use his label, with manuscript amendments, for some time.
Simon Jan Havelka was presumed to be the brother of Johann Baptist Havelka . His work featured a similar finely-executed style with a thin textured dark red-brown varnish.

Hawkes and Son were a highly successful British company active from 1865. The firm published sheet music and sold instruments, some of which were made in their workshop in Edgware, North London. Like most big retailers, they imported instruments from a wide range of European workshops and sold them at different grades: these included extremely fine instruments and bows. Hawkes and Sons merged with Boosey & Company to become Boosey & Hawkes in 1930.
George Haxton was a self-taught maker active from 1907. He established himself in Glasgow in 1910.
Jacob Haynes created typical London trade work on a Stainer model, featuring an exaggeratedly full arch and a greenish-brown varnish.
Second son and pupil of Heinrich Th. Heberlein Jr.
Son of Johann Gottlob and his pupil from 1820–23. He taught his own son, the famous Heinrich Thomas Heberlein Jr.
He is in fact one and the same person as the one called Jr.

Heinrich Theodore Jr is the most famous member of the Heberlein family and one of the best Markneukirchen makers of his era. He studied with his father, Carl August Heberlein, then moved to Leipzig to work for Ludwig Bausch and finally became the pupil of August Riechers in Hanover.
First known maker of the Heberlein family, Johann Gottlob was a musician as well as violin maker. His second son Carl August studied with him.

August Heck created personal and Stradivari models of violins, characterized by a low hollow arch and fine workmanship. He worked in Chicago around 1887 and applied a good orange-red oil varnish to his instruments.
Karl Willy Heckel apprenticed to Heinrich Todt from 1912 to 1915. He worked as Todt's assistant until 1923, after which he moved to Kraslice (Graslitz) to work for A.
Rudolf Heckel studied under Ernst Gläsel. He worked for Reichers in Berlin before establishing himself independently in Dresden in 1884.
Edward Heesom worked on a typical London trade piece based on an exaggerated Stainer model. It was branded below the button.
Luther D. Heiges completed 200 instruments using his own orange-colored oil varnish.
B. A.
Marton Hein studied under Schunda in Budapest. He worked in Vienna alongside Haudek and Gutermann.
Karl Paul Heinel was a pupil of A. L.
Oskar Bernhard Heinel worked as a pupil of Hans Jaeger . He was employed by C.
Oskar Erich Heinel was the son and pupil of Oskar Bernhard Heinel . He created fine copies of classical Italian works and also made historical instruments, viols, viola d'amores, and more.
Mathias Heinicke studied in Markneukirchen and Italy. He was active in Budapest and Berlin.
Nicolas Heinz studied under Frederick Konig from 1906 to 1911. He worked in Philadelphia for Julius Guetter and in various workshops in Chicago, including Lyon & Healy.

Pierre Jean Henri Hel was a violin and bow maker, the son, pupil, and successor of the French maker Joseph Hel of Lille. His work gained international recognition when Georges Enesco played one of his violins during a US tour in 1924.
Pierre Joseph Hel apprenticed to Salzard at Mirecourt for seven years before working for Sebastien Vuillaume and N. Darche at Aix-la-Chapelle in 1864.
Johann Joseph Held was a flautist and violinist in Euskirchen in 1861. He was a friend of Ole Bull and an amateur violin maker.
Carl Joseph Hellmer was the son of Johann Georg Hellmer . He was a pupil and assistant of Ulrich Eberle.
Johann Georg Hellmer was a pupil of Thomas Edlinger. He exhibited fine workmanship on a model derived from Amati and Klotz, featuring a diminutive scroll.
Petter Hellstedt was likely a pupil of Olof Arling and succeeded to his business. The earliest label was dated 1736.
Julius Hempel worked for Möckel in Berlin in 1897. He established himself in Hamburg from 1902, producing a small output.
Arno Erwin Hendel was active in Budapest, Breslau, and Vienna. He established himself in Markneukirchen in 1921.
John C. Hendershot established his work in Cleveland around 1893.
Frank Vernon Henderson was a violin and bow maker since his professional retirement in 1978. He studied under George Orth and authored How to Make a Violin Bow (Seattle, 1977).
Gustav Henning established his work initially in Miami, Florida, then in Denver, Colorado, from 1920, and finally in Seattle from 1928. He returned to Sweden upon retirement.
T. Henri created bows characterized by typical French style and craftsmanship.
Charles Henry was the son, pupil, and successor of Jean Baptiste Henry. He was a prolific maker of violins, violas, and cellos in various patterns.
Dominique (Henry JV) Henry was the brother of bow makers Jean Antoine and Etienne. He apprenticed in Mirecourt and established himself independently there around 1810, ceasing production around 1851.
Eugène Henry was the son, pupil, and successor of Charles Henry . He produced fine copies of Lupot featuring rich orange-red varnish.

Like many of his contemporaries who passed through the major workshops of Paris, Joseph Henry was born and received his early training in Mirecourt. He moved to Paris in 1837, at the age of 14, to work for Georges Chanot , where he produced workshop bows with the generic Chanot stamp.
Johann Baptist Herbig made an Amatise model that featured fine workmanship and was finished with golden-brown varnish.
Charles Edward Herbrig was a violin and bow maker. He was active in Montgomery from 1908 and established his work in St.
Frantisek Herclik was a wheelwright and violinist. He later trained as a violin maker, using Cremonese models in the Bohemian style.
Josef Bohumil Herclik was the son and pupil of Frantisek Herclík. He trained in Luby (Schönbach) and used Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Ladislas Herclik was the son and pupil of Frantisek Herclík. He also worked for Züst in Zurich and established himself in Kolin, Bohemia.
Edwin Lothar Hermann was a bow maker known for fine bows based on personal models. His brand was L HERMANN (with three stars).
John G. Herrick was active in New York, moved to Boston in 1900, and finally established himself in Terre Haute, Indiana, in 1914.
August Friedrich Herrmann was a bow maker in the Knopf style, active from 1900.
Emil Herrmann was a world-renowned authority on fine instruments, dealer, and connoisseur. He employed many distinguished craftsmen in his New York shop, including Simone Sacconi and Dario D'Attili .
Karl Herrmann was a pupil of Max Dölling. He also worked in Erlbach and Wernitzgriin.
Lothar Edwin Herrmann was a bow maker. He was the brother of August Herrmann.
Walter Herrmann was a bow maker and the son of Karl Hermann. Bows were branded with the name W.
Erwin Hertel was a student at Mittenwald starting in 1928 and moved to the USA in 1933 to work in Philadelphia. Later, he was employed by Emil Herrmann in New York alongside Simone Sacconi.
Franz Herzlieb created fine accurate copies of Stradivari and Guarneri using appropriate materials and shaded varnish. He received a gold medal at the London Exhibition in 1851.
Thomas Earle Hesketh studied under G. A.
Harrison Harvey Heskett was born in Londonderry, OH. He was active in Columbus, Ohio, and began working in Minneapolis in 1887.
Dutch maker, based in Cremona since 1975. Studied with Scrollavezza and Sgarabotto.
Georges Heynberg was the son and assistant of Émile Heynberg. He used models from Stradivari, Guarneri, and Gagliano.
George Herbert Hicks worked for G. A.
László Hidy graduated from the Hungarian State violin making school under G. Varady.
Successor of Apparut. Whose workshop he took over
Albert Edgar Phillips Hill was the stepson of Arthur Frederick Hill. He apprenticed in Mirecourt and worked for Fiorini in Munich for two or three years around 1904.

One of the finest craftsmen of the Hill family, Henry Lockey (also known as Lockey) was the son of the first Lockey Hill . It is possible that he trained under his uncle Joseph Hill II, and it is certain that he was working for John Betts by 1806.

Born in 1715, Joseph Hill was the first of the great Hill family of makers, restorers and experts.His earliest known label dates from 1753 and states his address as "High Holborn" in London, but it is possible that he arrived in the city from Alvechurch as early as 1746. By 1761 his workshop had relocated to Pall Mall, and in 1765 he made one final move to Haymarket, where he remained until 1780, when ... his oldest son Joseph II took over the shop.
Joseph Hill was the eldest son and successor of Joseph Hill (I). He took over the Haymarket shop in 1780, which was destroyed by fire in 1789.
Joseph Hill's fifth and most distinguished violin-making son Lockey Hill was born in 1756. He produced a great many trade instruments, often for the firms of Longman & Co. or Longman & Broderip.

William Ebsworth Hill began working in his father Henry Lockey's>/link_maker> (or, Lockey) shop around 1831 and was later apprenticed to Charles Harris after his father's death in 1835. The earliest origins of W.E.
The London-based firm W.E. Hill & Sons dominated the fine instrument and bow market in the late 19th – early 20th centuries.
Maximilian Vitalis Himmer studied the violin, but an accident hindered a career as a concert player. He began making instruments in Berlin around 1900 and established himself in Wilmersdorf.
John Wesley Hinchcliffe was a pupil of J. B.
Son of Johannes Hjorth and trained in the family firm. From 1863–65 he studied with Gabriel Lemböck in Vienna and Bernardel Père in Paris and his instruments show stylistic elements from both cities.
Knud Hjorth was the second son of Emil Theodor Hjorth and the brother of Otto Hjorth. He joined the family workshop with Otto in 1905 and worked alongside him from 1906.
Son of Emil Theodor and brother of Knud. The brothers joined the family workshop in 1905 and ran it together after Emil's retirement in 1906.

Emil Hjorth & Sons was founded by Emil Theodor Hjorth, who was the son, pupil, and successor of Johannes Hjorth. He worked for Lembock in Vienna in 1863, for Bernadel in Paris in 1864, and spent two years in London.
Ludwig Höfer studied under Baader in Mittenwald. He utilized Cremonese and Tyrolean models.
After graduating from the violin making school in Mittenwald, Andrea came to New York City where she was employed in the workshop of Jacques Francais Rare Violins, under the tutelage of Rene Morel.
Ignaz Hoffmann was a maker of violins and harps in Wölffelsdorff in the year 1776.
Anton Hofmann apprenticed to J. Martin Stoss in Vienna and succeeded to his business in 1838.
G. William Hofmann created Cremonese models.
Johann Martin Hofmann created a flat personal model that featured red-brown varnish and fine materials. He was a Hof-Laut- und Geigenmacher in Hochfürstl, located in Schillingsfürst, in 1805.
The earliest of the Hofmans family makers whose works survive today. He was probably assisted by his son, Matthias Hofmans IV.

Labels have been reported as early as 1619 and as late as 1740, leading to speculation that there might be two makers with the same name, possibly a father and son. Several instruments, including violins, pochettes, violas, and a theorbo dated 1619, are found in the Musical Instruments Museum in Brussels.
Karl Hofner was active from 1888 and removed to Bubenreuth after the Second World War around 1945. He established the business with his brothers Joseph and Walter.
Clifford A. Hoing trained as a wood carver and worked with Eric Gill before he began making violins in 1935.
Ernest Leopold Holder was the son and assistant of Thomas Jacques Holder and the brother of Thomas James. He was active in his father's workshop from approximately 1892 to 1914.

Thomas Jacques Holder established himself in Wardour Street in 1892, moved to Blackheath in 1898, and then to Leyland Road, Lee in 1912. He created clever and deceptive copies of various classical and minor Italian makers.

Thomas James Holder, son and pupil of Thomas Jacques Holder , was born in Cardiff and established his workshop at 16 place du Havre, Paris in 1913. He gained respect as a copyist and restorer, particularly known for his ingenious replicas of Italian instruments, especially those by Gagliano and Guadagnini.
Joseph Hollmayr was the son and pupil of Balthasar Hollmayr. He was active in Ingolstadt and began working in Neuberg in 1773.
Heinrich Holste was a violinist and wood carver. He was a self-taught violin maker who created over 600 violins, cellos, and basses.
Carl C. Holzapfel emigrated to the USA in 1892.
Emanuel Adam Homolka was a pupil of Strnad. He was active from 1821 and created a personal model featuring golden-brown varnish.

Ferdinand August Vincenz Homolka was the son and pupil of Emmanuel Adam Homolka. He became known as "the Prague Stradivarius" due to a particularly successful reproduction of a 1719 Stradivari violin.
Ferdinand Joseph Homolka was a pupil of his brother Emmanuel Adam Homolka. He worked in Stary Bydzov (Neu Bydschow), Czech Republic, starting in 1837, and from 1843 at Kutná Hora.
P. A.
British maker.

Carl Friedrich Hopf was active from 1862 in Brunndobra. He was the first maker of the 'Hopfgeigen' or Hopf model for the violin, which was subsequently utilized by several generations of his family and many commercial manufacturers.

Christian August Hopf produced various grades of work, including some unpurfled for trade sale and better instruments featuring purfling and superior wood. He utilized a very low flat arch with a broad edge channel.

Jacob Horil's work was inconsistent, though it sometimes exhibited a very accurate and precise finish. He employed a Tyrolese style characterized by a high squared-off arch and used a pale yellow or orange varnish.
Karl Adam Hörlein studied under Joseph Vauchel. He spent three years in Vienna with Anton Hoffmann and Gabriel Lembock.
Franz Hornsteiner created a Klotz model. The varnish was yellow-brown.
Franz Georg Hornsteiner was from Mittenwald an der Iser and was active in 1795.
Son and assistant of Johann Hornsteiner II and trained at the Mittenwald violin making school. Initially made zithers but made some violins later in his career.
Joseph Hornsteiner produced coarse and unequal work finished with meagre yellow-brown varnish. They were useful for cellos and double basses.
Joseph Hornsteiner was a pupil of Joseph Hornsteiner (I). He improved his workmanship, although the model remained similar.
Martin Hornsteiner used good materials and applied an orange-brown varnish of fine texture. Several small violas were created.
Mathias Hornsteiner was known as Tax'. He was the most distinguished maker of the family, influenced by Cremonese modeling and featuring Tyrolean archings of medium height.

Mathias Hornsteiner added the title `Gratzs' to his labels, likely to differentiate himself from Mathias Tax'. His work featured high arches and was characteristic of typical Mittenwald craftsmanship of high quality.
Joseph Horvath was a prolific amateur maker who specialized in violas based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. He used his own amber varnish.
Rudolph Höss was a lute and viol maker for the Bavarian Court. He was also credited with violins and violas based on the Stainer model.
Gabriel Houfflack was a pupil of Joseph Chardon. He established himself as an expert and dealer in Paris from 1910.
Keith W. Hovis was a bow maker.
Arthur W. Howe was a self-taught maker, receiving some assistance from Sewell Boyce starting in 1897.
T. Howell made a cornerless patent violin.
Andreas Hoyer produced poor Stainer copies, with most features exaggerated.
Friedrich Hoyer created undistinguished Tyrolean-style work with golden-brown varnish in Nürnberg in 1787. He also worked in Klingenthal in 1817.
Johann Christoph Hoyer was a violin maker known for his decent craftsmanship in the Tyrolese style. He worked in Klingenthal, where he became Principal of the Violin Makers' Guild in 1792.
Johann Friedrich Hoyer . Label printed in red:.
Otto A. Hoyer trained with E.
Edward Hoyle created personal models that featured some eccentric ideas. They generally utilized dark red oil varnish.
Johann Georg Huber was from a Füssen family of instrument makers. He sometimes utilized wood from old viols and employed neat workmanship.
Julius A. Hubicka was a pupil of F.
George Hudson worked from 'Cremona House' in Skegness as a self-taught maker. He completed about 100 instruments, using amber oil varnish in various shades of yellow and red.

William John Huggler established his work in 1959 and was advised by Simone Sacconi. He created six violins and 14 violas based on Stradivari and Guarneri models, using his own orange-brown oil varnish.
Thomas Andreas Hulinzky. He was a pupil of Eberle and created a high-arched personal Amatise model featuring small soundholes.
John Justus Hull was a barber and a self-taught maker. He created models inspired by Stradivari, Guarneri, and his own designs.

Andrew (Alexander) Hume was a self-taught musician and violin maker who was active in Dumfries, then in Peterborough, around 1915-1916, and finally at 34 Great Portland Street, London. He almost certainly relied on factory-made instruments that he imported, which he then adjusted and labelled.
Albin Hums was a pupil of E. Knorr and H.
Christoph Friedrich Hunger was the son of Samuel Hunger and a pupil of A. B.

Charles Hunnicut was a farmer and self-taught violin maker, receiving some guidance from David Struble in Washington. He made approximately 60 violins using the Stradivari model, utilizing local materials and imported commercial oil varnish that was golden- or orange-red in color.
Richard Husband created untutored work on a Stainer model. The instruments included violins and a small viola, all featuring thin yellow varnish.
Charles Claude Husson worked for Vigneron, Lamy, and others as a bow maker. His brand was CH.
Charles Claude Husson was the son of Charles Claude Husson (I). He worked for J.

Claude Charles Nicolas Husson was a bow maker of the Peccatte school who later developed a lighter Vuillaume style. He served as an important teacher to many Parisian archetiers, including Vigneron and Lamy.
Mars Auguste Husson was the son of Jean Baptiste Husson. He was a bow maker who worked for Vigneron.
Pierre Husson was a bow maker and the brother of Claude Charles Husson. Known as 'Husson-Dufour', he incorporated his wife Francoise's maiden name into his brand.

Théodore Husson was a pupil of Mougenot who collaborated with Bonnel in Rennes in 1887, taking over the Emile Bonnel workshop. He produced 100 violins and 10 cellos, primarily for Bonnel, who labelled them.
Auguste Husson Mariet, son and pupil of Pierre Husson, was a bow maker known for his work with J. B.
Carleen Maley Hutchins was an academic scientist and violin maker. Initially self-taught, she later received guidance from Karl Berger and Simone Sacconi starting in 1947.
Andrew Hyde was a musician and mechanic. He was an active violin and bow maker in Northampton from 1886.
Contemporary maker.
The violin model was a Guarneri del Gesù from 1742, featuring dark brown oil varnish.
Vincenzo Iorio was a prolific maker. His work represented the typical style of the modern Neapolitan school.
Contemporary maker.
Edward Poppleweil J'Anson was a pupil of William Booth. He was a skilled copyist of classical Italian work, and much of his output appeared under false labels.
Cyril William Jacklin was a pupil of Frank Howard in Nottingham before joining Frances Ciesla in business in 1921. He established himself independently in Robert Street, London, from 1927.

One of the best and most important 17th century Dutch makers, Hendrik Jacobs was rumored to have spent time in Italy, but it is more likely that his knowledge of the Italian tradition came from instruments circulating in the Netherlands during this period. His early work, which dates from roughly 1650 to 1670, follows Amati very ... closely, with slightly wide f-holes.
Rene Jacquemin studied under Audinot. He worked for Mougenot-Jacquet-Gand and Caressa in Paris.
Gabriel Xavier Jacquet specialized in double basses and was assisted by his son Moise-Gabriel. He was the son of Joseph Xavier Jacquet.
Charles Jacquot apprenticed to Nicholas ainé in 1819 and later worked with Breton. He established himself independently in Nancy from 1823.
Fernand Jacquot was the son and successor of Etienne. He apprenticed with Mougenot-Gauche in Mirecourt before working with his father in Nancy.
Pierre Charles Jacquot, son of Charles Jacquot and nephew of J.B. Vuillaume, was a leading French maker of the 19th century.
Hans Jaeger was a pupil of Glaesel. He worked in Regensburg and Amsterdam.

Andreas Jais was a pupil of Matthias Klotz, active in Tölz from 1711. He crafted instruments featuring a highly arched Stainer model, demonstrating fine workmanship with details such as ornately carved heads and, occasionally, painted purfling.

Anton Jais, son of Franz Jais, was active from 1769 and is noted for his use of a high-arched Stainer model. He demonstrated fine workmanship and materials, with varnish characterized by a good yellow-brown and dark red-brown.
Johann Jais, son of Franz Jais, worked in Bozen (Bolzano) and Tolz. He was a pupil of Matthias Kloz and created an Albani-like model using fine materials and a deep red-brown varnish.
Contemporary maker.
William James was a self-taught maker who created Stradivari and Guarneri violin models, as well as a Tertis viola model. He utilized terpenes resin varnish and local materials.
Gabriel Jamis was a pupil of G. Apparut and later became associated with Gaston Meyer.
Ferdinand Wilhelm Jaura apprenticed to his uncle Wilhelm Jaura in 1905. He established himself independently in Vienna from 1917 and in Munich from 1940.
Wilhelm Thomas Theodor Jaura was the eldest son of Wilhelm Josef Jaura, a guitar maker. He worked for W.
Henry Jay (Jaye) had no known connection with the earlier Henry Jaye. He was probably a pupil of Peter Wamsley.
F. S.

Pierre Napoléon Jeandel was a pupil of Charotte in Mirecourt starting in 1830 and moved to Rouen in 1835 to work with Joseph Charotte . He succeeded to Charotte's business in 1848 in partnership with Lucien Delau.
Contemporary maker. Won first prize 2001 at the Triennale competition in Cremona.

Shan Jiang started making violins in 1990, and received his master degree in violin making from the Central Conservatory of Music. He currently works in NYC and has won several medals and awards in VSA Competitions.
Anton Jirowsky worked for Thomas Zach from around 1887 and collaborated with Ignaz Lutz and Haudek from approximately 1896. He established himself independently in 1902.
Contemporary maker.
William Grieve Johnston apprenticed at the London piano firm of Broadwood before beginning work at the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons building the company's inlaid and veneered instrument cases.
Franz Jühling was associated with Richler until 1884. He manufactured very good commercial work in large numbers.
Curt Jung was a pupil of Adolf Hammig. He worked as an employee and successor of Otto Möckel in Berlin from 1925 to 1937.
Jan (John) Juzek. Guarneri model, fine workmanship.
Albin Kämpffe was a pupil of Kessler. He created copies of Italian, Tyrolean, and French works.
Contemporary prize-winning bow maker based in Port Townsend, USA.

Born in 1923 in the small Slovenian town of Okraj Ljutomer, Joseph Kantuscher enrolled at the Violin Making School in Mittenwald in December 1941. He worked for the Reiter Violin Shop for a decade, before opening his own shop in 1955.
Ladislav Kaplan emigrated to New York in 1893 and made his first violin in 1895. He was in business as Muller & Kaplan in 1922.
Bartholomaus Karner produced Klotz and Stradivari models. The workmanship was good, featuring thin opaque varnish.
Georg Karner was a pupil of Georg Klotz. He was in Mittenwald / an der Iser in 1795.
Antoni Karolkowski was a pupil of Rymwid-Mickiewicz and F. K.
Born in Dluzec, Poland. Studied violin performance under Kochanski and Jarzebski in Poland.
Albert H. Karr taught violin making by Silas Benjamin and John Hendershot starting in 1910.
Henryk Kaston was a bow maker from 1943. He was also a professional violinist and jeweller.
A. W.
Apprenticed at Thibouville-Lamy. Worked for Silvestre and Maucotel in Paris 1900-1905.
Joseph Kaye made over 150 violins using Guarneri and personal models, featuring yellow to red-brown oil varnish.
Edward Keenan made instruments based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. The scrolls were outlined in black, and they featured a yellow-brown varnish.

Helmuth Keller was the son-in-law of Martin Nebel, a violin maker in Philadelphia. Keller came to Philadelphia from Germany after WW2 to work for William Moennig & Son, and opened his own shop in the 1960s.
Philippe Keller created Stradivari models and personal models designed based on the principles of Max Grossman. The varnishes were golden-yellow or golden-red.
Andreas Kempter's name appeared in the records of Füssen, where he was likely apprenticed. He was active in Dillingen starting in 1725.
Alexander Kennedy was a pupil of Nathaniel Cross and taught his nephew John Kennedy . He worked in Market Street, off Oxford Street (now Great Titchfield Street), London.
Alexander Kennedy created a personal model with yellow varnish. He was based in Wallsend, 19..
Nephew of Alexander Kennedy . Active in Cooper's Gardens, Houghton Street, Clare Market, St Clement's Lane, Long Alley, and from 1778 in Johnson's Court.

Thomas Kennedy was the oldest son of lesser British violin maker John Kennedy , from whom he probably learned the basics of his craft before being apprenticed to Thomas Powell in 1795. He worked briefly for William Forster III before setting up his own ... workshop in London's West End, making instruments and bows.
Franz Xaver Kerschensteiner was a pupil of Peter Schulz. He exhibited very fine workmanship and finish.

Anton Kessel worked commercially, primarily on the Guarneri model, which was retailed in England by Foucher and later by Haynes and Co. His workmanship was good, featuring a decent golden-brown varnish.

Trained at the Swiss Violin Making School at Brienz under Adolf Konig. Worked for Arnold Dolmetsch Ltd. in Haslemere, UK, later moving to Albert Arnold Ltd. in Charing Cross Road with Cyril Jacklin until that business closed in 1955.
Ernst Kessler was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of August Voigt and Ludwig Otto .
Heinrich Kessler studied under Hammig in Dresden and later with Johann Padewet in Karlsruhe. He established himself independently in Mannheim from 1880.
Johann Georg Kessler was a Master in the Violin Makers' Guild from 1756. He used a Stainer model characterized by a high arch and often included carved heads.
Paul Kessler worked with his brother Ernst. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen in 1908.
Wilhelm Albin Kessler worked for Wenzel Lenk and Louis Noebe in Frankfurt-am-Main. He established himself independently there in 1897.
Wilhelm August Kessler was the son and pupil of Heinrich Kessler . He established himself independently in Mannheim in 1882.
Dominik Kief created a small model with a medium arching, diminished scroll, and dark varnish. The work was generally meagre and inartistic.
Hermann Kilian was the principal cellist of the Munich orchestra. He studied under Fiorini in violin making around 1906.
Contemporary violin maker and violinist. He studied violin making under Harry Reeve in Batesville, AR, from 1973.
Johann Christian Kinpolth demonstrated fine workmanship on the Amati model. He used well-chosen materials and applied a good orange-brown varnish.
One of the more mysterious figures in the history of violin and bow making is Nikolai (Nicolaus) Kittel, who was of Austrian descent but born in St. Petersburg around 1805 and died in 1868.

Son and student of Nikolai Kittel Sr.; also studied briefly with Honore Derazey in Mirecourt around 1859–60. Continued the family business in St Petersburg after his father's death in 1868, finally selling it in 1873.
Aloys Klein founded A. Klein et Cie in 1884, producing high-quality instruments on a commercial scale, with the workshop managed by Antoine Brubach .

Cornelius Kleynman was the step-son and probable pupil of cittern maker Franciscus Lupo. He was a contemporary of Hendrick Jacobs and an equally sophisticated early follower of Amati, although his surviving work was rare.
Franz Josef Klier was the son of Joseph. He apprenticed with R.
Otto Klier established in Bratislava (Pressburg) Slovakia in 1935. He moved to Neunkirchen Germany in 1966.
Joseph Klimits was a Geigen und Guitarmacher in Wien No. 50.
Albert W. Klinth, who worked as a publisher, studied violin making on the west coast of the USA, possibly in Seattle.
Aegidius Klotz was the son of Sebastian Klotz. He was regarded as one of the finest among this large family of Mittenwald makers.

Aegidius Sebastian Klotz is one of the best makers in the family tradition that founded the Mittenwald school of violin making. As with many other members of the family, Sebastian hewed closely to an Amati model, but in keeping with his father Sebastian I's ... work, Aegidius Sebastian's instruments have flatter arches than the usual Stainer model.
Ferdinand Klotz was the son of Johann Karl Klotz and produced typical yet undistinguished work in the family style. His location was Mittenwald an der Iser 17.

Mittenwald violin maker George Karl Klotz is sometimes designated a descendant of Matthias I , but expert opinion is divided on the question, and he may not have been part of that branch of the famous family. Like other makers of the Mittenwald school, his work shows a preference for Amati ... models.

Johann Carl Klotz was the son of Mathias Klotz (I) and is believed to have been a pupil of his half-brother Sebastian. He trained one apprentice, Phillip Sailer, from c. 1737 to 1742, as well as his sons, Wolfgang and Michael.
Joseph Klotz was the son of Joseph Thomas Klotz (I). His workmanship was typical of the Klotz family, though it lacked great distinction.

Joseph Thomas Klotz was the son of Sebastian Klotz. His work was very inconsistent, presumably created for sale in various grades, and featured the typical Klotz style characterized by low and medium arching and yellow varnish.
Son of Urban Klotz, a tailor. First maker of this extended and highly important family of violin makers.

Michael Klotz was the son of Johann Karl Klotz and is known for his rare instruments, which often bore the labels of Joseph Klotz. He created decorated work featuring ebony and ivory inlay on the outer edges and around the scroll, with some instruments having backs made of bird's eye maple.

Sebastian Klotz, son of Matthias Klotz, was a distinguished craftsman from the Mittenwald family of violin makers. He produced a violin labeled as "Haynes" and sold by Haynes & Co., London, around 1907, identified with the number 152.
Sebastian Klotz was the son of Aegidius Klotz . It was typical of Klotz's work, though undistinguished.
Matthias Kloz I and his descendants represent a continuous lineage from the 17th-century origins of the Mittenwald school to the 20th century. Georg I , Sebastian I , Aegidius ...

The lack of historical documentation surrounding the origins of Aegidius Kloz makes it unlikely that he is a descendant of Matthias I , founder of a major branch of the Mittenwald violin making family. His extant instruments date from the late 17th and early 18th centuries, and are markedly superior to those of Matthias I's ... grandson Aegidius Sebastian Kloz , who was born in 1733.

Son of Matthias I and older brother of Sebastian I , Georg Kloz I seems to have enjoyed some sort of honorable status during his career. His violins, which are well-made and on a somewhat wider model than those of his brother, possess an attractive yellowish-brown ... or reddish-brown varnish.
Frank R. Knight was an amateur maker and a pupil of W.
Knilling was a family of luthiers in Mittenwald, Germany, known for their mostly indistinguishable trade work.
Josef Knitl was one of the better makers of the Mittenwald school. The Klotz model was well made and featured fine transparent golden-brown varnish.
Family of luthiers in Markneukirchen Germany.
August Moritz Knopf studied under O. B.

Carl Heinrich Knopf was a violin and bow maker trained by his uncle Christian Friedrich . He established himself independently in Berlin in 1868 and was employed at the Lowendall factory from 1873 to 1885.

Christian Friedrich Wilhelm Knopf was a bow maker active in Dresden, known for his fine craftsmanship. He was the youngest son of Christian Wilhelm senior and trained his nephews, Johann Wilhelm and Carl ...
Christian Wilhelm Knopf was a bow maker known for his fine work. He was a senior member of the family.

Heinrich (Henry) Richard Knopf, son of Carl Heinrich , studied under his father and learned from his uncle Johann. He worked for Bausch in Dresden and Christian Adam in Berlin before emigrating to New York in 1880.
Son of Karl Wilhelm . Trained with his uncle, Christian Friedrich .
Karl Wilhelm Knopf was a bow maker, the son and pupil of Christian Knopf. He was the second son of Christian Wilhelm senior and the father of Johann Wilhelm and Carl Heinrich . ...
Albert Albrecht Knorr was the son and pupil of Albert Knorr. His work was similar to that of his father.
Paul Knorr worked in Breslau and Berlin as a pupil of M. Dolling.
William B. Knox established a business in Utica, where he made strings and instruments.
Albert Knupfer worked for A. Wunderlich in Markneukirchen and later with E.

Franz Joseph Koch devised a method of wood impregnation that he believed recreated the Cremonese method. He treated instruments known as 'Homogeneous Stringed Instruments, ' which included classical models, some featuring aged and shaded varnish.
Friedrich Kochendörfer was the son and pupil of A. F.
Ludwig Köhler studied under Pilat in Budapest. He worked in Berlin and Chicago, Mirmic, before returning to Budapest.
Samuel Kolstein was a double bass player. He was largely self-taught as a violin and bow maker.

Sándor Komáromy apprenticed to Andras Bergmann from 1903 to 1906. He subsequently worked for Otto Karl Zimmer and Stowasser in Budapest, for Bela Babas in Hermannstadt, and for Nandor Hock in Rakospalota.
First of the Konya makers of Hungary. Initially a photographer, then taught himself to make violins in the 1960s.
Contemporary Hungarian maker. One of two sons of Istvan Konya.
Born in Chicago. Worked for John Hornsteiner and the William Lewis firm in Chicago and later established own shop there.

Born in Washington, Cody Kowalski is a contemporary bow maker who studied under Charles Espey and is currently based in Germany. Kowalski won three gold medals in the 2016 Violin Society of America International Competition and two Grand Prix Awards in the 1st International Darling Bow Making Competition in 2016.
Contemporary maker.
Adolph J. Krug created a Stradivari model featuring slim soundholes and shaded oil varnish.
Lorenz Krumbholz trained in Markneukirchen. He worked for Otto Möckel in Berlin, Karel Van Der Meer in Amsterdam from 1916, and Joseph Vedral in The Hague from 1917.
John Thomas Kuczer completed 60 violins and six violas. He also made viola bows.
Johann Kulik was a highly regarded maker of refined Cremonese models. He used fine spirit varnish in appropriate shades and crafted particularly well-carved scrolls.

Born in Czechoslovakia in 1930, Joseph Kun settled in Ottawa, Canada in 1968. Best known for his design of a shoulder rest patented in 1972, he was also an accomplihsed bow-maker, winning three gold medals and one silver medal at the biennial International Violin and Bow Makers Competition in 1983.
Martin Kuntze-Fechner studied chemistry and earned a Doctor of Philosophy at Boston University (USA). He studied violin in Munich before taking up violin making.
Laurent (Lorenz) Küntzel was active in Breslau from 1815 to 1857. He subsequently established himself in Berlin, where he was appointed luthier to the court in 1858.
Wilhelm Paul Kunze worked for Kok in Amsterdam from 1892. He established himself independently in The Hague from 1902.
Frederick Kuster made 43 violins based on the Stradivari model and 39 violas modeled after the Tertis design.
Emile L'Humbert was a pupil of Derazey and Mougenot in Mirecourt. He worked for Blanchard in Lyons and for Jombart, Bernadel, and Silvestre in Paris.
Lucien L'Humbert was the son, pupil, and successor of Emile L'Humbert . He created this in 1949 at 34 Rue de Passy, Paris.

Marc André Joseph Laberte was the son of Pierre Alexis and served as the Director of Laberte-Humbert Frères, which merged with Fournier Magnié in 1920. He inherited the family firm and employed hundreds of makers, producing a wide range of instruments and bows from student to soloist level.

Founded in 1780, it became one of the largest and most significant businesses in Mirecourt. The company was known as Laberte Frères until 1925, when it merged with Fournier Magnié, which had been established in 1775 by Joseph Thiriot .
Mirecourt firm founded in 1876 by the violin making brothers Maurice-Emile and Pierre Alexis Auguste Laberte. At first it produced only factory instruments, some with fictitious labels such as 'J.
Fils Lacombe produced work in the style of Vuillaume, but it was of inferior quality.
Jacques Lafleur was a violin and bow maker. He apprenticed in Mirecourt and associated with the Adam family there.

Joseph René Lafleur was the son and successor of Jacques Lafleur . He became an active bowmaker around 1830 and was employed by Lupot from approximately 1833, working in his master's style with low plain frogs.
Antoine Lagarde was the son and pupil of Gabriel Lagarde from around 1815. He was a violin and bow maker who established himself independently from approximately 1825.
Louis Auguste Lagarenne apprenticed to Derazey. He became the son-in-law and partner of Hilaire Darche in Brussels in 1908.
Studied making in Indianapolis and Chicago. Worked in San Antonio, TX, then established own shop in Madison, WI, c. 1959.
Jean Nicolas Lambert was Master of the Luthiers' Guild from 1745 to 1746. His premises on rue Michel-le-Comte continued to operate after his death, managed by successors until 1789.
Albin Charles Lambotte was a medical doctor and a self-taught luthier. He was credited with 100 violins, violas, and cellos based on classical models.
Alfred Lamy was a bow maker. He was the son of Jean Joseph Baptiste Lamy.
Alfred Lamy was a bow maker and the son, pupil, and successor of Joseph Alfred Lamy. He branded his work as A.
Georges-Léon Lamy was the son, pupil, and assistant of Joseph Alfred ('Lamy père'). He became a casualty of the First World War.
Hippolyte Camille Lamy was a bow maker. He was the son, pupil, and successor of Joseph Alfred ('Lamy père').

Joseph Alfred Lamy did his early apprenticeship in Mirecourt with Claude Charles Nicolas Husson , where the Vuillaume - Voirin school was probably the dominant influence. After an ... eight-year period in Château-Thierry, where Lamy formed a friendship with Joseph Voirin , he moved to Paris in 1876 to work for Voirin's older brother François Nicolas and became his assistant.
Léon Jules Lamy worked for Thibouville-Lamy. He established himself in Paris in 1890 and primarily worked as a repairer.
Louis Lamy was a bow maker and a pupil of Cuniot. He produced several grades and qualities of work.

Born in Vicenza, Lanaro studied with Giuseppe Pedrazzini and Gaetano Sgarabotto and won a prize at the 1949 Cremona International Violin Making Competition. Lanaro emigrated to Argentina in 1951, then in 1955 he founded the National School of Violin Making in Mexico City at the invitation of the Mexican National Institute of Fine Arts, remaining as the school's director until 1972.
Umberto Lanaro was the brother and pupil of Luigi Lanaro and worked with him from 1945 to 1953. After spending five years in Argentina, he returned to Albignasego.

Although Carlo Ferdinando Landolfi was born in 1710, he does not seem to have taken up violin making before the middle of the 18th century. One possible impetus for this belated start in the trade is the arrival of G.B.

Pietro Antonio Landolfi studied under his father, Carlo Ferdinando , whose prolific output dominated making in Milan from the 1750s onwards. Following the deaths of the older generation makers such as Carlo Giuseppe and Carlo Antonio Testore, as well as the Grancinos, it was Carlo Ferdinando Landolfi who became the preeminent ... flag bearer of the Milanese school.
Contemporary New York-based French maker of violins and bows.
Benedikt Lang was the son of Ferdinand. He made cellos and double basses.
Franz Lange repaired it in November 1920 in Eulenberg.
Alfred Charles Langonet was the son and pupil of Charles Frank Langonet . He worked in the workshop of W.
Eugene Langonet was the youngest brother of Charles François Langonet . He apprenticed at Chipot-Vuillaume and was employed by Paul Mangenot before joining his brother in London at Hills.

Alfred Lanini was born in California and studied violin making locally before traveling to Milan in 1911 to study with Riccardo Antoniazzi . Upon his teacher's death six months later, he entered the workshop of Celeste Farrotti , where he remained until ... 1914.
Loris Lanini created 130 violins, eight violas, eight cellos, and two double basses using personal models with golden-yellow oil varnish. He worked in Pontedera in the year 19..
Ernest Francis Lant was a pupil of Harry Bidduiph. He specialized in violins, violas, cellos, double basses, and bows.
Bohuslav Lantner was the son and pupil of Ferdinand Lantner. He worked in Germany before settling in Prague in 1891.
Ferdinand Martin Lantner was a pupil of Lehner. He established himself independently in Prague in 1862.
J. A.

Bowmaker Marcel Lapierre cast his net widely among the many Mirecourt workshops active in the twentieth century. He trained with Jérôme Thibouville-Lamy from 1921 to 1923 and began working for François Lotte in 1931.

Etienne Laprevotte was appointed luthier to the Duke of Bordeaux. The quality of his work was inconsistent; some instruments were rather meagre in wood and varnish, while others featured better materials and workmanship.
Jean Larcher was a pupil of Collin Mezin. He created classical models featuring personal arching.

Christian Frederik Larsen, born in Odense on March 16th, 1846, was the son of Lars Christian Henric Larsen. He took over the family business with his brother Wilhelm (1848 - 1919) after their father's death in 1866.
Joseph Anton Laske was a pupil of J. Kolditz.
Enzo Lassi was the son and pupil of Francesco Lassi . He also studied briefly with Gaetano Pollastri.
Francesco Lassi was a self-taught maker, active from 1906. He completed over 400 instruments based on classical and personal models.
William Laugher was an amateur maker credited with 50 violins and 10 violas, often utilizing the Reichers system.
Robert Laumann studied under Nandor. He worked for J.
Albert Laurent was the son, pupil, and successor of Emile Laurent. He created fine copies of classical Cremonese models with golden- and red-brown varnish.
Émile Laurent was the son and pupil of Emile Laurent (I). He worked for E.
Émile Laurent apprenticed to Auguste Dane. He subsequently worked for Joseph Hel in Lille.
Jean Paul Lauxerrois was a bow maker who studied under Lotte in Mirecourt. He established himself independently in Paris in 1959 and later retired to Bordeaux.
Son of Jean-Paul Lauxerrois, he also studied with Bernard Ouchard in Mirecourt. In 1984 he established himself in Grenoble, then moved to Lyon in 1988.
Antonio Maria Lavazza died prematurely in a duel with G. B.
Carlo Antonio Santino Lavazza was the son of Pietro Antonio Lavazza. His work, a fine example of the Grancino school, exhibited some influence from Landolphi.
Jean Lavello established himself independently in Paris. He was a pupil of Pierre Audinot and served as the luthier for the Conservatoire and Colonne Orchestra.
Francesco Lazzaretti reported fine work in the Veneto style with orange-red varnish. It was little known.
Giovanni Lazzaro was known as `Rosario. ' He studied under Pietro Pettinato, a sculptor and amateur violin maker. His work included Stradivari and personal models featuring dark golden varnish.
Guillaume Le Blond worked in London after Dunkirk, according to an inscription on a piano in the Berlin Music Museum. He plucked instruments in the Brussels and Berlin Museums.
J. C.
Charles Le Lyonnais apprenticed with E. Audinot in Mirecourt.
Rome-based contemporary maker. Originally from Switzerland; studied in Cremona.
Antonio Lecchi probably worked at Cavalli's `Monteverdi' workshop. He established himself independently around 1920.

Giuseppe Lecchi was one of many Italian violin makers descended from a family of woodworkers. After serving in WWI and returning to cabinet-making, Lecchi became an apprentice of Cesare Candi in 1923.
Antonio Lechi created a large personal model featuring flat arching. The workmanship was decent, and the varnish displayed various tints.
Jacques Leclerc was the name used in violins made by Laberte-Magnié. Instruments d'Artistes were associated with him in the year..

Joseph Nicolas LeClerc worked in the `Quinze-Vingt' Hospital. One violin, dated 1770 and characterized as mediocre with yellow varnish, was part of the Snoeck collection and is now housed in the Berlin Museum of Music.
Born near San Francisco. Worked for Bein & Fushi.
Percy Lee was a viola player and a maker of violins, violas, cellos, and viols, credited with approximately 50 instruments.

Korean-born violinist who studied violin making in Mittenwald and moved to the USA in 1970, working for the Kenneth Warren firm. Co-founder of the Chicago School of Violin Making (originally the Kenneth Warren and Son School) and since 2003 its Director-Emeritus.

Andreas Carl Leeb was the first maker in Vienna to adopt the Stradivari model, preceding Geissenhof. Initially, he used dark varnish that later turned black; during his Viennese period, this varnish gradually became lighter, achieving a fine clear golden-brown color.
Together with Edgar Bishop , Albert Leeson produced some of the finest gold-mounted bows in the history of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons .

Jean Baptiste Lefèbvre was active in Amsterdam from around 1726. He worked in two distinct styles: one followed the traditional Dutch manner, featuring the Arnatisé model with long corners and whalebone purfling; the other was more aligned with the Mirecourt tradition, characterized by low arches and short corners, using yellow and golden-brown varnish.
Toussaint Nicolas Germain Lefèvre was a violin and bow maker. He established his workshop in the Rue de Cimetière Saint-Jean and worked at 20 rue Saint-Antoine starting in 1813.
Charles Leggatt began working at the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons shortly after Sydney Yeoman , around 1885.
Alfred Leicht was a pupil of Emil Stark. He worked in Brunswick and for Mockel in Berlin.
Rudolf Herbert Leicht was the brother and pupil of Max Leicht. He was active in Berlin and Hamburg.
Johann Christoph Leidolff was the son of Nicolaus Leidolff . He was appointed as a luthier to the Viennese court.
Work was rarely encountered. He made a large Amatisé or Stainer model that was neatly constructed.
Emile Leipp studied violin making with Carchereaux in Paris starting in 1932. He authored Cremona Varnish (Paris, 1946) and produced a small output, utilizing his own orange-brown varnish.
Alois Leja was a pupil of A. Ferenczy.
Dino Lelli created personal designs. He demonstrated good workmanship with various shades of oil varnish.
Gabriel (Gábor) Lemböck. Pupil of Peter Teufelsdorfer and J.
E. Lemoine worked in Belgium, France, and Germany before establishing himself in Liège.
Joseph Rudolf Lenhart emigrated to the USA in 1902. He created classical Italian and Stainer models in various grades, utilizing both spirit and oil varnishes.
According to Grillet, a pupil of Dominique Peccatte, although Millant sees no working connection. On military service 1848-1862 and stationed at Rennes, where he may have encountered Bonnel.
Johann Nicolaus Lentz was born in Tyrol, Austria. He was employed by Bernhard Fendt and worked in Duke Street, St James's, and Chelsea.
John Frederick Lentz was the younger brother of Johann Nicolaus Lentz . He created similar work.
Eleuterio Leonardi was a pupil of Francesco Fabbri. He was credited with 100 violins and cellos.
Giovanni Leoni created a Stradivari-derived model. The work was unremarkable and featured red-brown varnish.
Guido Leoni trained as a cabinet maker in San Benedetto from 1927 and became active as a violin maker there in 1961. He was credited with 70 violins, 15 violas, and 2 cellos.
Paolo Leonori was a cellist and luthier. He was credited with 40 violins, nine violas, eight cellos, three viols, and various other instruments.

Giovanni Francesco Leonporri was possibly of Tyrolean origin and worked in Milan, Rome, and L'Aquila during the mid-18th century. He created a small model featuring a slightly pinched arch, with inconsistent workmanship reminiscent of Testore and Landolphi.
Giuseppe Lepri was a pupil of Augusto Pollastri and was active in Sant'Arcangelo di Romagna from 1914. His instruments featured Stradivari, Pollastri, and personal models with fine golden varnish.
Joseph Dominique Lété apprenticed to Moitessier. He worked for Gand in Paris and with J.
J. C.
Edward Lewis was the presumed son and successor of Edward Lewis (I). Various addresses in London were provided, with Drury Lane being the only one that was easily decipherable on manuscript labels.

Chicago-based firm, formerly one of the largest violin houses in the USA. Founded in 1874 by William Lewis (1835-1902) with partners Newell and Gibbs, and originally named Lewis, Newell & Gibbs, before being renamed the Chicago Music Company.
John Leyden credited with approximately 50 violins. The violins were based on Cremonese models and featured well varnished finishes.
Eric Johan Lidholm studied under E. A.
Johann Gottfried Liebich was a pupil of Sebastian Rauch in Wroclaw. He established himself independently there in 1789 and also made harps.
Gottfried Liebig created a slender model featuring extended middle bouts, a high arching, and a narrow edge. The materials used were of good quality, though the varnish was an opaque brown.
Johann Liebl created a Mittenwald style instrument using quality materials and a hard yellow varnish in 1823. He was a violinemaker in Griesbach.
Remerus Liessem was a maker of citterns and guitars, as well as some violins and cellos of trade quality. His work featured a Germanic style and utilized dark golden-yellow varnish.
Albert Lind established himself in Boston after gaining experience in Europe. He created 112 violins, along with some violas and cellos, using Stradivari, 'Grand Amati', and personal models.
David Lindsay was credited with around 40 violins of some distinction, including Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Michael H. Lindsay was a musician and self-taught violin maker, active from around 1860 in Stockton-on-Tees.
Lloyd Liu was a bow maker and jeweller in New York. He established a bow making factory in Old Bennington, Vermont.
George Herbert Lock was a professor of mathematics at Shrewsbury College and an amateur luthier. He was credited with 60 violins and three cellos.
John Logan credited with 50 violins based on Cremonese models. He used well-cut scrolls and fine yellow oil varnish.
Jacob Lomax is a pawnbroker and amateur luthier. His work is decent.
Giovanni Longiaru trained as a wood carver with Gottardo in Venice and studied violin making with G. V.
Important firm of London musical instrument agents active from around 1767–1790. It was founded by James Longman, then became Longman Lukey & Co.

Company of music dealers founded by James Longman. Their brand (used on the back beneath the top button) was 'Longman & Co.' between 1767–1769, then became 'Longman Lukey & Co.', then 'Longman, Lukey & Broderip' and finally 'Longman & Broderip'.
Firm of London musical agents established by James Longman, which used the brand 'Longman Lukey & Co' between 1769 and 1775. Later became Longman and Broderip.
Paul Lorange worked for Blanchard in Lyons, Gautie in Toulouse, and Mougenot in Brussels. He established himself independently in Lyons in 1899.
Son of Paul Lorange (I). Worked in Marseilles with Diter.
Gaspar Lorenzini was probably a pupil of J. B.
Associated with B. S.

One of the more colorful characters in 19th-century British violin making, John Frederick Lott II followed in the footsteps of his father John Frederick I, who worked for Thomas Dodd , mostly building well-regarded double basses. John Frederick II began making violins and guitars for the London trade early in life before abandoning ... violin making in 1823.

Son of violin maker Georges Lotte , François Lotte probably apprenticed with Charles Nicolas Bazin in Mirecourt, where he is listed as an employee until 1921. From 1922 to 1925 he worked for Eugène ...
Georges Lotte was a violin maker who worked for J. B.

Roger François Lotte was a bow maker, the son, pupil, and assistant of Francois Lotte. He began making bows alongside his father around 1936 and initially adopted his father's elegant and traditional style, later developing a more modern and heavier approach.
Alfred J. Loughton copied instruments of Gasparo da Salò, Stradivari, and Guarneri, using red and yellow varnishes.
Jean Louvet was known as Louvet le jeune. The flat arch featured coarse workmanship and a dull yellow-brown varnish.
Giuseppe Lucci was a pupil of Pietro Borghi and Leandro Bisiach. He created Cremonese models with fine workmanship and a good golden-red varnish.
Berlin-based bow maker. Previously worked for Hieronymus Köstler in Stuttgart.
Otto Luderer was a pupil of E. Reinhold Schmidt.
William H. Luff was an important and influential post-war violin maker in England.
Jacob O. Lundh emigrated to the USA in 1882.

François Lupot was the son and pupil of Laurent Lupot and was active in Luneville around 1746-1754. In 1754, he was appointed violin maker to the Duke of Württemberg in Stuttgart, a position he held until 1768.

Born in Orleans, François Lupot II was trained as a violin maker by his father, Francois I, and was the younger brother of the renowned violin maker Nicolas Lupot. It is unclear how he learnt bow making, but his early work shows a pronounced Tourte influence and he seems to have worked for Leonard Tourte in Paris.

Among the great French violin makers, Nicolas Lupot deserves special recognition for his talents. Known as the French Stradivari , Lupot trained with his father François in Orléans before moving to Paris in around 1796 and set up his business near the ... shop of François Pique on the rue de Gramont.
Gustav Lutschg was a violinist and violin maker. He was a pupil of Methfessel and worked with Mougenot in Brussels.
Ignaz Anton Lutz was the son and pupil of Ignaz Lutz (I). Later, he worked for various workshops in Vienna, Berlin, Paris, and London before emigrating to the USA.
Louis Lyeki worked actively from 1906 for Johann Stowasser in Budapest before emigrating to the USA. He established himself in St.
Mario Maccaferri was a guitar virtuoso who worked in Sienna. He was primarily a guitar maker but received 2nd prize for a cello in Rome in 1927.

Carl Mächler trained at Mittenwald under Kochendörfer. He was employed by Winterling in Hamburg, Kreiling in Munich, Niedt in Würzburg, Held in Cologne, Otto in Dusseldorf, Kessler in Mannheim, and later with Wahl in Karlsruhe and Zust in Zurich.
Cesare Maggiali was an amateur maker active in Fossola, Carrara from 1951, later establishing himself at 52 Corso Cavour, La Spezia. He received guidance from I.

The best known maker of the Brescian school, Giovanni Paolo Maggini was born in Botticino and moved to Brescia around 1586 to train with Gasparo da Salò . After twenty years of work for da Salò, Maggini struck out on his own and began to develop his own models.
Stelio Maglia studied at the Cremona school, graduating in 1946. He exhibited in Cremona in 1949.
Gabriel Magniere cited one instrument dated 1890 and numbered 233. Another instrument, dated 1819, was cited by Vannes.
Frederick Daniel Mahoney worked for F. W.
Etienne Maire was the son and pupil of E. Maire-Breton.

One of the great bow makers of the mid 19th century, Nicolas Rémy Maire was probably apprenticed to Étienne Pajeot . He set up his own establishment in Mirecourt in 1826 but went bankrupt during the French economic crisis of the 1830s.
Erminio Malagutti was a pupil of S. Scarampella.
Johann Stephan Maldoner created coarse and unattractive work, although it featured delicate scrolls. He used plain materials and ordinary brown varnish.
François Alexandre Maline was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Jean Joseph Maline .

Guillaume Maline was the father of Nicolas Maline and a maker of violins and bows. His work featured a large Stradivari model, characterized by clean professional craftsmanship, quality materials, and a dull yellow-brown varnish.

Born in Mirecourt to family of violin- and bow makers, Nicolas Maline probably apprenticed with his father, Guillaume, and may have worked for the Pajeot workshop. In 1840 he started to make bows for J.B.
Nicolas Auguste Eugène Maline was a bow maker, the son, pupil, and assistant of Nicolas Maline. He served his apprenticeship with his father and assisted him from 1870.
Pierre Maline was a bow maker and the son of Francois Alexandre Maline. He worked for Cuniot-Hury in 1906 and for Feret-Marcotte starting in 1911.
Eugene Malteau recorded one cello by Vannes, featuring long corners and a dry yellow varnish that recalled the work of Mast.
Pietro Antonio Malvolti was possibly a pupil of Christofori. The model was derived from Stainer and exhibited the Florentine style.
Camillo Mandelli was known as 'Camillo da Calco' and was a pupil of Bisiach and Antoniazzi. He established himself in Buenos Aires from 1889 to 1920 before returning to Calco.
Francesco Manfredi was a violinist and composer. He completed 90 instruments and introduced a personal model around 1926.
Albert Michel Mangenot was the son and assistant of Paul Alexandre Mangenot . He was killed in World War I and was known for creating fine viola d'amores.

Amati Mangenot was the son and pupil of Paul Alexandre Mangenot and also trained with Laurent in Brussels. He established himself independently in Clermont-Ferrand in 1922 and moved to Bordeaux in 1925.
Apprenticed to Justin Derazey. Subsequently made cellos for Collin¬Mezin.
Pietro Mangiani described a violin featuring flat arching and golden-brown varnish. There was no other support for this maker; a fabricated label was possibly involved.
Gerard Mangin created cellos based on the Stradivari pattern and used red-brown varnish. They were branded in a triangular form: 'Gerard Mangin Mirecourt'.

Louis Mangin produced poor commercial work characterized by dull yellow-brown varnish and low-quality materials. His products were branded as `Mangin a Mirecourt', `Mangin a Paris', or 'Louis Mangin'.
Thomas Howells Mann created a violin noted by Woodcock, featuring a Stradivari model, yellow-brown varnish, and a particularly well-cut scroll. It was made in Bedford in 1909.

Pierre Mansuy established himself in Mirecourt, though he sometimes gave Paris as his place of work. He produced superior Stradivari copies in the Mirecourt style, featuring the characteristic dull yellow varnish typical of that region, but occasionally utilized an improved orange or red-brown recipe.

A student of Carlo Ferdinando Landolfi , Pietro Giovanni Mantegazza is considered the greatest maker of the Mantegazza family, which became linked to the great connoisseur and collector Count Cozio di Salabue in 1776. The relationship was a fortunate one for the family, as Cozio's patronage afforded them access to his extensive ... collection of fine instruments as well as steady repair work and commissions.
Guido Maraviglia created Classical Cremonese, Florentine, and personal models. He was a teacher of G.
Many violins, cellos, and guitars of ordinary workmanship were known, according to Henley. Vannes mentioned that the work was unknown, which remains true.
Eugène Marchand worked as a pupil of Durand and Laurent. He was employed by W.
Abbondio Marchetti produced a personal model that featured good professional workmanship and a fine red-brown varnish.
Edoardo Marchetti, son of Enrico Marchetti, briefly partnered with his brother Vittorio before establishing himself independently around 1902. He worked in association with R.

One of the finest Turinese makers of the 20th century, Enrico Marchetti moved to Turin in 1874 and apprenticed early on in the workshop of Benedetto Gioffredo ‘Rinaldi’ and later in the shop of Antonio Guadagnini before opening his own business in ... 1881. His early work showed great promise and was awarded numerous medals.
Giovanni Antonio Marchi moved to the city of Bologna around 1745 after the death of his father, a carpenter. He began making violins in 1755, likely as a pupil of Alessandro Fiorini.
Pupil and successor of Giuseppe Lucci in Rome.

The identity of violin maker Don Nicolo Amati has been the subject of considerable speculation for several hundred years. Thought to have been a nom d'etiquette of a violin making Bolognese priest with the surname Melchioni or Marchioni, the name 'D.

Giuseppe Marconcini, son of Luigi Aloisio Marconcini, produced fine work on the Amatisé model, utilizing hard clear orange or yellow-brown varnish. His purfling was made of ebony, and he generally used plain materials.
Luigi Marconcini moved from Bologna to Ferrara around 1766. He created a large model derived from Cremonese designs.
