Custode Marcucci began his career as a farmer and cooper before training at the Institute of Art in Bottajo. He established his workshop in San Agata, Ravenna, where he worked alone and became a prolific maker, credited with over 300 violins, 600 cellos, and 49 double basses.

Little is known about the early life of Romano Marengo, but at some point in his youth he moved to Turin, where he was employed by his relative Enrico Marchetti . After Benedetto Gioffredo "Rinaldi's" death in 1888, Marengo took over his thriving shop ... and worked for Rinaldi's widow.

Antonio Mariani often received comparisons to Brescian work, which frequently featured double purfling and decorative purfling designs, although no direct connection could be established. The decorative inlays appeared rather crude and unimaginative.
Ludovico Mariani was presumed to be the son of Antonio Mariani . He created rustic work in Pesaro in 1694.
Albert Marissal was the son and pupil of Olivier Marissal . He was a violinist and luthier who received a diploma in The Hague in 1949.
Olivier Marissal was the son and pupil of Auguste Marissal. He worked with Stradivari and Guarneri models and produced fine work using red-brown oil varnish.
John Marnie was a weaver and gardener. He later became a pupil of J.

John Marshall established his business in New Street, Covent Garden, possibly succeeding Benjamin Cooke, whose 'Golden Harp' enterprise was located there until shortly after 1745. Active in the 1750s-60s, Marshall is recognized as one of the best English interpreters of Stainer , producing instruments characterized by solid ... craftsmanship and a balanced approach compared to other Stainer copyists.
Biagio Caruana Marsigliese was a double bassist and violin maker who was active from 1905. He received gold medals at Padua, Rome, Florence, and Cremona.
E. Martin was a commercial bow maker.

Born in Mirecourt in 1837, Jean-Joseph Martin began his apprenticeship early to provide financial support to his family after his father's premature death. At 21, he moved to Paris to work for Jean Baptiste Vuillaume , lacking even the funds to travel there by coach.
Max Alfred Martin was the son, pupil, and assistant of Wilhelm Ernst Martin.
Otto Oswald Martin worked in various locations in Germany from 1884 to 1893. He emigrated to the USA in 1893 and established himself in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, the same year.
Paul Martin worked as the chief assistant for Jean Bauer in Angers starting in 1946. He was subsequently awarded medals at Cremona in 1982 and Kassel in 1983.
Marcello Martinenghi was the son and pupil of Stelio Martinenghi. He was active in Milan starting in 1933 and in Bassano del Grappa from 1950.
Oreste Martini was a pupil of Scarampella. He received medals at Cremona, Padua, and Paris.
Giuseppe Martino, originally from Messina, Sicily, emigrated to the USA in 1915. He was employed by O.
The model featured a large flat arch and exhibited undistinguished workmanship. It had a thick, hard spirit varnish that ranged from dark red to very dark brown, primarily due to oxidation.
Joseph Laurent Mast was the son and pupil of Jean Laurent Mast . He apprenticed to Didier Nicolas and established himself independently in Toulouse around 1800, on the rue des Balances.
Instruments were labelled from Paris and featured a large flat model with red-brown varnish.
Nicolas Mauchant produced commercial work in various grades and models. He created Maggini copies of notable distinction.
Charles Maucotel was a pupil of Blaise Mast. He was employed by Gand in Paris starting in 1834.

Charles Adolphe Maucotel worked for Vuillaume and was the brother of Charles Maucotel . He established his business in the Galerie Vivienne, Paris, in 1844, moved to Croix des Petits Champs in 1852, and finally settled in rue Princess.
Justin Maucotel created fine work based on classical Italian models, which included double-purfled Brescian types. He branded his work as 'Justin Maucotel Luthier. '.
Carolus Maurizi is the name used in commercial instruments imported and retailed by Beare and Son in London. He is based in Bologna, 19.
Francesco Maurizi created an Amatise model characterized by a poorly controlled outline, narrow edges, and short corners. The soundholes were rather constricted, particularly in the upper half.

Leonhard Maussiell's earliest work dated from around 1707 featured very fine craftsmanship on a model reminiscent of both Stainer and Tecchler. It had a deep edge flute with a medium high arch and a tightly wound scroll, which was sometimes substituted with carved lion's heads.
Andreas Ferdinand Mayr served as a luthier to the Archbishop's court. He created Stainer models that featured high arches and a scroll that was quite oval-shaped.
Leo Mayr trained at Mittenwald under M. Klotz, H.

Born near Manchester, Walter Mayson was an and prolific eccentric maker who seems to have been self-taught. Despite the relative mediocrity of his work, he appears to have done reasonably well in the city of Manchester until the arrival of George Adolph Chanot , whose training far outclassed that of Mayson.
Contemporary maker based in Piacenza, Italy. Graduate of the Istituto Liutario A.
James McIntosh worked early on a high-arched model featuring painted purfling. After 1869, he adopted a more classical Cremonese model.
John McIntosh specialized in copies of J. B.
William McNeill created a Guarneri model featuring a high arch and oil varnish. It was made in Edinburgh in 1891.
Jean-Jacques Meauchand was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Joseph Meuchand.

Born in Naples in 1964, Mecatti studied at the International School of Violin Makers in Cremona under Vincenzo Bissolotti, graduating in 1986. He moved to Paris and worked with Pierre Barthel and Bernard Sabatier until 1990 before studying bow restoration with Jean and Pierre Grunberger.

Nicolas Médard was the son of Henri Médard (I). Instruments were labelled from both Paris and Nancy, although it remains uncertain if he ever worked in Paris; this became a common practice among later Mirecourt luthiers.
James Meek completed 80 violins and violas, utilizing classical Italian models and his own design. He employed oil varnish in various shades with quality materials.
Karl Meier trained in Luby (Schönbach). He worked at various workshops in Markneukirchen.
Wilhelm Meier-Pauselius studied under August Diehl and Georg Winterling from 1910 to 1913. He worked for various makers in Germany, Italy, France, and Scandinavia.
Hans Meierl was a maker of violins and guitars. He was assisted by his son, Hans Meierl (II).
Friedrich Wilhelm Meinel was the son and pupil of Christian Friedrich Meinel (I). His work was similar to that of his father.
Paul Meinel was the brother of August Meinel (II). He apprenticed in Markneukirchen and was employed by N.
Jakob Meinertzen was a maker to the court and a viol maker. He was credited with a few violins.
Walter Heinrich Meinhold was a pupil of E. Tauscher and established himself independently in 1948 in Erlbach.
Friedrich August Meisel was a prolific maker known for a wide range of grades, from decent hand-finished work to rather crude commercial productions.
Friedrich Wilhelm Meisel engaged in coarse trade work. The soundholes were long and featured a poor opaque varnish.
Johann Georg Meisel created Stradivari-derived models. His rustic workmanship utilized fine materials.
Henry Meissner was brought up in Germany but returned to the USA in 1925. He traveled with an evangelical choir.

The Melegari brothers Enrico Clodoveo and Pietro are unique figures in the history of violin making in Turin. Presumed to be self-taught, they worked independently from their contemporaries and their work has little in common with the Turin school.

The Melegari brothers Enrico Clodoveo and Pietro are unique figures in the history of violin making in Turin. Presumed to be self-taught, they worked independently from their contemporaries and their work has little in common with the Turin school.
Adolfo Melli was a pupil of Dante Guastalla . He demonstrated good workmanship with lighter shades of golden-orange varnish.
Johann Georg Melzl worked in Munich before he took over the business of G. A.
Charles Mennégand apprenticed in Mirecourt. He was a pupil of Rambeaux in Paris from 1840 to 1845.

Emile Mennesson worked in Paris before joining Martin and Joseph Guarini in Mirecourt, where he later established his own extensive workshop from 1876 to 1881. He took over the Guarini business and used the name as a professional trademark for high-quality commercial instruments.
Andrew Mentiply was active in Ladybank and Gateside, Fife, before moving to Glasgow in 1915. He apprenticed as a joiner and later worked on the railway.
Jules Merciolle was a pupil of Grillon and worked for Mangenot and Audinot in Mirecourt, later moving to Paris to work with G. Bernardel in 1899, and subsequently with Caressa and Francais.
Pietro Mercolini worked in various locations in southern Italy. He was a pupil and associate of Maurizi, using classical Cremonese models.

Antonio Merighi moved to Milan around 1803. He completed a significant amount of work for Count Cozio di Salabue until 1810 and is recorded by the Count as having possessed the notable 1707 Stradivari cello known as the `Countess of Stanlein'.
Luigi Merighi established himself professionally in 1976 as a self-taught maker. He demonstrated good craftsmanship on Cremonese models, using varnish supplied by Pellacani in golden-brown shades.
Pietro Merighi made decent instruments with dark red varnish in Parma in the year 1794, known as leoni'.

Charles Meriotte trained in Mirecourt and was active in Lyon from approximately 1750 to 1763, after which he established himself in Paris. His work featured the old Mirecourt style with dull yellow-brown or red-yellow varnish.

Joseph Merlin worked in Paris around 1750 before moving to London in 1760. There, he received a commission to construct a barrel-organ for the Princess of Wales and collaborated with a goldsmith in New Street, Covent Garden, near John Marshall 's workshop.
Pauli Merling apprenticed with Bojesen Petersen before relocating to Dresden to work for Rudolphe Heckel. He was later employed by Rummelhoff Hansen in Oslo and Emil Hjorth in Copenhagen.
Maurice Mermillot apprenticed to Gaillard in Mirecourt. He was employed by Buthod in Paris in 1852 and moved to Rennes in 1853.
Alfio Messina was active in Catania from around 1920. He was well-regarded as a teacher.
Pietro Messori studied under Giuseppe Sgarbi starting in 1893. He worked for Fiorini in Munich before returning to Modena.
Josef Metelka was the son and pupil of Josef Metelka (I). His professional workmanship was very fine, utilizing excellent materials and varnish.
Gustave Methfessel apprenticed to P. Schulz in Regensburg and worked for Lembock in Vienna before establishing himself independently in Berne from 1864, later relocating to Hilterfingen.
Enzo Meucci was a pupil of G. Arcellaschi.
Joseph Meyer was the maker of the Alemannische school. He was the son of Theus Meyer and a pupil of his step-father Adam Kirner.
Magnus Andreas Meyer was possibly a pupil of Joachim Tielke . He created highly decorated viols and also produced violins on a large broad model featuring yellow or yellow-brown varnish.
Reinhardt Meyer began making violins in 1888. He established himself in Worcester, Massachusetts as a violinist and maker from 1918 to 1926.

Alessandro Mezzadri was a musician and violin maker, recognized as the founder of the Ferrara school of violin making. His style was individual and loosely modeled on the Amati, though it varied, with some instruments also reflecting Maggini influences.
Commercial brand for late 20th century Mirecourt violins.
Johann (Janos) Michelberger worked as a pupil of Andreas Bergmann. He collaborated with Mihaly Reményi, Karl Otto Zimmer , and Robert Laumann .
Jacques-Pierre Michelot established his business at the sign 'A la Mélodie' on rue St-Honoré. He was primarily a maker of guitars and lutes.
Plinio Michetti was a pupil of E. Peluzzi in Genoa and possibly acquainted with Candi and De Barbieri.
Otto Migge established a violin making school in Koblenz in 1880, known as Atelier für Cremoneser Geigenbaukunst. He worked in London starting in 1896 and moved to Eastbourne, Sussex in 1908.

George Miles was a self-taught British maker based in London, known for producing around 90 instruments during his lifetime. His early work on the Stainer model was eccentric and poorly accomplished, but he later developed a more satisfactory Stradivari pattern, utilizing his own red-brown varnish.
Jean-Jacques Millant was a bow maker and the son of Roger Millant . He apprenticed with Morizot Freres in Mirecourt from 1946 to 1948 before returning to his father's workshop in Paris.
Max Millant was the brother of Roger Millant . He was the grandson and pupil of S.
Roger Millant was the brother of Max Millant . He was a grandson and pupil of S.
Alexander Millar was a hairdresser and violin maker. He was a pupil of Thomas Hardie .
Norman Miller was a self-taught violin maker starting in 1956. He completed nine violins and three violas, with several based on a 1742 Guarneri violin model and later on a personal pattern.
Samuel Jansen Miller established himself professionally in Dryden from 1884. He was self-taught by reading Heron Allen's Violin Making as it was and is.
George E. Millis was a carpenter and a self-taught violin maker.

Patrick Gordon Milne was the son of Peter Milne and established himself in Aberdeen in 1896, initially at 11 Windmill Brae, then moving to Back Street after 1898, and later to Guild Street after 1812. In 1920, he relocated to Glasgow.
Frank Milner was a violinist and amateur maker known for good workmanship. One example he created, named 'The Flame, ' was made from root wood sycamore featuring complex grain and figure.
Louis Frank Milton established himself in Bedford in 1923. He was a pupil of A.
Active from around 1897, he was a musician and a self-taught violin maker. He completed 130 violins, eight violas, four cellos, and three double basses, all based on Cremonese models.
Contemporary maker in Ravenna, Italy. Trained with Renato Scrollavezza in Parma and has worked with other makers including Horacio Pineiro and Rene Morel.
Son of Paul Emile Miquel.

Paul Émile Miquel was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Nicolas, a luthier and organ builder located on Faubourg Saint-Vincent, and he established himself independently at 5 rue de Pre de Paradis around 1870.
Claude Augustin Miremont was a violin and bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Sebastien Miremont.
Max Möckel was the son and pupil of Oswald Möckel . He worked in Warsaw starting in 1897, then in Moscow, and around 1899-1902 in St.

Oswald Möckel studied under Carl Grimm and Christian Adam, later establishing his own shop in Berlin in 1869. He was known for his classical models, which featured distinctive personal developments and fine red oil varnish.
Otto Möckel, son and pupil of Oswald Möckel, also trained with Carl Grimm. He worked in London for Richard Bruckner from 1890 to 1892 before rejoining his father in Berlin.
Giuseppe Modaudo worked for the Carabba Brothers in Catania starting in 1894. He emigrated to the USA in 1905 and established himself in New York, later moving to Chicago.
William Moennig apprenticed to his uncle Adolf in Budapest, Hungary. He moved to the USA in 1896 to study with his brother-in-law Julius Guetter in Philadelphia.

William Hermann Moennig, son and pupil of William Heinrich Moennig, studied violin making with Paul Dorfel and bow making with Paul Heberlein in Markneukirchen, and later attended the Mittenwald School of Violin Making. In 1936, he became the first American to obtain his Master's certificate.
Last of the Moennig line. Headed the family business in Philadelphia from 2004 until it closed in 2010.

Alberto Fernando Moglie completed his first instrument in 1908 after studying under Antonio Sgarbi from 1920 to 1907. He worked for mandolin maker Luigi Enbergher, and later for Rudolpho Fredi and Giuseppe Rossi .
Charles Moinel was the son and pupil of François Moinel . He also trained with Emile Germain.
Charles Amedée Moinel was a bow maker and the son of François Xavier. He trained in Mirecourt with L.
Daniel Moinel was the son and pupil of Charles Moinel . He apprenticed with his uncle L.
Louis Moitessier was the son of Prosper Antoine Moitessier . He was a prolific maker known for large models.
Prosper Antoine Moitessier was primarily an organ builder. The Musical Instruments Museum in Brussels housed one violin labeled: P.

Angelo Molia was a pupil of Bernardo Calcani, likely serving as an assistant towards the end of Calcani's life, although there is no documentary evidence of this connection. His work, characterized by a somewhat rustic finish, focused on small models derived from Stradivari.
Giuseppe Molinari was presumed to be the son of Antonio Molinari . He was known solely for plucked instruments, including a mandolin located in the Musee de la Musique, Paris, which was labelled.
Guillaume Max Möller was the son, pupil, and assistant of Paul Max Moller. He trained at Mittenwald and was employed by A.

Paul Max Möller was born in Adorf and studied in Markneukirchen. He worked in the Nurnberger shop from the age of 14, moved to St Petersburg to work for Max Möckel, then joined Karel van der Meer in Amsterdam, where in 1913 he opened his own workshop.
Pierre Mondy created a violin with fine wood and yellow varnish, which was cited by Vannes. The manuscript label indicated the year 1828.
John King Monk produced decent work derived from Stradivari. His small output included some instruments afflicted by 'innovative' bass bar arrangements and other concepts.
August Hermann Mönnig was a fine bow maker. He was the son and successor of Johann Monnig.
Fritz Mönnig worked for A. Hollinger in Kiel, G.

Domenico Montagnana arrived in Venice in around 1701 at the age of 15, shortly after Matteo Gofriller had established himself as the leading light in the emerging Venetian school. However, it seems that Montagnana apprenticed not with Goffriller but with Matteo Sellas, who later also employed Carlo ...
Atillio Montanari studied under R. Ronchini.
Luigi Montanari worked for L. Bisiach and was a pupil of R.
Erminio Montefiori settled in Genoa in 1856. He trained as a sculptor and became a self-taught violin and bow maker starting in 1883, located at 4/4 vico S.
Armando Monterumici was a musician and a pupil of R. Fiorini.
Luigi Montevecchi trained as a cabinet-maker at the Bologna Academy of Fine Arts. He developed a personal model based on Cremonese forms.
Antonio Carlo Monzino trained at Markneukirchen, Germany, and later in Mirecourt, France. From 1929, he taught in Italy with the help of employees from the company, A.
Milan family firm of bowed and plucked instruments.
British maker based on the Isle of Man, active around the turn of the 20th century.
Arnaldo Morano was a significant maker, restorer, and connoisseur from Italy in the 20th century. Initially self-taught, he exhibited at Cremona in 1937 and received a gold medal at Cremona in 1949.
Paolo Morara was an artist and musician. He trained as a violin maker under G.

Gio Battista Morassi was born in 1934 outside Udine and after showing early promise entered the International School of Violin Making in Cremona. He attended the school from 1951 to 1955 under the tutelage of Giuseppe Ornati and Ferdinando Garimberti , ... and after his military service returned to take up a career educating some of the best twentieth century makers from Italy and abroad.

Cited in most dictionaries, with speculation that he was the pupil of the lute maker Pietro Darden Two labels are quoted from violins or fragments in the Brussels Musical Instruments Museum (ex Snoeck) with Mantua as origin, and from P. De Wit giving Venice, but both are dubious.
Carlo Moretti was credited with 200 violins, violas, and cellos. He produced fine work featuring golden-red varnish.
Egidio Moretti trained as a cabinet maker. He was an active luthier from around 1924 and worked in Lavagna, Genoa from 1945, but returned to Udine after 1950.

Felice Mori Costa's distinctive workmanship featured an elegantly slender design, characterized by long soundholes and low-set nicks cut in an unusual manner, reminiscent of Guadagnini. While his choice of materials could be plain, he often applied a fine varnish that varied from dark golden-yellow to plum-colored.
Alfred Moritz was a commercial manufacturer in Dresden. He sold through agents and was especially popular in London under the title 'Excelsior. ' His work was decent and featured a pale varnish.

André Auguste Morizot specialized in cambering bow sticks for the joint output of the family workshop Morizot Frères, where he was the son of Louis Joseph Morizot. He also crafted top grade bows featuring silver or gold mounts.

The five sons of prolific and successful Mirecourt bowmaker Louis Morizot père all trained with their father and worked in partnership. They each specialized in different types of bows or aspects of the bow making process, enabling them to produce an impressive number of bows with assembly-line efficiency.

Louis Gabriel Morizot was a bow maker and the son of Louis Joseph Morizot. He was known as 'Le Pompier' and primarily focused on crafting double bass bows and frogs for the family business, Morizot Frères.

Louis Joseph Morizot, or Morizot père, learned bow making from Eugène Cuniot-Hury and Charles Nicolas Bazin , for whom he worked from about 1901 to about 1914. Morizot then began working for Eugène ...
Marcel Louis Morizot was the son of Louis Joseph Morizot and was known as 'le petit vieux'. He prepared rough bow sticks in the family workshop, Morizot Frères.
René Morizot was the sixth son of Louis Morizot and the last of the dynasty, known as 'Le Gamin'. He was a violin maker and a pupil of Audinot, who was a popular and influential teacher.
Nicolas Morlot was a pupil of Nicolas Morlot aine. He created a large Stradivari model that featured flat arches and was finished with pale brown or yellow varnish.
Georgy Alexeyevich Morozov studied under D. P.

Born in Biloxi, MS on October 26, 1953 and studied classical piano at Cornish College of the Arts. After his career as a violin maker in Florida, especially praised for his violas, he died on January 19, 2018, in Tucson, AZ.
Archibald Morrison established himself as a musician and violin maker in Glasgow from 1854. He worked for John Mann starting in 1860 and became independent in 1865.

Robert Morrow’s bow shop is located in Port Townsend, Washington, where he completed an apprenticeship with Charles Epsey. Examples of his work have won five Gold Medals from the VSA and an Hors Concours status.
John William Mortimer created a small model with his own varnish, which was quite successful. His double basses held particular value.
Leon Mortin succeeded Vuillaume-Darte in 1891. The model was Stradivarian and featured chestnut red varnish.
Apprenticed to G. Deroux from 1855.

Léon Mougenot first learned violin making under Émile Laurent , and later apprenticed with his father's cousin Georges in Brussels from 1887 to 1894. Mougenot returned to France, working in Lyon and Paris and later at the London firm of W.E. ...
Frederick G. Moverley made violins based on the Guarneri pattern and violas following the Terns model.
David Mowbray was active in Melrose from around 1878 and moved to Leith in 1890. He produced good work based on Cremonese models.
Hidalgo Moya trained in Mirecourt, France. He established himself in Aylestone around 1890 and continued until he was stricken with ill health in 1920.

Luigi Mozzani established a workshop for guitar making in Cento in 1910 and began making violins there as well from 1917. He followed a Guadagnini model for violins, violas, and cellos, using dark red varnish.
Antonio Mucchi was thought to be a pupil of Soliani. He created several good cellos in the style of Rocca and Pressenda, using yellow oil varnish.
Albert Carl Muller was a violin and bow maker who studied under Louis Prince starting in 1938 and later trained with Abraham Koodlach. He worked for Sherman & Clay and Emil Herrmann .
Carl Albert Müller was the grandson and pupil of Christian Friedrich Müller (II). He worked for M.
Hans Muller was the son of Otto Müller. He studied under Nürnberger in Markneukirchen and later worked for Kotovice in Poland, Stowasser in Graz, Voss in Charlottenburg, and G.
Heinrich (Harry) Müller studied under O. Windisch from 1920 to 1923 and later with K.

Josef Müller was a violin and bow maker, the son and pupil of Andreas Müller, a wind instrument maker and dealer. He distinguished himself as a flautist and as a maker and repairer of brass and woodwind instruments, taking over his father's shop in 1873.
Ernest Mumby was a pupil of Whitmarsh. He used good workmanship and materials for his personal model.
Romedio Muncher worked for Cavalli's 'Monteverdi' workshop. He later established himself independently.

Alexander Murdoch established himself in Aberdeen by 1865, at 9 Minster Lane until around 1900. He later formed a partnership with Alexander Dingwall, creating Murdoch and Dingwall at the Market Gallery in Aberdeen.
Daniel C. Murray completed about 60 violins.
David Murray was a violinist and violin maker who had previously worked as a miner. He was credited with over 100 instruments.
James Murray was a railway engine driver and an amateur violin maker. He was credited with approximately 30 violins featuring various models and mediocre yellow oil varnish.

Umberto Muschietti was a double bass player and maker, credited with 160 violins, 18 violas, eight cellos, and two double basses. One of his basses, a replica of his own five-string Santagiuliana instrument, is now housed in the Museo Stradivariano in Cremona.
Johann Muschke was a pupil of Jan Krampera. He established himself in Frankfurt in 1867.
Cestmir Musil was the son of Jan Musil and created highly finished copies of classical Italian instruments. The work featured fine materials with spirit varnish and lightly rubbed edges.
Giuseppe Mussolesi was a pupil and assistant of C. Colombo.
Jean-Baptiste Léonidas Nadegini worked with F. Spidlen in Moscow.
Giuseppe Nadotti possibly gained experience with G. B.
Jean Théodore Namy worked for Salomon's widow starting in 1772. He established himself independently in place du Louvre in 1788.
Frank Donald Napier apprenticed at W. E.
William Napier joined the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in 1889 after apprenticing as a pianomaker with the Broadwood firm.
Contemporary Italian maker based in Pistoia. Studied with Mauro Scartabelli and Ovidio Giarelli.
John H. Nebeker was a bow maker and violin repairer.

Fred Nebel was the son and pupil of Andrew Nebel and also studied with his uncle Martin, as well as in Mittenwald, Germany. He worked in Mittenwald for Johann Bader and collaborated with Michael Dötsch and Theodor Schrage in Berlin.
Trained in Germany, he moved to America in 1922 and worked at the Wurlitzer firm from 1922-1924. He then moved to Philadelphia to establish his own shop.
Martin Nebel graduated from the Mittenwald School in 1910. He worked for Johann Stiibiger in Vienna and for Eugen Gärtner in Stuttgart.
Joseph Neff trained as a piano maker in Germany. He emigrated to the USA in 1840, initially settling in New York before establishing himself as a piano maker in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Gilles Nehr was born in the south west region of France. He started studying bow-making at the age of 17 with his cousin, Jean-Pascal Nehr, in Marseille, and graduated from Mirecourt at age 20.
Brother of Jean-Pascal Nehr and apprenticed with him from 1995. Graduated from Mirecourt in 1998 and opened his workshop in Paris in 2002.
Stefan W. Nemes was a descendant of Samuel Nemessanyi.

Leading Hungarian violin maker Samuel Nemessanyi apprenticed with Thomas Zach and Johann Baptist Schweitzer in Pest before moving in 1858 to Prague, where he worked for Anton Sitt, gaining first-hand experience with fine Italian violins. Two years later ... he returned to Hungary and worked in various towns before settling in Pest, where he lived through multiple marriages, many children, financial tumult, and emotional instability, all the while enjoying success as the city's best maker and restorer until his premature death in 1881.
Alfred Neudörfer was the son of František Neudöfer. He produced finely finished work, including Guarneri copies made with excellent materials and transparent spirit varnish.

Rudolf Neudörfer grew up in the Czech Republic and went to the Violin Making School in Luby, learning the craft of violin making from his teachers Bohumil Pechar and Hans Schmirler. He then worked at the Cremona cooperation for string instruments there until he escaped socialist Czechia by posing as a ski coach at the Olympic Games in 1964.
Johann Christoph Neumarker created one violin with red-brown varnish, as cited by Vannes & Henley.
Joseph Neuner created a cello with amber-brown varnish, which was dated 1806 and cited by Vannes.
Son and pupil of Johann Neuner (III). Studied the cello.
Mathias Neuner was the son of Johann Neuner and used the spelling 'Neiner' on early labels. A prolific maker, he exported his work throughout Europe and employed many craftsmen.
Mathias Neuner continued the firm of Neuner & Hornsteiner. He was the grandson of Mathias Neuner (IV).
Brand used for a bowmaker named Neudorfer who made bows for the Finkel workshop.
Brand used for bowmaker Rudolf Neudorfer who made bows for the Finkel workshop.
Charles Neveu established his business in the rue Manton-Devenest from 1893 to 1905. He subsequently abandoned lutherie to become a commercial trader in instruments.
Utili Nicola was initially a maker of plucked instruments. He later developed an eccentric violin design featuring inverted corners.
Antoine Nicolas was noted by the Principal of the Luthiers' Vannes as having 'work unknown, ' but Henley provided an unverifiable description of low-quality work. A label was supplied by De Wit.
Known as 'le Sourd' (`the deaf one'). Son of Antoine Nicolas .
François Fourrier (François II) Nicolas was the son and pupil of François Nicolas (I). He was employed by E.

Joseph Nicolas was the son and pupil of Didier Nicolas and succeeded his father in 1816, continuing his work with little noticeable differentiation. After his death, the rights to the triangular label 'A la Ville de Cremonne, ' used by Didier, were purchased by J.
Karl Niedt was a pupil of E. F.
Simpert Niggel was the son-in-law of Johann Ott and the most important maker of this family. He demonstrated fine craftsmanship on a model derived from Stainer.
Vahakn Y. Nigogosian was an active violin maker from 1925.
Gottfried Nilsson was the son, pupil, assistant, and successor of Nils Nilsson. He continued the tradition of fine instruments and restoration established by his father.
William Nisbet was a self-taught craftsman who made approximately 120 violins. His early work concentrated on Maggini models, later shifting to an Amati-derived pattern.
Louis Noebe was a violinist and violin maker. He created Stradivari models with decent workmanship and varnish in various suitable colors.
Floriano Nofri was a violin and bow maker, active from approximately 1948 to 1982. He received some assistance from G.
Contemporary Italian maker trained in Cremona. Worked for Giorgio Scolari before setting up independently in Cremona.
Giuseppe Nonini was a self-taught maker. He received diplomas in Trieste in 1931 and in Friuli in 1948.
John Norborn was found in the works of other makers, particularly Jacob Ford . He was assumed to be a general music dealer.
Heinz Nord was a pupil of M. Schuster in Markneukirchen and K.

The late-17th-century viol maker Barak Norman was one of the most important figures in the transition to violin-family instruments in the English tradition. His earliest known viol dates from 1689, and by 1692 he had set up a workshop in St.

John Norris was a pupil of Thomas Smith and a business partner of Robert Barnes, who was also a pupil of Smith, from 1765 to 1794 in Great Windmill Street. After their business partnership dissolved, he relocated to 35 Coventry Street, where Richard and William Davis continued his shop.
Václav Nosek was reported by Henley to be the best maker in the region during that period. He used fine materials and his own oil varnish.
John Note was brought to the USA with his family in 1890. He was employed by Gabriel Francois in Pittsburg and was a highly regarded repairer and restorer.
Natale Novelli was a pupil and nephew of G. Pedrazzini.

Pietro Valentino (Marco) Novello apprenticed to Giorgio Serafin in the workshop known as 'Alla Cremona' around 1773 and remained there after Giorgio's death in 1779, when A. Bellosio took over the business.
Adolf Nürnberger was a bow maker and the nephew and pupil of Johann Christoph Nürnberger . He worked in Dresden alongside J.

Throughout the late 19th and 20th centuries, the Nürnberger family workshop produced a great number of high-quality bows, many on the firm's signature Tourte model. The traditional family brand, which went into use around 1920, is "*Albert Nürnberger*" and was used by various makers of the family, including Franz ...

Carl Albert Nürnberger built on his father Franz Albert Nürnberger Jr. 's success to bring his family's long tradition of bow making to its peak in the 1920s. He apprenticed in the family workshop Markneukirchen, where he gained the skills required to produce the firm's signature Tourte -model ... bows.
Son of Karl Albert and currently the last of the Nürnberger dynasty of makers. Uses the family brand "*Albert Nürnberger*".
Franz Albert Nürnberger was the son and pupil of Christian Gottlob Nürnberger. He became a founding member of the Markneukirchen bow makers guild in 1888.

Fifth in a line of successful German bow makers, Franz Albert Nurnberger Jr gradually developed his family's model into one of the most convincing interpretations of François Xavier Tourte in modern bow making. He began branding his bows around 1890 and inherited the workshop in 1894.
Johann Christoph Nürnberger worked for five years with J. B.
Karl Albert Nürnberger was a bow maker and the son and assistant of Karl Albert Nürnberger (I). He was also the father of Christian Albert.
Fl. c. 1920. Commercial violins
Emigrated to USA in 1912. Originally from Schönlind (not one of the Markneukirchen family of bow makers).
Terrence O'Laughlin worked as a shoemaker in Connecticut. He was active as a violin maker in Boston starting in 1895 and was credited with several hundred violins.

Although Carlo Giuseppe Oddone apprenticed for several years at the Benedetto Gioffredo Rinaldi workshop, his more formative training took place in England, where he traveled to work for Frederick William Chanot in 1889. In light of Chanot's background ... in the French tradition, Oddone's experience in his workshop gave him an entirely different perspective on methods of construction and repair than he would have absorbed in Turin.

Son of a farmer who dabbed in woodworking, Giuseppe Odoardi first became acquainted with violin making after being called upon to repair an instrument in his rural hometown outside Ascoli Piceno. While his clients were mostly local at first, his skills quickly made him known to professional musicians passing through the region, who turned to him for repair work and also commissioned instruments from ... him.

The Cremona workshop 'Officina Claudio Monteverdi' was established by the maker Aristide Cavalli and his son Lelio in 1880. It employed many pupils and produced trade instruments of a lower quality than Cavalli's own personal ones.
Violins were made for him by Albert Lind , using the Stradivari model and featuring good varnish and materials.
Christian Olivier established a partnership in Paris with Paul Bisch to sell instruments. He was not a maker himself.
J. Olry was a pupil of Georges Chanot.

Lars Jorgen Rudolph Olsen apprenticed to Hjorth & Sons in Copenhagen from 1904 to 1909. He worked for Otto Möckel in Dresden from 1909 to 1913 and briefly in Berlin and Amsterdam for Edward Vandermeer and Max Möller, where he studied bow making.
Anton Olson was a violin and bow maker. He emigrated to the USA in 1903 and established a violin business in Worcester, Massachusetts in 1926.
James Omond was a clock maker and amateur luthier. He was active from 1872 in Stromness, Orkney, before later relocating to Kirbuster.
Walter Opel apprenticed to Max Heischkel in Erlbach. He worked for Lüdemann in Cologne and for Storck in Strasburg.
Imre Orban studied under G. Várady at the Hungarian State Violin Making school around 1922.

Archimede Orlandini exhibited personal models in Cremona in 1937. He received a certificate of honour in Cremona in 1949, a silver medal in Cremona in 1971, and a silver medal for varnish in Bagnocavallo in 1980.
Giuseppe Ornati was the son of Carlo, an amateur luthier. He studied under C.
Enrico Orselli was a pupil and assistant of Soffritti. He established himself independently in Pesaro from 1924.

Mariano Ortega was the son and pupil of Silverio Ortega , learning his craft alongside his father in the 1800s. His work featured a fine and tasteful style based on Amatisé or Stradivari-derived models, characterized by well-proportioned designs and the use of good materials.

Silverio Ortega was a pupil of Dom Vicenzo Ascensio and succeeded him as pithier to the Spanish court. He was unfortunately best known for his involvement in the cutting down of the 'Spanish Bass', the Stradivari cello made for the court in 1696.
William Frederick Oskay studied under Harry Stoutenburgh from 1957 to 1959. He established himself in Kingston, New York, in 1959 and moved to Port Ewen in 1961.
George A. Ostertag apprenticed to Franz Kerschensteiner.
Anton Ostler studied at the Mittenwald School of Violin Making starting in 1908. He worked in Munich, Nancy, and Saarbrucken.
Joseph Ott was born in Langendorf in Mähren in 185..
Carl August Otto was the son and pupil of Jacob August. He established himself in Ludwigslust, Mecklenburg, in 1832.
Carl Wilhelm Friedrich Otto was the son and pupil of Jacob August Otto. He also trained with his eldest brother, George August.
Georg August Gottfried Otto was the eldest son, pupil, and successor of Jacob August Otto. He produced fine work using good varnish and materials.
Louis Otto was the son and pupil of Johann Karl August Otto. From 1865 to 1866, he worked with his cousin Ludwig in Cologne.
Ludwig Otto was the son and pupil of Georg August Gottfried Otto . He worked in Erfurt starting in 1850, in Cologne from 1855, and in St.
Rudi Otto was the son and pupil of Paul Max Otto and apprenticed to R. Fenzel.
Wilhelm Otto was the son, pupil, and successor of Louis Otto . After serving an apprenticeship with Simoutre in Paris, he returned to Düsseldorf in 1900 and took over his father's business in 1920.
Son of Emile Auguste Ouchard, he began making at 13. At the end of 1941 he moved to Paris with his father.

Émile François Ouchard was apprenticed to Eugène Cuniot-Hury and took over the Mirecourt firm after Cuniot's death in 1910. Ouchard's son Émile Auguste began to assist him as early as 1913, and in 1923 Ouchard 'père' set up his own firm.

Bowmaker Émile Auguste Ouchard first studied with his father Émile François at the Mirecourt shop of Cuniot-Hury and began to assist him around 1913. Father and son continued to work together after Émile Francois opened his own shop in 1923, and ... Émile Auguste began to produce increasingly well-crafted work in collaboration with his father.
Jean Ouvrard was a pupil of Claude Pierray . He served as master of the violin makers guild from 1742 to 1743.
Ivy Rimmer Owen was the daughter and assistant of John William Owen . She trained as a violinist and studied wood carving with a Mr.

John William Owen was a trained engineer and a self-taught violin maker active from 1884. He was credited with approximately 200 violins, violas, cellos, and double basses, along with many bows, all made at his home, "Amati House. " His good oil varnish, of his own formula, ranged from yellow to deep red.
Contemporary British bow maker based in Oxford. Winner of various awards.
Michel Pacherele was the son of Jacob Pacherele. Although one label indicated Paris, as cited by Vidal, he was recorded as having lived continuously in Mirecourt during his active years.

Pierre Pacherele was probably trained in his hometown of Mirecourt, though information about his early life is scant. In the 1820s he travelled to Italy, and spent some time in Turin, where he probably met Giovanni Francesco Pressenda .
Carl Padewet was the brother of Johann Padewet (I). He established himself in Munich in 1856.
Johann Padewet was the son and pupil of Johann Padewet (I) and also completed an apprenticeship with A. Riechers in Hanover.
Johann Karl Padewet was the son, pupil, and successor of Johann Padewet (II). He also studied under F.
Giuseppe Secondo Paganini worked briefly in London. He established himself in Florence in 1907.
Luigi Paganini was active in Faenza and Forli from around 1856 to 1903, before moving to Florence. He produced copies of classical Cremonese work and had a limited output.
Aldo Pagliacci was a self-taught maker. He created copies of Landolfi using red-brown or orange varnish.
Thomas D. Paine created a large personal model featuring a truncated volute and a pointed chin to the pegbox.

The son of Louis Simon Pajeot , Étienne Pajeot learned the trade early in the family workshop. Pajeot's brand first appears around 1815, and his early bows are very much in his father's style, with rather pointed heads and low, elongated frogs.
Louis Simon Pajeot was a bow maker who was active from around 1780. He also had some violins of trade quality attributed to him.
Giovanni Battista Pallaver studied under A. Bisiach and R.

Pietro Pallotta was active in Turin and Perugia in the late 18th to early 19th centuries. He created personal models characterized by full arching, medium height, and strong edge flute, with long soundholes that were widely set and occasionally slanted.

Edward Pamphilon was one of numerous members of his family who supposedly made violins in and around Essex in the 1600s. Historical evidence indicates that Pamphilon worked roughly from 1660 to 1700, likely transporting his instruments to London for sale in various shops.
Giovanni Battista Panizzi was a violinist and a self-taught maker of violins and bows. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models, employing fine materials and craftsmanship.

George Panormo was the third son of Sicilian-born violin maker Vincenzo Panormo and worked with his younger brother Louis in Bloomsbury, London, starting in 1848. He also briefly worked for the Banks brothers in Liverpool, as indicated by a cello dated 1736 that bore his label.
Son of George Panormo and nephew of Louis Panormo . Took over Louis's instrument making business around 1855 and ran it until his death.

Son of Vincenzo Panormo , Joseph Panormo is believed to have been born in Naples and worked for his father alongside his brothers, George and Louis , in various locations. By 1801 ... he was working independently in his own shop in London, where he made guitars, violins and cellos of good craftsmanship and strong personality.

Louis Panormo, the youngest son of Vincenzo, was active in London from 1817 as Panormo & Co, Musical Instrument Makers and Bow Makers at 26 High Street, Bloomsbury. He moved to 46 High Street in 1830 and also operated businesses at 6 Greek Street and 22 Compton Street, consolidating them at 31 High Street in 1850.

Vincenzo Panormo was assisted by his sons Joseph, George and Louis. Joseph's hand is apparent in several instruments labeled Vincenzo, some of which are signed 'J.P.' or 'Joseph Panormo' on the top block.

Born Vincenzo Trusiano in Palermo, Sicily, Panormo changed his surname after moving with his family to Naples in around 1759. From Naples he appears to have traveled to Paris via Marseille, arriving in Paris around 1770.
Silvio Vezio Paoletti was a pupil of V. de Zorzi in Florence. He established himself independently in 1925, taking over the shop of P.
Luigi Paolini was a prolific maker, active in Rimini and Todi. He worked with Rocca, Amati, and Stainer models.
Jean Baptiste Paquotte was the nephew of Sébastien Paquotte. He apprenticed with Simon as a bow maker and later studied violin making with Sébastian in Paris from 1841 to 1849.
Placide Paquotte was the son, pupil, and successor of Jean-Baptiste Paquotte. He was a fine craftsman, excelling as both a maker and a restorer.
Rudolfo Paralupi was active as a guitar maker in Rome from 1922 and made his first violin in 1927. He received awards in Rome in 1929 and in Parma in 1933.
Gaetano Pareschi created classical Cremonese and personal models. He used golden- or red-brown varnish.
Rémy Parizot was active in Nantes from 1774. His workmanship was ordinary, but the arching and varnish were good.

One of the most accomplished and mysterious British violin makers, Daniel Parker seems to have been a contemporary of John Hare and may have studied with Barak Norman . He probably did much of his work for those two makers, in addition to other London shops, but there is no evidence that he served a formal apprenticeship or ever ... set up on his own.
John Parkinson was a coal miner and amateur luthier. He worked in collaboration with his brother-in-law, Fisher.

Romolo Parmeggiani was a violinist and a maker of violins and bows, known for his Stradivari model. He received awards in Fiume and Cremona in 1937, as well as in Rome in 1952 and 1954, and also in New York.
Léon Paroche was the name given in commercial Mirecourt instruments sold in London by Beare & Son. Modèle / Billancourt / B&SL Anno..
Piero Parravicini worked as a pupil of R. Antoniazzi.
Christian Franz Partl worked with coarse materials and dull brown varnish. Some instruments were unpurfled and lacked linings or blocks.
Ignaz Christian Partl was the son of Michael Andreas Partl. He created a neatly made Stainer model featuring a high arch with small edges and corners.
Frank Passa was a violin and bow maker and a pupil of S. F.

Domenico Pasta was the son of Bartolomeo Pasta and worked alongside his brother Gaetano in the workshop called 'Alla Pallada'. The models were inspired by Amati and Maggini, showcasing convincing workmanship.

Gaetano Pasta was the son of Bartolomeo Pasta and was active in Brescia from around 1694. His work featured exuberant classical forms along the lines of Amati and Rogeri, characterized by impetuous craftsmanship.
Brussels-based bow maker. Period bow copies.
Benjamin Patocka was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of Josef Metelka in Paseky in 1879.
Jan Bohuslav (Johannes Godefridus) Pauli was the son of Antonín Pauli. His work was unremarkable and featured a transparent yellow varnish.
Joseph Pauli was a pupil of J. G.
Carl A. Paulsen was the son and pupil of P.
Peter Christian Paulsen emigrated to the USA in 1882. He was a pupil of P.
Albin Ludwig Paulus was a pupil of Dolling. He established himself independently around 1890 and was assisted by his son Kurt.
Johann Georg Paulus was a luthier to the Pmssian Court. His work was fine but lacked distinction in any way.
Konrad Friedrich Paulus was a pupil of his brother Albin Ludwig Paulus . He worked for J.
Otto Paul Paulus studied under his brother Albin Paulus. He worked for Tieteman in Wolfenbütttel and established himself independently in Markneukirchen starting in 1904.

Antonio Pazarini was a very rare Genoese maker of the 18th century who worked in association with Bernardo Calcani. Surviving instruments suggest that they produced instruments together, often labeled 'Antonius Pazarinius et Calcanius / Genuae 1740.' Pazarini's models were large, featured a medium high arching, and had an orange varnish
William Pearce was the brother or father of George. He was an employee of Forster.

Son of François Peccatte and nephew of Dominique , Charles Peccatte was born into a family of great bow making pedigree. Following his father's death in 1851, he began his apprenticeship in 1862 to his stepfather Auguste ...
Dominique Peccatte was born in Mirecourt and at first was apprenticed to a hairdresser. On the recommendation of Nicolas Vuillaume he moved to Paris in 1826 to join J.B.

Younger brother of Dominique Peccatte , François Peccatte began his apprenticeship near Mirecourt but left to join his brother in Paris sometime around 1840. Dominique was a formative influence on his brother's work, which features square, strong heads and is of good quality.
Vasco Pecchini was a violin and bow maker. He studied under E.
Costanzo Pedicino was an artist and a self-taught luthier. He created both Stradivari and personal models.

The son of a carpenter, Pedrazzini was born in the village of Pizzeghettone near Cremona. In 1903 he moved to Milan where he appears to have taught himself violin making, with some advice from Romeo Antoniazzi .
Antonio Pedrinelli was a prolific maker known for models and copies of many classical Italian makers. His work was produced in various grades, featuring different levels of finish and materials.
Antonio Pelizon was presumably the son of Francesco Pelizon. His work was impetuous and not dissimilar to Landoll Arching, being of medium height but rather box-like.
Giuseppe Francesco Pelizon worked with the bow maker V. Schilling.
Giuseppe Pellacani was a violinist and a pupil in lutherie of N. Bianchi in Nice and E.
Giuseppe Peluzzi was active for some time in Turin. He produced very fine and clever work on Cappa and Rocca models.
Penzel was part of a family of luthiers and bow makers who worked in Vogtland, Germany, and Bohemia, Czech Republic.
Gustav Penzel was a bow maker of some distinction. He used good pernambuco wood and branded both octagonal and round sticks with the mark: PENZEL.
P. Robert Penzel was a pupil of O.
Ignaz Penzl was presumably connected to the Penzel family. He engaged in commercial work on an Amati/Stainer-derived model.
François Pérain used hard orange-red varnish and fine materials. It was branded on the back: 'françois perain'.
Sergio Peresson was active from 1943. He moved to Caracas, Venezuela in 1947 and was employed as a repairer for the Venezuelan Symphony Orchestra.
Eugène Perrard apprenticed to L. Mougenot.
Étienne Perrin created typical Mirecourt work featuring a flat arch and thin spirit varnish. It was branded: `E.
François Perrin created typical commercial work of the time and place. It featured low arching with a decent golden-orange or brown varnish.
James Perry was the younger brother of Thomas Perry (II). He produced coarse work, some of which was unpurfled.
Stephen Perry created models based on Guarneri and Brescian designs. Approximately 12 were made using local materials.

Thomas Perry was thought to have been a descendant of Claude Pierray of Paris. He established himself in Christ Church Yard from 1741 and was recorded as 'Mr Pierrie, Fiddle Maker' at the time of his death.

Thomas Perry was an Irishman by birth, but is thought to have been related to the French violin maker Claude Pierray . He followed in the footsteps of his father, also a violin maker, and took over his shop in Dublin around 1766.

A general examination of his style suggests the influence of Nicolas Lupot , but it is not known for certain where Jean Pierre Marie Persoit learned bow making or who initially employed him. Around 1825 he began to mark some of his bows with a characteristic "P.R.S.," which explains why his name was misspelled as "Persois" for ... so long.

Jago Peternella, originally from Legnano near Verona, emigrated to the USA in 1934. He is known for his fine craftsmanship and materials, reflecting the style of Giulio Degani, with whom he is thought to have trained in Venice.
William L. Peters created classical Italian models featuring distinctive edge work.
Zenon Wladimir Petesh made six violins around 1935. He was a pupil of Didtchenko in Warsaw, Poland from 1937 to 1940.
Emilio Petraglia was a pupil in Buenos Aires of Giovanni Capalbo . He was appointed luthier to the Argentine government in 1937.
Jakob Petz was the nephew of Franz Petz and created fine work on typical Tyrolean models. The long form featured arching of medium height.
Marianus Petz was the successor to J. F.
Franz Paul Petzold worked in Gablenz in 1885 and established himself in Chemnitz from 1888. He produced good professional work with spirit varnish.
Hermann Petzold was the son and pupil of Ernst Hermann Petzold . He worked in Nuremberg, Magdeburg, and Gera in 1931, and in Berlin from 1934.
Ernesto Pevere was a pupil of E. Soffriti.
Alfred Peyrot was a professional cellist and a self-taught amateur maker and repairer. He may have been influenced by J.
William V. Pezzoni worked in the violin department at the C.
Friedrich August Pfab apprenticed in Klingenthal. He worked in Hamburg starting in 1844 and received several medals.
Berthold Walter Pfretzschner was a bow maker. He was the son, pupil, and assistant of Hermann Pfretzschner.
Carl Friedrich Pfretzschner was admitted to the guild of violin makers in 1765. He was a distinguished maker of the Saxon school and a prolific craftsman.
Carl Friedrich Pfretzschner worked for Bausch in Leipzig in 1863. He established himself in Markneukirchen from 1871 to 1886.
Carl Gottlob Pfretzschner was the son and pupil of Christoph Gottfried Pfretzschner. He apprenticed in Dresden and worked in Hamburg before returning to Markneukirchen around 1830.

Without question one of the most important figures in the history of German bow making, Hermann Richard Pfretzschner initially studied with his father Carl Richard before working for one year in the shop of J.B. Vuillaume in Paris, in 1874.
Johann Gottfried Pfretzschner was the son of Johann Elias Pfretzschner (I). He was admitted to the Markneukirchen guild of luthiers in 1751.

Johann Gottlob Pfretzschner created instruments with good workmanship on an elongated Stainer model. The high arch featured a characteristically Saxon head with an exaggerated tail behind the eye of the volute.
Richard Hermann Pfretzschner was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Hermann Richard Pfretzschner.
Edward Phillipson served as an Evangelical Minister at Aspatria from 1890 to 1925. He was a self-taught maker of violins and bows, utilizing Stradivari models.
Worked c. 1894. Label is branded to the back, with the date handwritten.
Gasparo Piattellini was the father or brother of Luigi Piatellini (I). He created fine work based on the Gabrielli/Amati model, featuring a medium arch and excellent red-brown varnish.
Luigi Piattellini was a pupil of G. B.

Armando Piccagliani produced 600 instruments in total, including violins, violas, cellos, and double basses. He exhibited a viola and cello at Cremona in 1937 and received awards at Genoa in 1922 and at Rome.
Handel (Andrew) Pickard established a music shop at 127 Kirk Gate, Leeds, using the name 'Handel' for business purposes. He was likely a pupil or assistant of Dearlove.
Active since 1880, he was awarded first prize at the Augsburg Exhibition in 1886. He was succeeded in business by O.
Marie Louis Piernot was a bow maker. He studied under C.

Claude Pierray established himself in the rue des Fossés, Saint-Germain-des-Prés from 1725, further identified as `proche la Comédie Français'. He was the most influential maker of the 'vie= Paris' school, working with a form very similar to that of Amati.
Jean Etienne Pierre created a highly arched original model that featured brown varnish. It was rarely encountered.

Antonín Pilar apprenticed to Benjamin Patocka in Jicin. He worked for Cermak in Kutna Hora (Kuttenberg) in 1899 and for Stowasser in Graz in 1900, then spent eight years in Berlin working with Möckel.
Apprenticed to his uncle Antonín Pilar (I), in Berlin. Worked for O.
Paul (Pál) Pilát studied under T. Zach in Vienna from 1872 to 1877.
Paul Ernst (Pál Ernö) Pilat was the son and pupil of Paul Pilat (I). He emigrated to the USA in 1921, initially working for Frederick Markert in New York before establishing his own workshop in 1932.

François Pillement was known as 'Tillement père', as some authorities believed that 'Jean François Pillement' was his son, although this appears not to be accurate; 'Jean' and 'Jean François' referred to the same person. He produced many fine violins, cellos, and double basses, which were branded and labelled variously as 'Mirecourt', 'Paris', and 'Cremona', yet all were made in Mirecourt.
Alphonse Pillyser created Stradivari and Guarneri copies. His work featured fine craftsmanship with rich red varnish.
Joseph Pineau was a pupil of A. Tolbecque starting in 1896.
Born in Buenos Aires Piñeiro began working on string instruments at the age of fourteen under Franco Ponzo. He later worked for Henry Viret in Argentina and Pierre Gaggini in Nice.
J. Pinel produced work characterized by narrow edges and a transparent varnish in shades of orange-brown or red.
Harold A. Pinney was a pupil of George Granger.

A contemporary and friend of Nicolas Lupot , François Pique was born in Roissy Ile de France, near Paris. It is unclear where he learned violin making, but by 1787 he had moved to Paris and set up his own workshop.
Ivo Pires was active in the USA from 1967. He was employed as a repairer by the Carl Fischer Co. in Boston.
Enrico Piretti was a maker of various types of bowed and plucked instruments. His workmanship was noted for its quality.
Claude Pirot established his workshop in the rue des Grands Augustins. He created fine Stradivari models, some featuring slightly raised arches; the best examples were comparable to those of Lupot.
Giovanni Pistucci was a pupil of V. Postiglione and was active from 1885.
Carlo Pizzamiglio trained as a cabinetmaker. He was a self-taught violin maker starting in 1932.
Mario Pizzolini was a pupil of G. Peluzzi.
Johann Franz Placht was the son and pupil of Franz Placht (I). He created similar work featuring flat arches, one-piece backs, and deep orange varnish.
Walter Plain apprenticed as a cabinet maker in Edinburgh. He possibly trained under his neighbor in Brunswick Street, Robert Ballantyne , whose work resembled his own.
Alberto Platner was presumed to have been of Tyrolean origin. He was first recorded with the Dominicus family in Rome, who were makers of plucked instruments.

Initially thought to have been a student of fellow Roman David Tecchler Domenico Michele Platner apprenticed with his father in Rome in the early 18th century before taking over the family workshop. Little is known about his working life, but he is known to have trained his nephew Giulio ...
Emil Pliverics worked as a pupil of A. Setzer in Budapest.
Charles Plumerel worked for Daniel in Marseilles. The relationship to Charles Plumerel (II) below remained unclear.
Jean Plumerel worked in Mirecourt, producing ordinary instruments with a plain yellow varnish. Violins and cellos are extant.
Jan Podesva was a houslar in Brno in the year 19.. He worked with Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Timofey Filippovich Podgorny was a self-taught maker and repairer. He established himself professionally in Krasnodar (formerly Yekaterinodar) from 1900.
Frantisek Podlaha created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. He worked in Prague in the year 19..

Probably the most renowned of Giuseppe Fiorini's students, Ansaldo Poggi owed his introduction to his teacher to the friendship between Fiorini and Poggi's father, an amateur violin maker. After learning the basics at home, Poggi moved to Zurich to train with Fiorini and then returned to Bologna several years later. ... />The impact of Fiorini's style is especially evident in Poggi's first independent work.
Dominique Poirson was a bow maker who trained in Paris with Nicolas Maire starting in 1865. He worked for J.
Elophe Poirson completed around 300 instruments (violins, violas, and cellos) between 1876 and 1918. His work was of good quality and featured his own varnish.
Louis Poirson modeled his work after Stradivarius. The work and varnish were of decent quality.
Enrico Politi was the son, pupil, and assistant of Eugenio Politi in Rome. After his father's death in 1909, he worked with his brother Fernando before relocating to Turin.
Eugenio Politi was a pupil of E. Ceruti.
Raul Politi was the son and pupil of Enrico Politi and also trained as a violinist. He created over 150 instruments based on the Stradivari model.
Edwin Pollard was active in Blackpool from 1862. He generally used the Stradivari model, although he made 12 violins on Stainer forms between 1910 and 1912.

One of the finest twentieth century Italian violin makers, Augusto Pollastri first worked as a messenger boy for Raffaele Fiorini and became a full-fledged apprentice around 1894. He quickly became one of the most skilled makers in the Fiorini workshop, and opened his own shop in the late 1890s.

Bolognese violin maker Gaetano Pollastri followed the models developed by his brother Augusto , with whom he worked from a young age. After Augusto died in 1927, Gaetano opened his workshop and began to sign his own instruments, inheriting a significant portion of his late brother's customers.
Anton Poller studied under J. Ringer in Luby and later worked in Markneukirchen, Mittenwald, Berlin, and other locations.
Michael Poller worked in the Klotz manner. The workmanship was generally over-sized with a medium arch.
It was thought to be of German origin. A fine viol resided in the Basle Museum.
Charles Jean Joseph Poncin was a violin and bow maker. He worked for the piano maker Erard in Paris before moving to Brussels in 1879.
English bowmaker of the Hill school who studied briefly with William Retford in the 1960-70s.

Until rather recently, the existing work of brilliant Marche violin maker Andrea Postacchini was thought to be by two different makers. After his father's death during Postacchini's infancy, he was sent to a religious order where he picked up violin making from a monk who made instruments in his spare time.
Vincenzo Postiglione was presumed to be the son and pupil of Vincenzo Postiglione (I). He apprenticed to Vincenzo Jorio from 1847 to 1852 and established himself independently in Naples in 1855.
Johannes Pot was a carpenter and self-taught maker. He returned to Amersfoort, Netherlands in 1931 after working several years in Indonesia.

Joseph Pouillé created a large Stradivari model with golden-orange or yellow varnish. It was repaired in Lille by Pouillé, luthier, on rue Basse in 1879, after being worked on by the same luthier on rue Basse in 1878.

Hans Poulsen apprenticed in Germany, gaining experience with the Schuster and Breinl firms before establishing himself in Copenhagen around 1885. He was a prolific maker of instruments known for their consistent quality, using good materials and soft oil varnish.
R. Poulton was described by Henley as a little known English maker.
Herman Pourtois was a doctor of medicine and an amateur violin maker starting in 1934. He studied under A.
Émile Pouzol is active in Avignon and in Rome before he emigrates to the USA around 1930.
Clark Powers trained in England, Germany, and Austria before settling in Boston, Massachusetts, in 1888. His personal model featured oil and spirit varnishes.

Eugenio Praga studied with Nicolò Bianchi from 1869 and soon began making instruments for himself in parallel to working with Bianchi. When Bianchi moved to Nice in 1878, Praga took over his workshop.
Gustav Adolf Prager was a bow maker who was later assisted by his son Hans.
Oskar Gustav Prager worked for A. Brückner and C.
Ronald William Praill was an amateur maker. He created a large personal model, as well as patterns based on Stradivari and Pressenda.
Hermann Wilhelm Prell was a bow maker. He was a pupil of A.
Julius Hermann Prell studied under his brother-in-law C. A.
Ronald Prentice was a professional double bass player. He trained in violin making at Mittenwald under K.
Active instrument maker from 1809 whilst working as a farmer. Possibly a pupil of Benjamin Willard 1805-1810.

Considered the father and perhaps finest maker of the modern Turin school, Pressenda was generally assumed to have been a student of Storioni. A more thorough comparison of their instruments, however, does not support that theory, and it is more likely that he was trained in Turin in the 1820s by French makers before opening his own shop.
John Preston cited in the Hill Archive. His work is presently unknown.
John Priestnall was active in Rochdale from 1840. He was credited with 300 violins, 30 violas, six cellos, and eight double basses.
Bretislav Prihonsky apprenticed to Cermak at Kutná Hora. He established himself at Vrsovice in 1925.

Established in 1723 at 'the sign of the Musical Instrument Maker in the Side, Newcastle-upon-Tyne', early labels spelled his name as `Pryor', but all records and labels after around 1720 referred to him as 'Prior'. He was recorded as the Assay Master in the city and was also a wood turner and maker of false teeth.
Enrico Prochet-Didero was an amateur maker and a pupil of E. Guerra.
Ladislav Prokop was the son, pupil, and successor of Franz Dominik Prokop. He established himself in Chrudim from 1876 to 1925.
Ladislav F. Prokop specialized in the professional production of high-arched personal models using quality materials.
Dante Pucci was a guitarist and luthier. He crafted bowed and plucked instruments of notable finesse.
E. F.
Fritz Pulpaneck trained in Berlin and was active in Leipzig and Dusseldorf before emigrating to the USA in 1904. He worked in New York prior to establishing himself in Los Angeles in 1911.

Francesco Puppati was a lawyer, violinist, and amateur maker. He claimed to have re-discovered the secret of Stradivari like many others, although his work was ordinary and loosely based on Stradivari patterns.

François Marie Pupunat was a cabinet maker and a self-taught maker of violins and bows, active in Lausanne from 1836. His work was of good competence, resulting in the completion of 300 violins, 25 cellos, and 20 small violas.
T. E.
Joseph Puskas studied under Paulus Pilat from 1938 to 1941. He established himself in New York before relocating to Los Angeles in 1947.

George Pyne was a violinist and self-taught maker. He worked for Edward Withers for 20 years and was credited with approximately 500 violins and violas based on classical Cremonese models, some of which utilized imported parts.

Following a tumultuous childhood in China where he learned to play the violin, Zheng Quan worked at the Beijing Music Research Institute under the tutelage of the violin maker Dai Hongxiang, before completing the violin-making course in Cremona in 1983. In 1990 one of his violins won the gold medal at the Tchaikovsky violin competition in Moscow, and in 1991, another one of his violins ... won first prize for sound at the Stradivarius competition in Cremona.
Charles Quenoil trained with V. Audinot and worked for C.
René Quenoil was the son and pupil of Victor Quenoil . He also studied under A.
Victor Quenoil was the brother of Charles Quenoil and a pupil of Deblaye. He joined his brother Charles in Paris from 1907 to 1914.
Johann (János) Racsinczky was a pupil of B. Varju.
Loránd Rácz worked in The Hague for J. Vedral and L.
Johann Radeck created fine workmanship on the Stainer model. The arch was of medium height with narrow edges.
Otello Radighieri was a self-taught maker, guided by G. Guicciardi.
Alessandro Radrizzani studied under Rossi and Degani in Venice. He worked for Bisiach.
Angelo Radrizzani was the brother of Alessandro Radrizzani . He was a pupil of Bisiach and was assisted by his son Carlo.
Carlo Radrizzani was the son and pupil of Angelo Radrizzani and the uncle of Alessandro. He focused mostly on restoration and classical models.

John Rae, a Scottish violin maker and the son and grandson of bagpipe makers, worked in London from approximately 1875 to 1920. He derived personal models and completed over 130 instruments, including violas and cellos, which featured internal inscriptions.
George R. Raeburn was the brother of Alexander Raeburn (I).
John Raeburn was the eldest brother of Alexander and George Raeburn. He worked as a miner, was a violinist, and made violins part-time.
Claude Victor Rambaux apprenticed to Moitessier in Mirecourt from 1820 to 1824. He worked with Thibout in Caen starting in 1824 and with Francois Gand in Paris from 1827.
Manuel Ramirez was a celebrated guitar maker. He constructed violins and guitars and served as a luthier at the Real Conservatorio in Arlaban No. 10, Madrid.
William Ramsay created a small production Stradivari model that featured golden oil varnish in 1899. He was based in Biggar.
Haim Rapoport studied in St Petersburg under A. A.
Born in Russia, Haim Rapoport studied violin making with Shvalm Wishnegorsky and Andre Chardon before settling in Tel Aviv.
Anders Rasmussen was a pupil of F. W.
Ludovico Rastelli modeled according to Henley. No other source exists.
August Rau was one of the finest bow makers of the German school. He worked for W.
Sebastian Rauch produced a Stainer model. The varnish was thin and golden-yellow or pale brown.
Thomas Rauch was the son of Sebastian Rauch I and is believed to have been a pupil of Thomas Edlinger. He moved from Prague to Wroclaw around 1737.

After graduating from the Cremona International School of Violin Making in 1985, in the class of Maestro Giorgio Scolari, Frank Ravatin was first head of restoration and making in Jacques Camurat's workshop in Paris. In 1991, he opened his own shop in Le Mans and devoted himself more and more to making new instruments.
Carlo Ravizza was a pupil of Antoniazzi. He produced violins, violas, and cellos in the fine Antoniazzi style, featuring a beautiful golden-red varnish.

German-born Jacob Rayman was one of the first violin makers in Britain, and his violin from 1641 is probably the oldest surviving English violin. He was trained in the Bavarian violin making center of Füssen before moving to London around 1625, and his workmanship reflects that training rather than the methods of the English viol-making tradition.
Robert John Raymond credited with 37 violins, 39 violas, and five cellos.
Rudolph Raymond described violins of gong pattern with small soundholes, deeply sunk edges, and a scroll. The work featured good materials and yellow varnish.
Nicola Reale was a self-taught violin maker and repairer, active in Washington from 1909.
Dudley B. Reed was a part-time violin maker active from 1922.
Joseph Reed created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. The oil varnish ranged from light to dark orange-brown.
Bologna maker. Studied with Otello Bignami.
Dante Paolo Regazzoni was a pupil of G. and L. Bisiach in Milan.
August Anton Reichel was the son of Friedrich August Reichel (II) and a maker of violins and bows. He apprenticed with his uncle W.
"Bassist and bow maker at Guarneri House in Hudsonville MI".

Knute Reindahl was born November 16, 1857 in Telemark, Norway, where fiddle making had long been a tradition in his mother's family. Beginning with Knute's great-grandfather, Olav Gullbekk, ca.1760 - 1836, several generations had produced makers of both Norwegian Hardanger fiddles and classic Italian instruments.
Anton Reinhard manufactured commercially good quality instruments in various grades. Models included those of Amati and Stradivari.
Johann Reiter was a violin and bow maker, the son, pupil, and successor of Johann Baptist Reiter . He established his workshop in Oberer Markt, Mittenwald.
Michael (Mihály) Reményi apprenticed to E. Bartek in 1881 and later with G.
Jean Mathurin Remy was the son, pupil, and successor of Mathurin-François. He established himself at 30 rue de Grenelle-Saint-Honoré in 1817.
Jules Remy was the son and pupil of Jean Mathurin Remy . He established himself in Passage Brady in 1854 and at 60 Faubourg Saint-Denis from 1872.
Established at rue des Bouchers, Paris from 1773. Moved c. 1774 to rue Saint-Honoré, at the sign 'Aux Amateurs'.
Walter Gerhard Renz was a bow maker. He apprenticed with his uncle M.
Luigi Repetti completed 60 violins based on his personal model of small proportions. He was later assisted by his son Giuseppe.
Charles Résuche established a workshop in Lyons in 1890, in the passage de l'Arque, in partnership with Justin Diter. He later moved to rue de l'Hotel de Ville.
Probably the greatest bow craftsman of his time, William Charles Retford was initially hired by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons as an instrument repairer.
William Richard Retford was a bow maker, the son of William Charles Retford. He studied under his father at W.
Raoul Rettberg was a violin and bow maker. He moved to New York in 1921, where he worked with Martin Lange to develop their 'Gala' instruments.
Alexander Ricard was active in Springfield from 1917. He created Cremonese and personal models, using his own oil varnish with yellow, brown, and red coloration.

Frederick George Richards was an architectural wood carver and amateur violin maker active from 1935 in Eastbourne, Tunbridge Wells, and Torrington. He completed 30 violins and 10 violas based on Stradivari models.
Arthur Richardson took up violin making around 1915, having worked initially as a woodcarver and pattern-maker. He was largely self-taught.

Frank Richardson completed 220 instruments, utilizing a varnish that was shaded red-brown and made from local materials. He worked in Canton, Maine, and in Caribu, and included a manuscript label in his instruments.
André Richaume worked with his uncle Victor Fetique from 1927. He established himself independently in 1932 on the boulevard de Clichy.
Antoine Marius Richelme worked as a pupil of Yong in Marseille in 1850. He subsequently collaborated with Luppi-Coviaux, manufacturers of tailpieces and chinrests, among other items.
Malcolm F. Richmond was a pupil of Smillie and created fine classical models inspired by Cremonese designs, exhibiting a style very much like Mirecourt, possibly utilizing imported parts.
August Riechers was a violin and bow maker. He was the son of Reichers from Hanover and was recognized as one of the finest German makers of the 19th century.
Joseph Riedel was described by Henley as typical Bohemian work with yellow-brown varnish. Its current status is unknown.
Bow maker. Son and pupil of Karl Riedl.
Dominicus Rief was the son and pupil of Matthaus Rief, below. He was generally recognized as the best maker of the family.
Georg Rieger created an Amatisé model with dark brown varnish. The work was decent, while double basses received more appreciation.
Joseph Rieger was the son and pupil of Philipp Jacob Rieger. The style was slightly Italianised Tyrolean, though not highly finished.
Archibald Ritchie was the best maker of the Dundee school. He was a part-time self-taught maker who was professionally employed at Dundee harbour.
William J. Ritchie created heavily built violins that featured thick varnish.
Hermann Ritter designed the five-stringed Viola Alta, which was made by K. A.
London firm, originally named Hawkes & Son, founded in 1865 by William Henry Hawkes. In 1930 it merged with Boosey & Company to become Boosey & Hawkes.
Giacomo Rivolta worked at the sign of 'Santo Re Davide' in Contrada Santa Margherita. He derived models from Gagliano and Stradivari.
A. J.
Studied in Cremona with Pietro Sgarabotto, Gio Batta Morassi, Francesco Bissolotti and Pier Luigi Galetti. Returned to New York City in 1975.
John Robertson worked in Dalmeny before later moving to Broxburn. He was awarded medals at exhibitions in Edinburgh and Glasgow.
Alfred George Robinson accurately made a Stradivari model. It featured fine varnish and received a Diploma of Honour at The Hague in 1949.
Robert Robinson was a violin and bow maker and a pupil of his father, R. C.
William Robinson made a personal model derived from Stradivari and Guarneri patterns. He was an amateur maker, not associated with William Joseph of Plumstead.
Enrico Rocca was born in Turin and moved with his family to Genoa as a young child. He studied with his father Giuseppe , who died in 1865 when Enrico was just 17.

Born in Barbaresco, Giuseppe Antonio (or Joseph) Rocca moved to Turin in 1834 to work in the shop of Giovanni Francesco Pressenda . After three years, he opened his own shop, and although his work ranks among the best of 18th century Italian violin making, his life was marked by financial struggles, illness and grief. ... />Rocca initially followed the work of his teacher, albeit with stylistic aberrations that suggest a budding interest in Cremonese models.

According to Sesto Rocchi's own account, his interest in violin making stems from an early childhood crash course in repair: after dropping his violin, he attempted to fix it without arousing the suspicion of his parents. Some years later Rocchi entered the School of Violin Making in Parma, where he studied with Gaetano Sgarabotto . ...
P. Roccus owned a cello that Vannes noted was in the possession of M.

David Bailey Rockwell was a violin and bow maker. He was active in Boston from 1878 to 1895, in Providence in 1901, in Detroit in 1908, in Philadelphia in 1915, in Hartford, Connecticut in 1920, and finally in New York in 1923.
Harry Victor Rockwell was the son and pupil of Joseph H. Rockwell .

Giovanni Battista Rogeri settled in Brescia around 1675 after working as an apprentice of Nicolò Amati in Cremona from 1661. After the plague devastated northern Italy in the mid-17th century and Amati died in 1684, Rogeri was one of the few active violin makers in the region.

The son of Giovanni Battista Rogeri , Pietro Giacomo Rogeri began to assist his father around 1690, though his earliest known instruments date from 1705. Rogeri usually spelled his name in a Latinized form 'Ruggerius' leading to speculation about a connection between the Brescian Rogeri and Cremonese Rugeri families, but their ... work shows few similarities.
French bow maker trained in Mirecourt by Bernard Ouchard. Worked in Paris then the Island of Bréhat.

Pieter Rombouts almost certainly began to assist his stepfather Hendrik Jacobs early in life, and the changes in Jacobs' output from about 1685 on supports that assumption. Though his craftsmanship is somewhat heavier than that of Jacobs and the heads are less delicate, Rombouts' skill in the channeling of edgework is unmistakable. ...
Adolf Romer was a musician and luthier who became the luthier for the Freiburg court in 1899. He received several exhibition medals and created copies of Stradivari and Guarneri.
Raffaele Ronchini was a violin and bow maker. He used a Stradivari-derived model and applied a coarse finish with yellow-brown varnish.
Pupil of Joseph Scott . Employed by him as a farm labourer and instrument maker at his farm Haltcliff, Eskett, near Caldbeck 1795-180o.
Alfred James Rope was a self-taught violin maker from 1890, while still serving as a soldier in Aldershot Barracks. He resided in Woolwich in 1927.
Walter S. Ropes was a pupil and assistant of J.
Giovanni Rosadoni was active in Pavia, Como, and Milan. His models featured Stradivari designs with golden-yellow varnish.
The work was of decent professional quality and featured a brown varnish. It included a manuscript label that imitated print.
Christian Heinrich Wilhelm Röscher was a cabinet maker and amateur maker. He used good materials and workmanship in his Stradivari model.
