John Rose, son and pupil of the viol maker John Rose I, continued to work in his father's Bridewell Palace workshop after the elder John's death until 1607, when he was given notice for unpaid rent. Dating of all instruments attributed to 'John Rose' is tentative, and it is likely that the younger Rose was responsible for most surviving work.
Samuel 'Luthier' Rosenthal was a violin and bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Joseph Rosenthal starting in 1901.
Domenico Rossi made copies of individual classical Italian violins in Piacenza in the year 1903.
Emilio Rossi was the son and pupil of Domenico Rossi and focused on guitars and cellos. A cellist himself, he created a personal model featuring golden-red varnish.
Giovanni Rossi established himself independently in 1819. He moved to Pavia in 1847.
Giuseppe Rossi lived in Citadella, Padua, and studied at the Venice Academy of Fine Arts. He subsequently moved to Rome, where he developed an interest in violin making.
Stelio Rossi studied under I. Sderci in Florence.

Franz Georg Rost apprenticed to August Voigt in Markneukirchen from 1883 to 1886. He worked for Paulus in Leipzig, Hammig in Dresden, Louis Otto in Düsseldorf, Laumann in Budapest, and Zimmermann in Leipzig.

A descendant of Nicolò Amati's wife, Giovanni Rota became part of Lorenzo Storioni's workshop and household in 1787. He worked for Storioni for the next five years, and almost certainly got to know Nicola Bergonzi , since Bergonzi also lived there from ... 1790 to 1795.

One of the most prolific of the Markneukirchen violin workshops, the firm of Ernst Heinrich Roth produced an enormous number of consistently high-quality instruments in the 20th century. Roth, son of a violin maker, started his firm in 1902.
François Roudhloff was a pupil of Breton. He established himself in Paris in partnership with Mauchand.
Maurice Rougier initially resided in rue Chevalier Paul. He moved to rue Grinelle, Toulon, in 1942.

Lodewich Willem Joannes Roumen was active in Gröningen around 1802, in Kampen around 1804, and in Utrecht in 1810. He established himself in Amsterdam in 1825 at 159 Kalverstraat and retired to Kampen in 1843, leaving the business to his son Joannes.
Luigi Rovatti established himself in Buenos Aires in 1885. He was a prolific maker, having created 1, 000 instruments, including violins, violas, cellos, and guitars.
Azzo Rovescalli worked as a pupil of R. Antoniazzi and was employed in the Monzino shop located in Milan.

Manilo Rovescalli, son and pupil of Azzo Rovescalli , was born in 19.. and made violins with a small pattern and dark red varnish, as well as violas and guitars. He worked in various cities, including Cremona and Milan.
Tullio Rovescalli was the son and pupil of Azzo Rovescalli . He specialized in making violins in a small pattern with dark red varnish, as well as violas and guitars.
Frederick Rowe was active from 1909. He established himself professionally in Minneapolis in 1920.
John Rowe was a violinist and teacher, as well as an amateur violin maker. He completed 10-12 violins based on the Stradivari model, showcasing good workmanship.
Arthur John Rowley was a violinist and luthier. He created over 100 violins based on classical Italian and personal models.
Andrew Roy established himself as a clock and violin maker in Allhallowgate. The model resembled that of Testore.
Self-taught British maker who worked in Cambridge. Made guitars and period instruments as well as violins, violas and cellos.
Rigart Rubus created characteristic Russian school violins that featured rounded edges without overhang. The soundholes resembled those of Stradivari.
Joseph Ruddiman was the most prolific maker of the Aberdeen School. He was probably a pupil of R.
Johann Michael Rudert established himself in Korneuburg. He moved to Vienna in 1813.

Carlo Rugeri was the youngest son of Francesco Rugeri and was trained by his father in the family workshop. Upon Francesco's death in 1698, Carlo inherited his father's tools, likely because he was the only son who remained in the workshop.

Francesco Rugeri has traditionally been considered a student of Nicolò Amati , although there is no documentary evidence that he was present in the Amati workshop. The two families certainly had close a social relationship, with Amati acting as godfather to one of Rugeri's sons.

Giacinto Rugeri was the son, pupil, and assistant of Francesco Rugeri, and he predeceased his father. Very few authenticated works are known to exist today, with almost no instruments surviving that bear his own label, 'Giacinto filio di Francesco / Ruggieri detto it per 16..'.

Giovanni Battista Rugeri was the son, pupil, and assistant of Francesco Rugeri. Very few authenticated instruments bore his label, but those that did were sophisticated and beautifully crafted in the Rugeri tradition, with some featuring a deeper red varnish than typically associated with Francesco.
Vincenzo Rugeri was born in 1663 just outside the walls of Cremona. He worked in his father Francesco's shop alongside his brothers Giovanni Battista, Giacinto and Carlo.

George Ruoff created one alto viola known for its distinctive model and fine craftsmanship. It featured a medium high arch and very large open soundholes with short wings, which were set slanting across the front.
Wilhelm Ruprecht established at 102 Landstrasse, moving to 767 Weiden in 1855. He used Brescian and Guarneri models.
Karl Rutz worked as a pupil of F. Sprenger in St.
Josef Vojtech Ruzicka studied in Prague. He worked in Opava.
Josef Antonin Čermak was a pupil of Benjamin Patocky. He worked in Kutná Hora from 1898 to 1910.
Benigno Saccani varnished and labelled mass-produced Czech and German instruments. He exhibited at the Brussels Exhibition in 1910.

Simone Fernando Sacconi became an assistant in the workshop of Giuseppe Rossi while he was still a schoolboy, and had attracted his own clients by the age of 16. His tremendous skills as a copyist, particularly of Stradivari and Guarneri , ... were evident from a very young age.

Paul Sadka apprenticed with bow maker Noel Burke in Ireland, and continued his training in Cremona, Italy, later working in the south of France, Paris and Budapest. In 2009, Sadka returned to England to set up his workshop in his hometown of Cornwall, and afterwards settled in Normandy in France.
Pierre Saint Paul worked in the rue de la Comedie Francaise until 1741. He later operated in the rue Saint Andre des Arts at the sign 'A la lyre d'Apollon'.
George Saint-George was a violinist and viola d'amore player. He was an early pioneer in researching historical musical instruments.
Award winning contemporary bow maker, restorer and expert based in New York. Grandson of William Salchow.
Pioneering American who studied bow making in Mirecourt with Georges Barjonnet.
Jean Baptiste Deshayes Salomon established himself in Paris on the rue de Grenelle Saint Honoré at the sign 'Sainte-Cécile'. He married Barbe Deshaies, the widow of J.
Dominique Salzard created a large Stradivari model using good materials and applied a yellow-brown varnish. The instrument was branded with 'D.
François Salzard was assumed to be the brother of Dominique Salzard . He created similar work based on a large Mirecourt/Stradivari model.
Leone Sanavia primarily made plucked instruments. One violin, dated 1946, was recorded.
Joseph Sandherr was the son of Adolf Sandherr and a pupil of Fiorini in Munich. He worked independently in Griinestrasse, Dresden from around 1918.
Alois Sandner made fittings and parts of violins for wholesale manufacture and sale.
Anton Sandner studied under K. Fischer in Luby.
Carl Sandner was probably the son of Johann Sandner (I). He established himself in Fruhmessgasse, Luby, as a guitar maker.
Max Sandner was a pupil of U. Wourlitzer and later worked for Berger in Erlbach and Glasel in Markneukirchen.
Wenzel Sandner worked for J. W.

Neapolitan maker who moved to Rome later in his career. Believed to be largely self-taught, but early instruments show stylistic similarities with the work of Giovanni Tedesco, suggesting that he may have been the pupil of Tedesco.
Giovanni Battista Sanoni created a high-arched model featuring a well-carved scroll with a good texture orange varnish. It was rarely encountered.
Gaetano Santagiuliana was probably a pupil of Giorgio Serafin . He was a maker of guitars and violins, especially well-regarded for his double basses.
Giacinto Santagiuliana was the son and pupil of Gaetano Santagiuliana . He generally used Stradivari models with a flat arch that were not highly refined.
Giuseppe Saretta was a sculptor and a pupil of Carlo Oddone. He established himself in Montebelluna and briefly worked in Padua and Treviso.

Probably the finest bow maker of the 20th century, Eugène Sartory studied with his father in Mirecourt and went on to work for Charles Peccatte and Alfred Lamy in Paris. In 1889 Sartory set up his own workshop at the age of 18, becoming completely self-sufficient ... as a maker by 1910.

Mitsuaki Sasano is a Japanese bowmaker who trained with Rudolf Neudorfer in Germany and Stéphane Thomachot in France. Having initially set up a workshop in Paris in 2001, he then decided to settle on the French Riviera in Nice.
David E. Saunders studied under Goerge Orth in Seattle in 1948.
Stephen Saunders was a cello and double bass player and an amateur luthier. He gained access to the Stradivari owned by Dr.
Self-taught maker, after apprenticeship as a joiner. Followed Heron-Allen's book Violin Making as it was and is.
Edmond Saunier worked for D. F.

Giuseppe Scarampella was the son of Paolo Scarampella and a pupil of Nicola Bianchi in Paris. In 1866, he worked for Luigi Castellini in Florence, later succeeding him as the curator of the Cherubini Museum.

Younger brother of Giuseppe Scarampella , Stefano Scarampella was born in Brescia in 1843 and later worked in Mantua. A carpenter by trade, he only took up violin making seriously around 1890, and his fully mature body of work dates from about 1902 to 1915.
Arthur Scarbrow worked first in the case department of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons before switching to bow making.
Max Schaffner was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of C.
Oswald Schaller was the son and pupil of Friedrich Wilhelm Schaller. He worked for Louis Otto in Düsseldorf.
Karl Schau was a civil engineer and an amateur maker. He created the work in Schwechat in 19.
Robert Schenk created a very original personal model. The varnish was of poor quality and brown.
Ernst Schetelig was a pupil of A. Paulus.
Johann Scheverle was born in Augsburg, Germany. His name was rendered in various ways: Schaferle, Schefferle, Schwertle, and Schewerle.
Carlo Schiavi was a pupil of L. Digiuni and F.
Oswald Anton Schilbach was a pupil of E. W.
Emil Schiller worked in London at Castle Street and Soho Square until 1910. He settled in Bad Elster, Germany in 1930.
Johann Georg Schlosser was the son and pupil of Johann Christian Schlosser . His instruments were in a similar style.
Arnold Otto Schmidt was the son and pupil of Anton Otto. He worked for E.
Contemporary bow maker in Dresden, Germany. Father of Daniel Schmidt.
Carl Franz Schmidt was the son of Franz Schmidt and received diplomas at the Vienna Exhibition of 1873. He created copies of classical Cremonese and Brescian models.
Ernst Albin Schmidt was a pupil of A. A.
Ernst Reinhold Schmidt apprenticed to J. Kretzschman from 1871 to 1874.
Johann Martin Schmidt was likely the son of Carl Schmidt. He was born in Pressburg in 1805.
Kurt Schmidt was a violin and bow maker, the son and pupil of Christian Paul Schmidt. He worked for R.
Max Willibald Schmidt trained in Markneukirchen, Germany. He emigrated to the USA in 1913 and worked in San Francisco and Seattle.
Paul Gerhard Schmidt studied under G. Jiirgens in Hamburg and A.
Lucien Schmitt studied the violin at the Geneva conservatoire. He trained for two years as a luthier in Mirecourt with Mougenot and studied bow making with Bazin.
Arthur L. Scholes worked from c. 1890 to 1914 in Rushden, Northamptonshire, before moving to Bedford.
Herbert Emil Schönfelder worked as a brother and pupil of Emil Karl Schönfelder. He was employed by H.
Johann Adam Schönfelder was the son of Johann Schönfelder and became a master of the violin makers guild in 1729. He was regarded as the finest maker in his family.
Most dictionaries noted some training in Italy, but this could not be determined and seemed unlikely, based on reports of a Latinised version of his label.
Johann Paul Schorn is a musician and luthier active in Innsbruck from 1654 to 1684. He is appointed to the service of the Archbishop of Stuttgart in 1684.
Canadian maker. Studied violin making with his uncle Fernand Schryer in Quebec, then took up an apprenticeship at Geo.
Paul Schubert was a bow maker and the son and pupil of August Schubert. His work was of fine quality.
Johann Heinrich Schult was a pupil of O. Schünemann and established his workshop in Königstrasse, Lübeck in 1896.
Henry F. Schultz was a pupil of Clark Powers from 1886 to 1891.
Otto Schünemann became a luthier under the influence of Dr. Wirsing of Prague and was mainly self-taught in Hamburg.
Arno Schünzel trained in Mittenwald. He subsequently worked for G.
Schuster was a family of luthiers based in Markneukirchen, Germany, and Luby (Schönbach), Czech Republic. They operated in both locations.
Adolf Curt Schuster was a bow maker who worked in Markneukirchen around 1920. He studied under Pfretzschner and served as an assistant to A.
Carl Gottlob Schuster created a large Stradivari model with medium arching and golden-brown varnish. One example is located in the Bach Museum in Eisenach.

Edouard Schuster established his business in Brussels in 1910, initially located in the Galerie de Commerce (Passage Hirsch). In 1925, he moved to boulevard Emile-Jacquemain, and later to place de La Reine.
Kurt Schuster studied under Glässel and Fiorini in Munich. He established himself independently in Leipzig on Lampestrasse from 1908.
Max Schuster worked for H. Gütter in Breslau, C.
Rudolph Schuster was a pupil of J. Pötzl and worked in Lemberg, Hamburg for Winterling, Stuttgart for E.
Anton Schwartz created a Stainer model with a high arch and yellow-brown varnish. It was quite an undistinguished work.

Bernhard Schwartz established himself as a musician and luthier in Strasbourg around 1764. He became a member of the city guild in 1780 and was recorded in archives as a 'luthier' in 1796 at 69 Finkwillerstrasse.
Georges Frédéric Schwartz was a bow maker and the son and pupil of Bernard Schwartz. He specialized in bow making from around 1810, creating pieces in the style of Tourte.

Theophil Wilhelm Schwartz was the younger son, pupil, and successor of Bernhard Schwartz , and he worked alongside his brother Georg Frédéric Schwartz, contributing to the instrument production labeled as "Fréres Schwartz." Throughout his career, Theophil primarily produced violins, violas, and cellos, characterized by a ... very clean style in the French manner, based on the Stradivari model. His instruments featured full low arching and a golden red-brown varnish.
Giovanni Schwarz apprenticed to his uncle E. Degani until 1888, when he established himself in Venice.
Rodolfo Schwarz was the son and pupil of Giovanni Schwarz . Active from 1920, he established himself independently in 1928.
Johann Baptist Schweitzer was a pupil of F. Geissenhof in Vienna.

Giuseppe Scialé recorded in 1842 at 91 Via del Corso as a maker of violins, guitars, wind instruments, and harps. The only authenticated works that exist are guitars, although Henley cited copies of violins by Gaetano Guadagnini.
Farmer and part-time maker. Active in Haltcliff Farm, Eskett (formerly Heskett New Market), parish of Caldbeck.
Renato Scrollavezza graduated from the Cremona School of Violin Making. He established himself in Parma and became one of the senior figures in 20th century Italian lutherie.
Igino Sderci trained as a wood carver and was initially self-taught as a violin maker before becoming a pupil of L. Bisiach in Sienna during World War I.
Luciano Sderci was the son and pupil of Igino Sderci and created work similar to that of his father. He exhibited in Cremona in 1949 and received medals in Rome in 1954.
George Seaver credited with 92 instruments. The model was Guarneri and featured red-brown varnish.
Lájos Sebok was a pupil of Braun in Szeged and worked for Remenyi in Budapest as well as Babos in Szeged. He established himself in Orosháza in 1913 and produced good professional work.

maker_name: Seidel, Adolf K. F. W. B.
info: Adolf K. F.
Christian Wilhelm Seidel was the son of Johann Gottlob Seidel. He became a member of the violin makers guild in 1838.
Otto Seifert worked as a pupil of A. Bruckner.
F. A.

Carl Sennewald was an electrical engineer and a self-taught violin maker. He subsequently attended the Markneukirchen Violin-Making School and was active in Munich in 1905, Rudolstadt in 1907, Berlin in 1911, and Cologne from 1925.

Fabrizio Senta was known primarily for a viola da braccio located in the Claudius Museum, Copenhagen, which had a manuscript label dated 1716. He is recognized as one of the earliest makers to have made violins in Turin, although both Vannes and Henley claimed he worked there without providing supporting evidence.
Giorgio Serafin was the nephew and pupil of Santo Serafin. He worked for Montagnana and took over the business in 1751, marrying his daughter Antonia.

Santo Serafin is thought to have moved to Venice from his hometown of Udine around 1720, although his earliest labels date from 1725. It seems he set up his own workshop in 1733, so it is likely that he worked for other Venetian makers in the interim and developed his individual approach.
Paul Serdet apprenticed to Gaillard and J. Derazay.
Joseph (Giuseppe) Settin was an active luthier from 1904. He studied sculpture at the Academy of Fine Arts in Milan.

Originally self-taught, Gaetano Sgarabotto moved to Milan in 1901 to work with Riccardo Antoniazzi and later Leandro Bisiach . He returned to Vincenza in 1905 and moved to Parma in 1926, where he eventually ended up for good in 1957 after periods in Vincenza ... and Brescia.

Pietro Sgarabotto was the son and pupil of Gaetano Sgarabotto, working as a violinist and maker in Vicenza in 1920 and in Parma in 1930. He taught at the Parma Violin Making School from 1928 to 1932 and alongside his father from 1929 to 1936, later teaching at the Cremona School from 1959 to 1973.

Antonio Sgarbi was the son and pupil of Giuseppe Giuseppe Sgarbi . The family moved to Rome in 1878, where Antonio worked in his father's workshop until 1905, when he became luthier to the Bellini Conservatoire in Palermo, a position he held until 1911.
Giuseppe Sgarbi was known as `Jarino'. He was active in Finale Emilia from 1841 to 1890, later moving to Modena and finally to Rome.
John Henry Shal was a pupil of Frank Markusic in 1963. He completed 92 violins, seven violas, and one cello, using Stradivari and Guarneri models along with various commercial varnishes.
Elbridge G. Shaw worked in a workshop on Pleasant Street, Worcester.
Ian Shepherd was employed in the bow department of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons until 1976.

Most sources reported small violins featuring good golden brown varnish; their current status is unknown. One viola, illustrated in Hamma (Italian Violin makers), displayed exaggeratedly bulging archings, Germanic soundholes, and a grotesque scroll, indicating it was likely a later 18th century work.
Leading American bow maker and restorer based in Port Townsend, Washington. Worked for Hans Weisshaar then established own shop in 1983.
Ettore Siega was a pupil of E. Degani and G.
Iginio Siega was the son and pupil of Ettore Siega . He produced work similar to that of his father.
Born near Nancy, violin maker Hippolyte Silvestre moved to Paris around the late 1820s to work for J.B. Vuillaume .

Hippolyte Chrétien Silvestre was the nephew, pupil, and successor of Pierre and Hippolyte Silvestre in Lyons, and the son of Pierre and Hippolyte's sister. Initially trained in Mirecourt, he joined his uncles and took over the business in 1865.
Pierre Silvestre apprenticed locally in Mirecourt before moving to Paris to work for Nicolas Lupot and Gand père . In 1829 he left the Gand workshop to start his own business in Lyon.
Pierre Silvestre apprenticed locally in Mirecourt before moving to Paris to work for Nicolas Lupot and Gand père . In 1829 he left the Gand workshop to start his own business ... in Lyon.
Elek P. Simon was an amateur maker.
Franz Simon made a citizen of Salzburg in 1791 and was appointed luthier to the court. His work featured a Klotz model with narrow, outwardly projecting corners.

Nicolas Simon was a fine bow maker from Mirecourt, associated with the Pecatte school, known for strong heads, sticks of rounded triangular section, and frogs with an open throat. He established his business under his own name around 1840.
Nicolas Simon was a bow maker, the son of Jean Simon and the nephew of Nicolas Simon "FR". He worked in the Mirecourt workshop of his uncle.

Pierre Simon apprenticed and worked in his hometown of Mirecourt until 1838, when he moved to Paris to join the workshop of Dominique Peccatte . The Peccatte influence is quite strong in Simon's work, but in 1840 he decamped for the Vuillaume workshop, ... where he stayed until 1844 and produced many fine bows, some of the "picture" variety.
Amadeo Simonazzi was a pupil of S. Scarampella.
Charles Simonin apprenticed and assisted J. B.
Francois Simonin was born and trained in Mirecourt. He emigrated to the USA and was employed as the head of Eastman's violin repair department.
Nicolas Simoutre apprenticed to Lupot in Paris. He established himself in Mirecourt in 1817.
Nicolas-Eugéne Simoutre worked as the son and pupil of Nicholas Simoutre. He was employed by Darche in Paris in 1852 and by Roth and Schwartz in Strasbourg from 1856 to 1860.
Worked in association with John Simpson , below. Presumed to be his brother.
An important business in the London musical trade in the ifith century. Presumed to be a merchant rather than a maker himself.
Robert Simpson was an amateur maker who used commercial varnish produced by Angell of Bristol.
Thomas Simpson was a piano repairer in Walsall until 1898. Thereafter, he became a self-taught maker of violins and bows in Peterborough.

William Sinclair was an amateur maker known for a Guarneri model, as well as a unique design featuring sympathetic strings and another with double courses resembling a mandolin. His work was inconsistent and unrefined, utilizing spirit varnish.
Frank Sindelar was a pupil of John Hornsteiner in Chicago. He established himself independently there in 1917.
Axel Albert Sington studied under E. Lindholm and E.
Jules Sirgent worked as a pupil of E. Bourlier.
Ambrogio Sironi was a pupil of R. Antoniazzi, who held him in high regard.
John Walter Skinner apprenticed as a cabinet maker. He worked as a silversmith and became an amateur violin maker in retirement starting in 1908.
Alexander Smillie was active from 1887. He was primarily self-taught but appeared to have received assistance from George Duncan and owned two of his violins.
Andrew Young Smillie was the son, pupil, and assistant of Alexander Smillie . He was credited with over 200 violins, as well as some violas and cellos.

Arthur Edward Smith was a self-taught maker of significant achievement and influence, trained as an engineer and an amateur violinist. Inspired by Hill's Stradivari, his Life and Work motivated him to pursue violin making.
Bert Smith apprenticed as a motor engineer in Bowness. He began making violins as a self-taught amateur in 1938.
Dennis G. Smith was a pupil of William Luff.
Henry Smith was an important viol maker. Several extant examples, uncertainly attributed to him, were found in museums and elsewhere, but he was generally regarded as a leading figure.
John Smith made a Guarneri-derived model. The work was unrefined and featured oil varnish in various shades.

London maker who was employed by Peter Wamsley in the last years of his life and then took over the shop, the Harp and Hautboy in Piccadilly, having acquired the business from Wamsley’s widow in 1751. Smith also inherited Wamsley’s Royal Warrant and became violin maker to both George III and the Prince of York, remaining so until his death.

Thomas Smith initially established his work in Lakewood before moving to Indianapolis around 1950. He made approximately 73 violins, 12 violas, 3 cellos, and several viols, using Stradivari models and spirit varnish.

William Smith was a maker based in Stockport who produced undistinguished work in the London trade style. One of his instruments featured decorative purfling designs on a back made of bird's-eye maple and included a manuscript label.
Active second half of 19th century in Cambridge, MA.
William Smith established his workshop at Sycamore Street, Sheffield. One known cello is a Walmsley model featuring fine golden-brown soft textured varnish.
The relationship to Thomas Smith (I) and the surviving work remained unknown. Henley cited the label.
Early 20th-century maker based in Wetherby, Yorkshire. Label: 'William Edward Smith maker, Wetherby, 1906, no.20'
William J. Smith completed over 100 instruments.
Egidius Snoeck comes from a family of organ builders in Tournai. He is a pupil and successor of P.
Louis Socquet was born in Mirecourt. He established himself in Paris at the sign 'Au Genie de l'Harmonie', Place du Vieux Louvre.

Ettore Soffritti was born in Ferrara and is one of Italy's most revered makers of the early 20th century. On his earliest labels he indicated that he was a student of his father Luigi but given that the quality of Ettore's instruments is so much more advanced than that of his father it is widely accepted that he was influenced by other makers including Giuseppe Fiorini and Augusto Pollastri. ... />Soffritti's instruments show an extremely high standard of workmanship and precision, coupled with exquisite materials and refinement.

Luigi Soffritti was a carpenter and a self-taught violin maker known for his work featuring flat arches, planned materials, and broad edges, complemented by a good golden-brown varnish. He was the father of Ettore Soffritti, who succeeded and substantially excelled him in the craft.
Remo Solferino was a pupil of E. Soffritti.
Angelo Soliani, active from the 1770s, was one of the notable 18th-century makers from Modena. His craftsmanship demonstrated a direct knowledge of Cremonese instruments and was reminiscent of G.
Joseph Maurice Somny was the son and pupil of J. Somny-Ouchard.
D. Soriot made instruments in the Laberte Factory from 1890 to 1930 in Mirecourt.

Spirito Sorsana was probably a pupil of Giofreddo Cappa, as his workmanship and style were closely related. His violins featured low, flat arching across the breast, distinctive purfling with long splits in the white center, and soundholes that were set low and widely spaced, lightly fluted in the lower wings.
Berthold Specht was active from 1907. He established his workshop in Jena in 1921.
Worked for Metelka and Vitacaek in Prague. Established independently in Kiev, Ukraine from 1894, where he was briefly, assisted by his nephew J.
Son of Premysl Spidlen. Studied with his father and then in Mittenwald.

Otakar Frantisek Spidlen was the son, pupil, and successor of Frantisek Spidlen . He devoted most of his time to managing the shop and performing fine restoration and repair work as a very refined and successful maker.

Premysl Otakar Spidlen, son and pupil of Otakar Spidlen, was a highly influential maker of the 20th century, known for his immaculate workmanship and dedication to replicating the work of Stradivari and Guarneri. He was significantly influenced by contemporary theories of plate tuning and acoustical manipulation, contributing greatly through his own research and experience.
János Spiegel apprenticed to P. Pilát.
Jóssef Spitz studied under L. Rochert.
Eugen Sprenger was the youngest son and pupil of Anton Sprenger (II). He worked with his brother Adolf in Munich starting in 1900, after gaining experience in Switzerland and England.
Josef Ferdinand Sprenger was the son and pupil of Carl Boniface Sprenger. He also worked for Vauchel before assuming his role in his father's shop in 1875.
Jerome Bonaparte Squier was a farmer and shoemaker. He was an active violin maker in Lewiston and established his work in Boston in 1881.

Victor Carroll Squier was the adopted son and pupil of Jerome Squier. After learning the craft from his father, he worked for William Peters in Worcester, Massachusetts, as well as Treffle Gervais and George Drapier in Boston.
Ermanno Squilla worked in Rome for G. Corsini and S.
Daniel Achatius Stadlmann produced very fine and accurate work on the Stainer model. The arching had a medium height that was not unnecessarily exaggerated.

Johann Josef Stadlmann was the son, pupil, and successor of Daniel Stadlmann, appointed as a luthier to the Viennese court. His work, while similar to that of his father, was generally regarded as inferior.

Michael Ignaz Stadlmann was the son and pupil of Johann Joseph Stadlmann. He succeeded to the position of luthier for the Viennese court and served as a double bassist in the orchestra at the Royal Chapel from 1799 to 1813.
The personal model featured full arching and long corners that projected outward. It utilized good materials and had a golden-orange oil varnish.
John Stagg was employed by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons until 1983, when he left to pursue independent work.

The premier maker of the Austro-German school, Jacob Stainer may have been a student of Nicolò Amati in Cremona, though historical records are not complete enough to substantiate that assumption. In any case, his work, the earliest of which dates from the 1630s, bears strong similarities to Amati's.
Joseph Henry Stamps established a shop in Fort Worth around 1938. He created a Guarneri model and also produced copies of individual master instruments.
Carlton F. Stanley was a pupil and assistant of his uncle Freelan Oscar from 1924.
F. E.

Freelan Oscar Stanley developed photography processes for Kodak and built his own historically significant steam-powered car, the 'Stanley Steamer, ' in 1906. He was a highly proficient amateur violin maker with a distinctive personal model derived from Stradivari.
Robert A. Stanley was a prolific maker.
Albert Starck was the son, pupil, and successor of August Stark. He utilized a Stradivari model.
Anton Stauber was a pupil of G. Tiefenbrunner.
James Steele was a self-taught maker. He created the 1716 `Messie' Stradivari and personal models.
Giuseppe Stefanini studied under L. Vistoli and P.
Andreas Stelzer was cited by Vannes and Henley as a pupil of Hieronymus Amati (II). His work is currently unknown.

Alfred Stelzner studied mathematics and physics at Heidelberg University. He designed a radical new form of violin based on elliptical shapes, which were constructed for him by Wiedemann in Wiesbaden, along with his equally original cellone and violotta.
Herbert Stengel was a pupil of Petzold. He was employed by E.
Willibald Conrad Stenger was active in Topeka, Kansas, from 1903 and in Chicago, Illinois, from 1909 to 1942. His models were derived from Guarneri.
Charles Stéphan produced commercially made violins, violas, and cellos of good quality that featured deep red varnish.
Dale Walker Stevens was a self-taught violin and bow maker. He worked for the Salt Lake City Music Company, where he finished and varnished prefabricated instruments.
C. G.
E. Stieber was a pupil of M.
David Stirrat lived in Glasgow from 1794 to 1811. He established himself as a violin maker in Edinburgh starting in 1811.
Jacob Stöhr created fine work on the Stainer model. The arching was medium, and he used good materials.
Carlo Storioni was a fictitious name utilized in mass-produced factory instruments.

Though opinions of his work vary, Lorenzo Storioni merits high praise for helping to keep the great Cremonese tradition of violin making afloat in the late 18th century. An admiration for the work of Guarneri 'del Gesù' is evident, but in general Storioni followed 'del Gesù's spirit of individuality more than he copied his ... or any other maker's instruments.
Bernhard Stoss was the son of Joseph Anton Stoss. He moved to Vienna in 1806 and became a citizen in 1813.
Ignaz Stoss was the son and pupil of Eustachius Stoss. His work was rarely encountered, although small cellos were cited.
Martin (Johann Martin) Stoss. Stephan Stoss.

George Theodore Stott worked as an amateur for several years before establishing a professional business in Copperas Hill, Liverpool, around 1910. After this building was destroyed by bombing during the Second World War, he relocated to 69 Renshaw Street.
Harry L. Stoutenburgh was a self-taught violin maker who was active from 1935.

Antonio Stradivari is almost universally regarded as the greatest violin maker in history, and the value of his instruments reflects that exalted status. He was born in circa 1644, and his 71 year career is a vivid account of early mastery, fluid and ingenious innovation, and indelible craftsmanship and artistry.

Son of renowned Cremonese master Antonio Stradivari , Francesco Stradivari probably assisted his father in the family workshop starting in the 1690s alongside his brother Omobono , who specialized in the business side of the operation. He survived his father ... by only six years, during which time he was not particularly productive, but several printed labels from 1742 indicate that he did continue to make violins.

Omobono Stradivari was one of Cremonese titan Antonio Stradivari's sons from his first marriage. From around 1700 onward he seems to have made instruments from time to time, alongside his brother Francesco , but was largely involved in the business aspects ... of the enterprise rather than shop work and traveled frequently.
Josef Straub was the son of Simon Straub (III), as noted below. This work was typical of the family.
Jean Striebig was a Maître-Luthier based at 8, Rue Estivant, Mirecourt (Vosges). He produced quality work on both classical and personal models.
Casper Strnad was a pupil of T. Hulinzky.
Michael Strobl worked for J. J.
Established in 1921. Name usually inscribed in ink with date and city on both the top and back.
Johann Stübiger trained with his uncle Adam Stiibinger. He worked for Lutz starting in 1883 and for Zach from 1885, both in Vienna.
H. C.

Johann Christian Süss was one of the finest bow makers of the German school and was sometimes called the 'German Tourte. ' A pupil of Knopf in Dresden, he created models based on Tourte and Voirin. The sticks were stained dark red.
Masakichi Suzuki became interested in violins at the age of 14. His first instrument was based on Brescian lines and featured a slab cut front.
Born in Russia and studied as a violinist before moving to Israel in 1972. He studied violin making in Tel Aviv under Amnon Weinstein before enrolling in the violin making school in Cremona, Italy.
Seorim Swaine established himself as an amateur maker and repairer. He was a pupil of James McCauley and was active from 1910 in Rochester.
Verne Swan created a personal model featuring broad edges and pointed corners. It had a shaded orange-brown varnish and included a manuscript label.
Georg Szag was active in the USA starting in 1887 and produced good professional work based on a Stradivari-derived model.
Joseph Szendrey was a pupil of Toth in Budapest. He was a casualty of World War II.
Earnest (Ernö) Szép was a self-taught maker until he apprenticed with L. Tonté around 1936.

After his apprenticeship with Alajos Engleder and Samuel Nemessányi , Hungarian violin maker Béla Szepessy went to work for Thomas Zach in Vienna from 1874 to 1879. After a probable stint in Munich, he relocated to London, where he remained for 40 ... years.
Jacques Tainturie illustrated one bow by Millant/Raffin. No details were known about the maker.
Georg (Gyorgy) Tanczer was a pupil of Nemessanyi. He established himself in Budapest in 1882 and in Debreczen in 1899, and he was later employed by the Stowasser Company in Budapest.
The work exhibited a style reminiscent of Grancino, though it was of second rate quality. The outline was poorly governed, featuring small corners and soundholes that leaned outward.
Giovanni Georgio Taningard was a pupil and son-in-law of A. Platner.
Giuseppe Tarasconi was a double bass player and luthier. He studied under G.

Mirco Tarasconi was the son of Giuseppe Tarasconi and worked for Barnard in Liege, Laurent in Brussels, and Ornati in Milan. He was a student of Giuseppe Tarasconi, and his models were derived from Stradivari, featuring original designs.
Aubrey James Tarr exhibited a violin and viola at The Hague in 1949.
Thomas Tarr was a double bass player and a maker and dealer in musical instruments. His violins were of good quality, featuring full arched and well-articulated modeling.

William Tarr was a double bass player and maker, with his first instruments dated 1829. Over 200 basses were produced, later with the assistance of his sons Thomas, Joseph, and Shelley, which gained a very high reputation.
Bartolomeo Tassini created a Stainer model. The work was rather unrefined.
Pietro (Peter) Tatar was the son and pupil of Pietro Tatar. He established himself in New York from approximately 1960 to 1981.
Eduard Tauscher was a pupil of W. Meisel.
In 1956 David Taylor began his apprenticeship in the bow workshop of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons , where he became the firm's chief restorer of bows.
Malcolm Taylor joined the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in 1949 and returned in 1956 after leaving for military service.

Nathan Sleeper Taylor actively worked in Lewiston, ME from 1865 to 1918, completing approximately 140 instruments that showcased fine workmanship. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models, employing his own oil varnish and local materials.
Robert Taylor was the son of Stephen Oliver. He used a model derived from Stradivari and Guarneri.

Although David Tecchler was born in Lechbruck near Füssen in circa 1666 and arrived in Rome only in his late 20s, he is nevertheless considered the most prominent maker of the Roman school. Little is known about his working life outside of historical documents confirming his presence in Rome.

Giovanni Tedesco created a fine Stradivari model cello in the style of Gagliano, characterized by plain materials and a nicely concentric scroll with a thin chamfer. The varnish was a pale golden yellow-brown.
George Temple derived models from Stradivari and Guarneri. He also created large violas based on those of A.
Eugen Tenucci was of Tuscan origin. He studied under Ziist and served as the head of the violin department at Hug & Co. in Zurich starting in 1899.
Pietro Termanini is the brother of Giuseppe Termanini . He is known for similar work, which may be of slightly better quality overall.
Gerlando Terrana was a violinist who was active as a luthier in Milan from 1924. He completed about 100 violins, utilizing personal and Gagliano models.

Carlo Antonio Testore was the son, pupil, and successor of Carlo Giuseppe Testore , at the sign `dell'Aquila' in Contrada Larga. He was a more prolific maker than his father, though generally a less conscientious craftsman.
Carlo Giuseppe Testore was active in Milan from around 1683. He was a pupil of G.

Paolo Antonio Testore was the son, pupil, and successor of Carlo Giuseppe Testore . He was the least skilled maker in the family, often producing coarse instruments characterized by high arches and shortcuts, such as scratched and inked purfling, an unfluted pegbox, and poor-quality wood.
Moses A. Tewkesbury was a pupil of Abraham Prescott .

Gabriel Adolphe Thibout was the son and pupil of Jacques-Pierre Thibout . He created work similar to that of his father but developed his own model inspired by the Guarneri del Gesii 'Rode' violin from 1734.
Jacques-Pierre Thibout worked for Koliker in Paris starting in 1796. He established himself independently at 24 rue Montmartre in 1807.

Pierre-Louis Thibout worked at the same address in Grande Rue, probably as the brother of Antoine Thibout. His work was unrefined and based on an Amati-derived model, featuring a golden-yellow varnish.
The largest of the great Mirecourt instrument making firms. In 1857 Jérôme Thibouville joined forces with Charles Buthod and Charles-Claude Husson to form Husson-Buthod-Thibouville.
Produced work of the highest quality in the Viennese style. The high Stainer arch was somewhat reduced after 1775.
Probably Johann Georg Thir’s nephew. His sisters married Sebastian Dallinger and Franz Geissenhof and for a few years the three had their workshops in the same house.
Joseph Thiriot founded an important business that was later taken over by Laberte et Magni. His work was characteristic of Mirecourt, featuring a large flat model with yellow varnish.
Adolf Thomä was the son and pupil of Hermann Thoma. He worked at Hohendorf near Brambach from 1890 to 1938.
Alexander Thoma was active from 1887. He was the son and pupil of Jacob Thoma .
Arthur Adolf Thomä was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Adolf Thoma.
Jacob Thoma established himself as a clock maker in Vienna. He became a self-taught violin maker after his daughter's success as a violinist.
Mathias Thoma is a trade name for bows manufactured by Roderich Paesold of Bubenreuth, Bavaria, Germany.
Leading contemporary bow maker, based in Paris. Has trained many of the younger generation of makers and his bows are prized for their Sartory-like qualities.
Watkin Thomas established himself in Matthew Street, Swansea. He produced about 70 violins, several violas, and two cellos.
Thomassin studied under Clement. He established himself independently in Paris around 1825 and relocated to Le Mans around 1850.

Claude Auguste Thomassin, like his first cousin Louis, broke with the family's saddle-making tradition to become a bow maker. He apprenticed with Charles Nicolas Bazin , whose influence was paramount in Mirecourt during the late 19th century, and began working for the firm of Gand ... & Bernardel in Paris around 1885.

Born in 1893, Justin Thomassin was the son of Victor Thomassin and made bows in the English tradition working for George Withers and Dykes & Sons among others. His bows were often branded with the shop for whom they were made but occasionally bear the brand "T.
Louis Joseph Thomassin was a bow maker. He was a pupil of C.
Victor Thomassin, son of Nicolas Thomassin, was likely apprenticed to C. N.

By the time Charles and Samuel Thompson took charge of their family's workshop in 1763, it had become one of many London violin shops who sold instruments by various outside makers bearing the firm's labels. The business began with their father Peter around 1746 and by 1761 was known as Thompson & Sons.

Brother of Peter Thompson. Succeeded to the business of Daniel Wright: formerly the workshop of Barak Norman at the sign of the 'Bass Violin' (or the 'Bass Viol)' near the Chapter House on the north side of St.
Nathaniel Thorley was a musician and a part-time maker. His work represented typical provincial English craftsmanship of that period.

Thomas Thorley was the son and pupil of Nathaniel Thornley. Three instruments were cited in the Hill Archive: a cello featuring a flat arch, broad purfling with negligible edge flute, a large head, and 'tawny varnish, probably oil'; a small viola from 1879 with 'exceptionally long' soundholes and dark brown varnish; and a violin dated 1865.
Henry Thorowgood was an entrepreneurial dealer in trade work made by outworkers. The shop was established at the sign of the 'Violin & Guitar' in the North Piazza, Royal Exchange.
Charles Thouvenel was active in Luneville from 1788, but his work was branded from Paris. He produced good commercial work that featured red-brown varnish.

Nicolas Henry Thouvenel worked in the usual Mirecourt style on a large Stradivari model. He produced violins and cellos that featured a fine finish, often with a yellow to orange varnish and good one-piece backs.
John Thow created enlarged models of Stradivari and Guarneri. His work featured competent craftsmanship with pale golden-brown varnish.
Johann Georg Thumhardt was presumed to be the son of Johann Thumhardt . He was appointed as the luthier to the court of Amberg.
Johann Stephan Thumhardt was a pupil of Buchstetter. He established himself independently in Straubing from 1769.
Xavier Thumhardt was active in Ingolstadt from 1839. He moved to Munich in 1847.

Jakob Tiedemann was a self-taught maker known for Stradivari, Guarneri, and personal models. He created several copies of the Guarneri del Gesù 'Cannone' violin from 1743, which was owned by Paganini.

Joachim Tielke was the son of Gottfried Tielke and a celebrated and prolific maker of lutes, viols of all kinds, violins, and cellos. His workshop in Hamburg produced over 150 instruments, many of which are preserved in museums across Europe.
Hans Tietgen was self-taught, receiving some guidance from C. G.
C. W.
Thomas Tilley was a presumed pupil of Joseph Hill. He established himself in St.
Léon Tinceau established his work in the Rue du Bannier, Orleans. He produced fine work based on Cremonese models, featuring exceptional varnish that was sometimes shaded to imitate age.
Arrigo Tivoli-Fiorini was the nephew and pupil of G. Fiorini.

Richard Tobin was born in Ireland, where he is thought to have apprenticed with Thomas Perry and worked independently before moving to London with the encouragement of Vincenzo Panormo sometime before 1810. In the 1810s he seems to have worked ... for Henry Lockey Hill before setting up his own shop in 1823.
Ernst Edmond Todt was a pupil of E. L.
Heinrich Hermann Todt worked for C. Ficker in Budapest.
Domenico Tomassini was a musician and luthier. He created Cremonese and personal models, using golden-yellow or red varnish.
Giasone Tomasucci was a pupil of Leopardi in Spoleto. He established himself in Rome in 1926.
Carlo Annibale Tononi was the son and pupil of Giovanni Tononi . He worked in Bologna, largely in his father's style, until 1717 when he relocated to Venice.
This maker appears in reference books but there appears to be no evidence that he existed.
Giovanni Tononi, born in Bologna, was the son of Gaspare, a case-maker, and was active from 1688. He produced fine and distinguished instruments, though their quality varied.
Timothy Toomey apprenticed as a chemist. He became a professional violinist in 1891 and produced 12 violins and one viola in Bolsover, Derbyshire, up to 1897.

Angelo Toppani recorded as a 'violinaro' near Chiesa Novara in Rome from 1742 to 1748 and from 1760 'near the Palazzo del Drago'. His work was fair in the Tecchler style and featured pale golden-brown varnish.
Johannes (János) Tóth was a very influential Hungarian maker. He was the son of a cabinet maker and a pupil of J.

Theresia Toth studied the violin at Budapest Conservatoire and was the daughter and pupil of Janos Toth. She began making violins under her father's guidance in 1916 and received awards in Venice and Budapest.
Joseph Alexis Tournier was a Paris-based maker known for crafting violins, violas, cellos, and guitars. His work included a guitar located in the Musee de la Musique in Paris, branded as 'J.
Son and pupil of Nicolas Leonard Tourte, below. His contribution to the output of the Tourte workshop is not easily assessed.

Also known as Tourte 'le Jeune' to distinguish him from his older brother Nicolas Léonard , François Xavier Tourte is probably the most important figure in the history of modern bow making. His violin, viola, and cello bows are exquisite and command the highest prices for their extraordinary craftsmanship, elegance and appreciation ... by players.
Sons of Nicolas Pierre Tourte , the brothers Nicolas Léonard and François Xavier worked together and later separately in Paris.

While his younger brother François Xavier has historically been credited with the creation of the modern bow, research shows that Nicolas Léonard Tourte 'l'Ainé' began making bows before his brother, during a time of great innovation in French bow making. Desire for a wider tonal palette and accompanying modifications in ... playing technique in the mid-18th century led to sweeping new demands in bow construction.
Nicolas Pierre Tourte was initially employed as a carpenter until about 1742. His workshop produced both instruments and bows, and he became an important innovator in bow making.
Rodolfo Tramonti received medals in Bolzano, Rome, and Padua. Over 165 violins were completed using the Stradivari model with red oil varnish.
Raffaele Trapani's craftsmanship featured very finely finished details on a distinctive personal model with a large form. The full arch, of medium height, was inspired by Maggini.
Hermann Trapp studied under Pfretzschner. He worked at several shops before finally establishing himself in Wildstein in 1893.
Hans Trautner was a hairdresser and an amateur maker in Ansbach starting in 1898. He established himself professionally in Bandelstrasse in 1907.
Giuseppe Traverso trained as a carpenter. He produced 18 violins, three cellos, three guitars, and a mandolin.
Jean Trocard worked typically in Mirecourt, featuring pale yellow varnish and painted purfling. The back button was branded with a fleur-de-lys.
Carlo Troiani made musical instruments in Rome in the year 1885.
Silvio Tua studied under L. Bisiach in Milan from 1921.

The Tubbs bow making dynasty stretched over five generations, from the 1800s to the early 20th century, and was based in London. Thomas Tubbs, the first of the family whose bows can be identified, was active during the early 19th century and his son, William, continued the craft, probably receiving training from Edward Dodd as well as his father.

Alfred Tubbs was the son and pupil of James Tubbs and was active throughout his life in his father's shop, with most of his work bearing James' brand. He briefly ran the business between his father's retirement around 1888 and his own death in 1909, which led to James' return to work.
Charles Edward Tubbs is the son and pupil of William Tubbs . He works with his brother Edward in Castle Street starting in 1878, moving to 22 St Martin's Street around 1882.

Edward Tubbs, the third son and pupil of William Tubbs , began assisting his father William around 1850 and trained as a bow maker. He established his business at 38 Castle Street, London, in 1872, but emigrated to New York in 1878, where he was joined ... by his brother John in 1888.

James Tubbs was one of the finest and most prolific bow makers in the history of British violin making. His work rivals that of the finest French makers, and he is said to have made thousands of bows in his lifetime.

First in a line of important English bow makers that gradually succeeded the Dodd family, Thomas Tubbs probably trained with either James II or Edward Dodd I. He set up his own workshop around 1830, where he worked until 1863, supplying bows to various ... shops, including the Dodd's, John Betts , and Thomas Kennedy , which bear those makers' brands.

William Tubbs was the son and pupil of British bowmaker Thomas Tubbs and trained with James and Edward Dodd (II), becoming the successor to Edward Dodd III in 1851 after his death. He worked at several addresses in Lambeth and Soho, including Crescent Street Euston in 1841, 19 and later 41 Princes Street Soho in 1850, 45 Rupert Street in 1857, and finally at 1 Rupert Court from 1863.
Antonio Tunioli was a self-taught maker. He completed over 150 instruments, which included many ancient and plucked examples.
British bow maker. Started career as an engineering patternmaker (in wood) and taught woodwork, metalwork and engineering drawing.
William Turner worked in Aldgate, London, around 1650. Several fine viols existed in various collections.
Charles L. Tweedale was a parish priest who claimed to have received the recipe for Stradivari's varnish from the spirit world.
Herbert William Tyson worked on classical Italian models. He was quite prolific and used Millington's oil varnish, which had a golden-brown color.
Crescenzio Ugar was the son of Carlo Ugar from Urbino and was likely of German descent. He was first recorded in the Roman archives as a violin maker in SS.
Raised in Virginia, Christopher Ulbricht is the owner and luthier of Indianapolis Violins. He studied under Charles Woolf, Peter Prier, and Michael Vann.
Nicolò Ulcigrai was active in Trieste from 1912 and moved to Venice in 1931. He ceased work during the Second World War, resuming around 1945.
Georg Ullmann trained in Markneukirchen with E. R.

Antonio Ungarini created eccentric work characterized by a rather inelegant outline featuring short corners and a very full but low arching. The corners were short and broad, following the Brescian manner.
Raynaldo Ungarini was the son of Antonio Ungarini . He produced similar work, although the varnish was of lesser quality.

Very little is known about the life of Thomas Urquhart, though he was probably Scottish, and historical evidence suggests that he worked outside London and brought his instruments into the city for sale in various shops. Jacob Rayman is the only known maker in London who preceded him, as Urquhart's first verifiable instrument ... dates from the 1660s, and his work may even go as far back as the 1640s.
Eraldo Usuelli was a pupil of Paolo Erba . He established himself at Mariano Comense in 1913.
Utili was a guitarist who taught himself to make instruments. He initially specialised in guitars and mandolins before turning to violins.
Raffaele Vaccari was a pupil of C. G.
Geza Vadon studied under G. Varady at his Grosswardein Violin Making School in Budapest from 1923 to 1927.

Born in Asti in the mid 18th century, Giovanni Maria Valenzano was perhaps the most itinerant violin maker in Italian history. According to his labels, over the course of his career Valenzano worked in at least seven cities in three different countries, and he allegedly made keyboard instruments of all kinds in addition to guitars and violins.
Petrus Valenzano was presumed to be a brother of Giovanni Maria Valenzano, although no evidence is known. He mostly made plucked instruments, but a few violins are ascribed to him.
Frederic G. Vallance was a violinist on transatlantic liners before he established himself as a violin and bow maker in Detroit around 1913.
Violinist in the Concertgebouw Orchestra. Took up lutherie professionally from 1892.
Alphonse van Hoof trained in Markneukirchen. He worked in 'S Hertogenbosch, Holland in 1920, Borgerhout near Antwerp in 1923, and later in Tilburg and Antwerp.
Dr van Leeuwen was a doctor of medicine at The Hague. He was an experimental maker and received a silver medal for tone at Brussels in 1910.

Pier (Pietro) Lorenzo Vangelisti exhibited some influence from Stradivari in the scroll and soundholes, while belonging to the School of Gabrielli. The arching was of medium height, yet it appeared full and box-like with a deep and narrow edge flute.
Contemporary maker, who started career in 1975. Trained in New York with William Salchow.
Gyula Várady trained as an artist painter. He was introduced to violin making in the workshop of G.
Ferrucio Varagnolo was a talented maker from Padua who apprenticed with Leandro Bisiach in Milan. Unfortunately, he died early in his mid-30s after being committed to an asylum.
Nicholas Vasich studied under Bela Truppel in Novi Sad, Serbia before attending school in Markneukirchen, Germany. He later worked for Jan Dvorak and D.
Marcel Vatelot was born to parents from Mirecourt and was the great grandson of Blaise, a violin maker from Lorraine. He apprenticed with E.
Joseph Vautrin apprenticed as a violin maker to Chipot-Vuillaume in Mirecourt. He was appointed head of the workshop of Emile Germain in Paris.

Alfons Frantisek Vavra, son and pupil of Jana Baptisty Vávra, began working independently in Banská Bystrica, Slovakia, in 1920 under his father's name. He was joined by his brother Karel until 1925, after which he returned to Prague and opened a shop at 30 Jindrisska Street, where he remained for the rest of his life.
Son and pupil of Alfons Frantisek Vavra Sr. Together with his sister he worked for his father until his death.
Josef Vávra was the nephew of Jan Baptista Vávra and a pupil from 1927 to 1931.
Karel Vávra worked with his cousin Alfons Vávra in Banská Bystrica until 1931. He established himself independently in Korunni Street, Prague, in 1932.

Lorenzo Ventapane, nephew of Vincenzo Ventapane , was a significant maker in the Neapolitan tradition, presumed to be a pupil of Giovanni or Nicolo (II) Gagliano. He established his workshop in Calata Borgo di Loreto and later at 35 Strada Donnaregina, Naples, from 1800.
Pasquale Ventapane was possibly the son or brother of Lorenzo Ventapane . He claimed to have been a pupil of Antonio Gagliano according to his labels.
Vincenzo Ventapane was the presumed uncle of Lorenzo Ventapane . He was a pupil of Antonio and Raffaele Gagliano.
Verbeek was the stepson of Henryk Jacobs, from his first marriage. He worked in Jacobs' shop alongside Pieter Rombouts, another stepson from Jacobs' second marriage.
F. Verhasselt worked at the sign 'A Ste Cécile' from 1820 to 1850.
Andrea Verini was the son and pupil of Serafino Verini. He created Guarneri models that featured red varnish.

Carlo Vettori was born in 1940 in Firenzuola, a mountain town between Florence and Bologna. The son of master violin maker Dario I Vettori, he began his training at a young age in the family workshop and later refined his skills with master luthier Giuseppe Ornati.
Dario Vettori was a violinist and a member of the Benelli Quartet. He was a pupil of P.
The eldest son of Paolo Vettori and grandson of Dario Vettori.
François Vial was a pupil of T. Hüfner.

Giuseppe Viccari was an amateur maker of six violins based on the Stradivari model. He may also have been the same person as Rustom Vacaji, who was listed in Henley as a resident of Hampstead, London, in 1911; he was the inventor of a tubular 'tone improving' device.

J. E. Vickers was a violin and bow maker. He completed around 40 violins, 14 violas, six bows, and ten guitars, using Stradivari-derived violin models with oil varnish. Each instrument was signed and dated internally, with a label printed in blue.
Alfred Vidoudez was a pupil of G. Bazin in Mirecourt.
Pierre Vidoudez was the son and successor of Alfred Vidoudez . He graduated from business school in Geneva before attending the Mittenwald School of Violin Making.
Bernard Viedenhofer was a pupil of A. Hüber.
Holm Viertel was a pupil of R. Paulus.
Karl Georg Viertel was the son, pupil, and assistant of Holm Viertel . He established himself independently in Markneukirchen and qualified in the luthiers corporation in 1947.
Giuseppe Vignali was a cooper by profession and an amateur luthier of some distinction. He received honors at exhibitions in Rome, Milan, and Palermo.

Born in Mirecourt, bowmaker André Vigneron began to assist his father Joseph Arthur Vigneron at his shop in Paris at the end of the 19th century. His model at first hewed closely to that of his father, with somewhat thinner heads, and the two worked well together until the father's death in 1905.

Step-son of Mirecourt bow maker Claude Nicolas Husson , Joseph Arthur Vigneron apprenticed under his step-father until his death in 1872, when he began to work for Jean-Joseph Martin . In 1880 Vigneron left for Paris to work for the firm of Gand ... & Bernardel until 1888.
Luigi Villa was a self-taught violin maker. He was active from 1923 and later studied with Pallavicini in Boviso Masciago.

Italian violin maker who studied with Giorgio Scolari and Stefano Conia at the Cremona violin making school. Now works in Cremona and has received medals for his instruments from various competitions, including the Cremona Triennale of 1991.
Alexis Villaume worked for C. Aubert in Troyes until 1791, when he joined his colleague from the workshop, Charles Giron, to establish the company Villaume et Giron.
Gustave Villaume studied under Mougenot and Jacquet Gand in Mirecourt. He worked for Caressa and Français in Paris, as well as Jacquot in Nancy.
Vinaccia was a family of luthiers in Naples, Italy. They were chiefly known for making mandolins, but they also produced some very good violins in the Neapolitan style of the Gagliano family.

Antonio Vinaccia was the son, pupil, and assistant of Gennaro Vinaccia . He established himself independently in via Constantii from 1756, but evidence from known labels indicated that he returned to his father's workshop in rua Catalana around 1770.
Gaetano Vinaccia was the son of Antonio Vinaccia . He was active in the Rua Catalana, Naples, starting in 1779.

Gennaro Vinaccia was a senior member of this family of mandolin and violin makers. He established his business in Naples on rua Catalana at the sign 'Cremona. ' He was the father of Antonio, Giovanni, and Vincenzo.
Pasquale Vinaccia was regarded as the final of all mandolin makers. He was succeeded by his sons Gennaro (II) and Achille.
Vincenzo Vinaccia was the son, pupil, and assistant of Gennaro Vinaccia . He worked at the family shop located at rua Catalana, which later became known as 20 Calata de l'Ospedaletto.
Alfred Vincent was the first violin at His Majesty's Theatre and a violin maker. He was active in London starting in 1902 and was later assisted by his brother.

Giovanni Virzi, the son and pupil of Rosario Virzi, emigrated to the USA in 1912 and worked in New York with his brother Joseph Virzi from 1913. He established his own business in 1934, creating instruments based on Stradivari, Guarneri, and Amati models, characterized by his distinctive golden-orange oil varnish.
Younger brother of Giovanni Virzi. Emigrated to New York around 1913.
Louis Vissenaire established himself in Lyons around 1823. He was a prolific maker known for a large model featuring broad open soundholes and a bright red and red-brown varnish.

Louis Nicolas Vissenaire was the younger son of Louis Vissenaire (I) and served as a violinist in the orchestra at the Grand Theatre in Lyons. He succeeded to his father's business alongside his elder brother, Louis Vissenaire II , and his work was characterized by fine professional accuracy on a large model.
Luigi Vistoli established himself independently from 1910 after being a pupil of Nicola Utili. He spent several years in Venice and was a prolific maker.
Worked as a musician and made a large number of cello bows in the 1930s. Mostly self-taught.
Dominicus C. Vlummens was a pupil of his father, Isidor Vlummens, an amateur repairer.
Johann Georg Vogler was a violinist and cellist in the Würzburg court orchestra. He was the father of composer Abbé Georg Joseph Vogler.
Arnold Voigt was a violin and bow maker, a pupil of H. T.
The company was listed at 51 Monmouth Street, Cambridge Circus, and later in Sussex. It primarily focused on imported trade work.
Paul Voigt was the son of Paul Arno Voigt. He apprenticed in Markneukirchen and returned to his father's workshop in Manchester.
Simon Voigt was the presumed son of Johann Georg Voigt. He entered the corporation of luthiers in 1732.
Walter Voigt was the son of Johann Hermann Voigt. He worked for M.
Werner August Theodor Voigt studied under H. Reichel.
Wilhelm Johannes Voigt was the son of William Albin. He studied under R.

After his initial training and early work in Mirecourt, François Nicolas Voirin moved to Paris in 1855 to join his cousin Jean Baptiste Vuillaume . His work in the Vuillaume workshop gave him access to distinguished bow makers of the Peccatte school, ... but Voirin was inspired largely by the work of Pierre Simon and of Vuillaume himself.
Brother of Francois Nicolas Voirin. Bow maker.
Charles Pierre Voiry trained in Mirecourt. He established himself in Toulouse in 1925.

Perhaps the most gifted copyists in the history of violin making, the Voller brothers William, Charles, and Alfred are the objects of equal admiration for their tremendous talents and contempt for their nefarious business practices. They began in the London shop of George Hart and Son, where they were tasked with creating copies of important instruments that passed through the firm, which were always ... clearly labeled as such.

Stephan von Baehr began his instrument-making studies in 1988 in Markneukirchen, then went on to learn with Reinhard Bönsch in southern Germany. Further working experiences with Andreas Kägi and Christoph Götting inspired him and impacted his making.
Arthur Voss was a pupil of August Diehl . He worked in various locations across Germany before spending eight years with O.
Claude-François Vuillaume was the son of Charles-François Vuillaume (III) and the father of Jean-Baptiste. He was associated with coarse commercial work from the Mirecourt school.

In addition to being a renowned and prolific maker of fine violins, Jean Baptiste Vuillaume was a highly successful dealer, connoisseur and inventor. His workshop in Paris employed and trained some of the finest 19th-century violin and bow makers, including Maucotel , Silvestre , ...

Nicolas Vuillaume was the third son of Claude-François Vuillaume (IV). He worked for his brother Jean-Baptiste from 1832 to 1842, then returned to Mirecourt to produce student-grade instruments known as 'Stentor. ' These instruments were primarily made by A.

Nicolas François Vuillaume was the fourth son and pupil of Claude François Vuillaume (IV) and the younger brother of Jean-Baptiste Vuillaume . He worked in Paris alongside his brother Jean-Baptiste before establishing himself in Brussels around 1828, initially at 11 rue Mamix and later at 30 rue de l'Evêque.
Sebastien Vuillaume was the son and pupil of Claude-François Vuillaume (V). He worked for his uncle, Jean-Baptiste.
Anton Wachter was a prolific maker. His models were generally derived from Stradivari, although they exhibited strong Tyrolean influences.
Lorenz Wachter crafted viols, violins, and cellos in Germany. The work was neatly done and featured an opaque yellow-brown varnish, following a Tyrolean model.
Benedikt Wagner worked in Dürrwangen around 1750, according to some sources. His work on the Stainer model was very precise and commendable.
Joseph Wagner was appointed luthier to the court in 1774. His work from Tyrol featured a medium high arch and neat, precise craftsmanship.
Sebastian Wagner was the brother of Joseph Wagner . His fine work featured a slightly elongated Stainer model, characterized by rather flat curves into the corners from the upper and lower bouts.
Xavier Wagner was the son, pupil, and successor of Benedict Wagner. He created a flat-arched model using plain materials and a thick red-brown varnish.

Eugen Wahl was a wood sculptor and violinist who spent some time studying in Switzerland. He became an amateur violin maker in 1908 and established himself professionally in Kreuzstrasse, Karlsruhe, from 1909.
Born in Illinois. Studied with Carl Becker.
William Walker was a hairdresser and self-taught violin maker. He produced over 200 instruments based on Stradivari and Guarneri models by reading Heron-Allen Violin Making.
William Walton was a retired stationmaster and a self-taught violin and bow maker who began in 1887. He established himself professionally in 1922.

The tradition of great English cellos starts with Peter Wamsley, who worked in London in the early 1700s. His workshop had various names over the course of his career, all of them with the word "harp," which was typical of the period.
Michael Wanner produced fine work that was superior to much of what came from the town during this period.
George Ward produced fine work in the style of Perry, with whom he may have been associated. Perry held the Angelsey Street address listed on Ward's label from 1771.
Albert E. Warrick, a pupil and assistant of G.
Joseph Wassermann produced good and prolific work on classical models, especially Amati. Varnish featured shades of orange-brown and yellow-brown.
Frank Watson created over 200 instruments that featured orange shades of varnish.
The last pupil of William Retford , William Watson worked for the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons from 1945 to 1962.
Robert J. Webb worked in London.
Orrin Weeman worked for A. W.
Roelof Weertman trained as a naval architect. He became an amateur maker around 1915, receiving guidance from Hanken in Rotterdam.
Richard Weichold was a violin and bow maker. He was the son of August Weichold and a pupil of Pfretzschner in Markneukirchen.
Richard Weidemann worked for Sutterlin and later established himself independently. He created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri.
Ewald Weidhaas was a bow maker. He produced good gold-mounted dark brown octagonal sticks.
Paul Weidhaas worked for Liebig in Wroclaw and Winterling in Hamburg. He established himself in Markneukirchen with S.
Johann Blasius Weigert made violins, viols, and plucked instruments. His violins were without distinction and featured a dull brown varnish.
Eugenio Weiss was a professor of the violin at Trieste from 1880. He was a self-taught maker who created personal models and used shaded dark varnish.
All types of plucked and bowed instruments were known in various civic collections. Violins held little interest due to their meager varnish.
Johannes (Hans) Michael Weisshaar studied at the Mittenwald School. He subsequently pursued acoustics at Basle University.

Frederick Weller was a coffin maker and a self-taught luthier. He completed approximately 200 instruments by 1952, utilizing models derived from Stradivari, Guarneri, and Rocca, and applied his own varnish in a wide range of shades.
Frederick Wilhelm Weller was the son and pupil of Carl Wilhelm Weller. He established himself independently in 1881 and later relocated to Leipzig, where he worked on the Arndstrasse.
Gregor Ferdinand Wenger was active in Augsburg from 1701, when he married the widow of J. Fichtl.

Francis Werentle was presumed to be a member of the Werendle/Wörnle family of Mittenwald, representing an important link between the two similar schools of work in London and Germany. His work featured a fine Stainer model, allied in style to Thomas Smith or John Johnson.
Henry Werro was the eldest son of Jean Werro . He studied at Markneukirchen under August Wunderlich from 1910 to 1914.
Jean Werro was a pupil of Leon Fischesser in Geneva. He established himself in Islington, north London, in 1890.
Walter William Whitbread was an amateur violinist. He created personal models as well as those derived from Stradivari.
Asa Warren White was the son and pupil of John White . He worked for Henry Prentiss and formed a partnership with his brother Ira in Boston from 1849 to 1863.
Ira Johnson White was the son of John White and was active from 1825. He moved to Boston in 1829.
John White was the earliest violin maker established in Massachusetts in 1802. He moved to Barre in 1806 and made approximately a dozen violins.
Nehemiah White created American 'church basses' and double basses. He utilized local materials and incorporated inked purfling.
Edwin Whitmarsh was the son and assistant of Emanuel Whitmarsh . His work was generally considered inferior, but it was rarely found with his own label.

Emanuel Whitmarsh was a stonemason and self-taught violin maker who worked in Berwick Street, London from 1856 to 1885, where he repaired instruments for various dealers and produced a significant number of violins of good trade quality, utilizing various models and both oil and spirit varnish. In 1885, he moved to Kentish Town Road, followed by a relocation to Hackney in 1887, and finally established ... himself in Dalston in 1908, assisted by his son Edwin.

Maurice Whittaker was a musical instrument dealer. Instruments associated with his name are rare and typically exhibit the anonymous appearance characteristic of London trade work from that period, suggesting he may have also been a maker of original work.
Milton O. Wickes worked with Andrew Hyde in Northampton until 1904.
Anton Widhalm was the third son and pupil of Leopold Widhalm. He worked with G.

Gallus Ignatius Widhalm was the second son, pupil, and assistant of Leopold Widhalm. Most experts generally praised him, but no instruments with his personal label are known, as he worked for either his father or elder brother, both of whom used the Leopold Widhalm label.
Leopold Widhalm was generally recognized as the best German maker following Stainer. His model was very distinctive and graceful, featuring a medium high arch and deeply fluted edges.

Martin Leopold (Leopold II) Widhalm was the son, pupil, and successor of Leopold Widhalm and continued with his father's label. His work was generally less refined than that of his father, featuring higher arching and slightly uneven purfling.
Ernest E. Wild was a violinist, violin maker, and repairer who was active in Billings before relocating to San Francisco around 1956.
Franz Anton Wild created unattractive Stainer copies. They were well made but featured a high arch, deeply worked edge flute, and thin dark opaque varnish.
Albin Wilfer descended from a line of amateur luthiers. He studied under E.
William Wilkanowski was a Polish maker active from 1895 who emigrated to the USA in 1920. He worked for Oliver Ditson in Boston and later for Gretsch in New York starting in 1938.
Violinist-turned-maker, who carried out restorations for and sold instruments through J.&A. Beare Ltd in London for four decades.

Samuel Blakely Wilkinson worked at 'Ye Olde Fiddle Shoppe. ' He had inconsistent work on models derived from Stradivari. The materials were generally good, but the varnish varied in both colour and success.
William Wilkinson was the son-in-law, apprentice, and successor to Thomas Perry. He entered into partnership with Perry in 1790 and took over the business following Perry's death in 1818.
Hendrick Willems was presumed to be the son of Jooris Willems. Known or recorded are three large violas, a cello, a pochette, and a bass viol.

Johann Michael Willer granted citizenship in Prague in 1780 and became head of the luthiers guild in 1825. He successfully evolved from the Stainer-Tyrolean model of his early work to an elegant Amatise Stradivari model, while still retaining a slightly exaggerated edge flute to the arch.
Arthur Williams was a piano-tuner and repairer. He was taught violin making by Frank Howard.
R. J.
Josiah John Willmore studied under Frank Howard. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Derek Wilson was trained as a bow maker and repairer in the bow department of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in the latter part of the 20th century.

One of the last of the Hill bow makers, Garner Wilson joined the W.E. Hill & Sons firm aged 16, receiving his training from William Watson and Arthur Bultitude , with frequent ... advice from William Retford .
James John Thompson Wilson was a bow maker and violin repairer. He was a pupil of James Tubbs and worked for Charles Boulangier.
James L. Wilson studied under J.
The model was a flat-arched Stradivari. It featured decent work with yellow-orange varnish and was in the style of Hardie, possibly indicating a pupil.
Otto Windisch apprenticed to Himmer. He worked for Glaesel and Herwig.
Franz Winkler was a bow maker employed by Julius Guetter from around 1900 to 1910. His personal model was known as the 'Winkler', along with Tourte and Voirin models.
Georg Winterling worked in Frankfurt, Dresden, and Vienna as a pupil of B. Klier.
Established in Vine Court, Bishopsgate from 1650, moving nearby to Half Moon Alley 1656. Returned to Vine Court 1661, working closely with G.
Edward Withers was a cellist and the son of a confectioner. He purchased the shop of R. & W.
Edward Newman (Edward II) Withers. Son and pupil of Edward (I), active from 1856.
George Thomas Henry Withers was the younger son of Edward Withers (I). In 1878, he separated from his brother and opened his own shop in St Martin's Lane, moving to 22 Leicester Square in 1891.
Joseph Withers was active from 1856 and worked for J. Brown in Spitalfields starting in 1869, later establishing himself independently in Caledonian Road, north London, in 1879.
Anton Wittmann studied under E. Heidegger in Linz starting in 1892 and later with Stiibinger in Vienna from 1897.
Sebastian Wojtyszyn was the son and pupil of Gregory Wojtyszyn. He established himself in Philadelphia in 1971 and in Denver, Colorado, in 1972.
Leopold & Ferdinand Wolff established a factory in 1864. Many instruments were exported to England and labeled as if they were made there.
Daniel L. Wood recorded over loo instruments.
C. F.
James Worden was a musician, cabinet maker, organ builder, and violin maker. He completed about 50 instruments, which featured models derived from Stradivari and Guarneri.
Johann Paul Wörle worked in Nüremberg, Nagysombat, and Trnava before settling in Bratislava. He became a citizen in 1811.
Christoph Wörnle traded work for wholesalers. Some instruments were signed internally: `Christoph Wornle in / Mittenwald ao 1821'.
Matthias Wörnle was the son and pupil of Christoph Wornle. He engaged in trade work for wholesalers.
Anthony T. Wrona suffered a spinal injury during the Second World War.
Ignaz Wrozina was born and trained in Budapest. From 1885, he worked as a piano tuner for Methven and Simpson in Edinburgh.
Emile Wuichet created amateur work. The piece featured classical Italian models and was finished with orange-red varnish.
George Wulme-Hudson trained as a jeweller and violinist. He was a self-taught maker with some guidance from Thomas Jacques Holder and was active from 1897.
August Wunderlich worked for Raautmann in Magdeburg after being a pupil of L. Glaesel.
Friedrich Wilhelm Wunderlich was a bow maker and a pupil of Nurnberger. He established himself in Markneukirchen and moved to Leipzig in 1898.
Gustav Wunderlich was a pupil of Karl Kohler in Schönbach. He was a violin and bow maker who worked in Berlin and established himself in Leipzig from 1897.
Otto Felix Wunderlich was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of Moennig and worked in Cologne, Amsterdam, and Karlsruhe.
Anton Wurmer created a Klotz school with a very high arch in Garmisch in the year 17.
Sydney Braithwaite Yeoman was a bow maker for W. E.
The work is fairly coarse and is based on a Stradivari-derived model, finished with yellow-red spirit varnish. It is branded: 'Young ABDN'.
Marshall Youngman was a self-taught amateur maker from 1880. He established himself professionally in Halifax around 1890.
Carl Zach was the son and successor of Thomas Zach . In 1897, he founded the Carl Zach Company, which had offices in Budapest, Mannheim, and Darmstadt.
Trained with J. B.
It was not known by whom he was taught. Henley described violins and violas featuring high arches, some of which had carved children's heads and golden yellow varnish.
Giuseppe Zamberti established himself independently in via Piccoli, Vicenza, after 1918. He was a pupil of Sgarabotto.
Francesco Zanardi was a pupil of I. Sderci in Florence, Italy.
Aldo Zani was self-taught and used personal and classical models. He applied a clear yellow varnish, which sometimes appeared as a darker golden-brown.
Ferruccio Zanier was a dentist and part-time luthier. He was active in Genoa and Trieste, creating classical models as well as some experimental types featuring flat unarched plates.
Active 1980s
Giacomo Zanoli was the son of Giovanni Battista Zanoli . Evidence indicated his presence in Venice in 1742, and one cello contained an internal inscription reading 'Padua 1737'.
Giovanni Battista Zanoli rarely produced work that was slightly Germanic in style and not of the same quality as that of his son, Giacomo. He was from Verona and was active in the 17th century.

Antonio Zanotti was a notable 18th-century maker based in Mantua, contemporary with Camilli. He may have received instruction from Hieronymus (II) Amati or Pietro Guarneri, who died in Mantua in 1720.

Alessandro Zanti was possibly a descendant of Antonio Zanotti . Born in Reggio and later moving to Mantua with his family, he was a professional and well-known violinist who made instruments as an amateur, of which very few survive.
Francesco Zapelli studied under Batelli in Bologna and Muschietti in Udine. He worked for F.
Fratelli Zari created a Stradivari model featuring golden-yellow varnish, as described by Henley. It is currently unknown and unverified.
Antonio Zatta was known as Tonino. He studied under O.
William H. Zeswitz was a pupil and assistant of his brother-in-law, William Moennig, for 15 years.
Karl Otto Zimmer was the brother and pupil of Karl Zimmer. From 1885, he worked for Jüling and Weichold in Dresden.
Julius Heinrich Zimmermann established a music publishing company in St. Petersburg in 1876, eventually opening branches in Moscow in 1882, Leipzig in 1886, and Riga in 1905.
László Zsiga was the son of violin dealer and repairer Lajos Zsiga. He studied with D.
Annibale Zuffa was an amateur maker from c. 1980. In 1991, he was associated with Zuffa / 48027 Sloarolo (Ra).
J. Emil Zust was a pupil of W.

Anton Zwerger was active in Salzburg from around 1800 and illustrated a fine piccolo violin dated 1810 in Passau, Germany. His work was typical of the Mittenwald style, following the Klotz model, characterized by a medium high arch and thin red-brown varnish.

Samuel Zygmuntowicz, born 1956 in Philadelphia, was already a prize-winning sculptor before beginning his instrument-making studies at age 13. He studied violin making and restoration with Carl Becker and Rene Morel, and at the Violin Making School of America in Salt Lake City.
